|| before 8 September 1992 – 17 June 1993 |- | ''Minister of State for Government Affairs'' || [[Abdul Hai Elahi]]alternative name(s): ''Abdul Hai Alahi''. || 28 June 1992 – 17 June 1993 |} ===First Hekmatyar cabinet and power struggles (1993–1996)=== On 17 June 1993, Prime Minister [[Gulbuddin Hekmatyar]] and his cabinet were sworn in by President [[Burhanuddin Rabbani]].[https://www.nytimes.com/1993/06/18/world/afghan-cabinet-is-sworn-in-secretly-in-isolated-village.html ''Afghan Cabinet Is Sworn In Secretly in Isolated Village''], [[The New York Times]], 18 June 1993. On 8 September, Hekmatyar called on Rabbani and the cabinet to resign to allow for the establishment of a "neutral interim government" to be chosen in a free general election, but was denied."Chronology 16 July 1993 – 15 October 1993." Middle East Journal, vol. 48, no. 1, 1994, pp. 113–39. JSTOR, http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328665. Accessed 5 Feb. 2025. On 11 December, Rabbani allegedly approved the resignation of Hekmatyar and the transfer of power to Communications Minister [[Mohammad Amin Waqad]], however, the [[Hezb-e Islami Gulbuddin|Hezb-e Islami]] denied that he had stepped down as Prime Minister."Chronology 16 October 1993 – 15 January 1994." Middle East Journal, vol. 48, no. 2, 1994, pp. 327–55. JSTOR, http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328694. Accessed 5 Feb. 2025. On 19 December, [[Radio Afghanistan]] announced that [[Qutbuddin Hilal]] had assumed the duties of Prime Minister, which again was denied by Hekmatyar. Since 1 January 1994 when Rabbani's and Hekmatyar's forces openly went to war against each other,[https://www.nytimes.com/1994/06/26/world/afghan-forces-battle-guerrillas-in-capital.html ''Afghan Forces Battle Guerillas in Capital''], [[The New York Times]], 26 June 1994. the real power that the cabinet had at that point was heavily put under question because the ministers loyal to each side where only commanding to the President or Prime Minister respectively.Khalilzad, Zalmay. "Afghanistan in 1994: Civil War and Disintegration." Asian Survey, vol. 35, no. 2, 1995, pp. 147–52. JSTOR, https://doi.org/10.2307/2645023. Accessed 5 Feb. 2025. Rabbani refused to step down at the end of his term on 28 June 1994 and then again half a year later on 28 December 1994, and on 1 January 1995, [[United Nations]] peace envoy [[Mahmoud Mestiri]] returned to Kabul.{{cite report |ref={{sfnRef|Chronology of Events|1997}} |title=Afghanistan: Chronology of Events January 1995 – February 1997 |date=February 1997 |publisher=Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada |url=https://www.justice.gov/sites/default/files/eoir/legacy/2014/01/16/Af_chronology_1995-.pdf |access-date=19 August 2016 |archive-date=12 October 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171012061437/https://www.justice.gov/sites/default/files/eoir/legacy/2014/01/16/Af_chronology_1995-.pdf |url-status=live}} On 10 January 1995, Rabbani offered to step down and turn over power to a 23-member UN interim administration if Hekmatyar agreed to withdraw. On 12 February, the many parties agreed to a multi-party council which would take over on 20 February.[https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1995/02/12/afghan-factions-agree-to-multi-party-council/ef5643d4-eb38-4644-a2d2-ca114328ab8c/ ''AFGHAN FACTIONS AGREE TO MULTI-PARTY COUNCIL''], [[The Washington Post]], 12 February 1995. On that date though, the scheduled transfer of power was disrupted by demands from Rabbani for assurances that the new government includes the newly emerging [[Taliban]]."Chronology 16 January 1995 – 15 March 1995." Middle East Journal, vol. 49, no. 3, 1995, pp. 485–507. JSTOR, http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328836. Accessed 5 Feb. 2025. Hence, Rabbani further delayed his resignation stating he would not resign before 21 March. On 18 March however, Rabbani announces that hewould not step down on the UN-appointed date because the "mechanism for the transfer of power had not been established",[https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1995/03/20/afghan-militia-taliban-after-raising-hopes-is-driven-off/6c09b490-0925-4ec1-8651-3b6ee0fafd75/ ''AFGHAN MILITIA TALIBAN, AFTER RAISING HOPES, IS DRIVEN OFF''], [[The Washington Post]], 19 March 1995. and that the transfer of power to an interim government was delayed for another 15 days, which also did not happen. At that point, Hekmatyar already had to abandon his headquarters in [[Chahar Asyab]] due to the overwhelming force of the Taliban, further diminishing or ''de facto''"Chronology 16 July 1995 – 15 October 1995." Middle East Journal, vol. 50, no. 1, 1996, pp. 85–112. JSTOR, http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328898. Accessed 5 Feb. 2025. (but until November 1995 not ''de jure''){{cite book | url=https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=osu.32435083448365&seq=13 | title=Chiefs of State and Cabinet members of foreign governments / National Foreign Assessment Center 1995Sep-Dec 1995| date=2003}} ending his power as Prime Minister.{{cite book|last=Maley|first= William |title=The Afghanistan Wars|url=https://archive.org/details/afghanistanwars00male|url-access=limited|year=2002|publisher=[[Palgrave Macmillan]]|isbn=978-0-333-80291-5|page=[https://archive.org/details/afghanistanwars00male/page/n186 176]}} On 14 November 1995, Mistiri reported that Rabbani had agreed to step down and to transfer power to a 25-member transitional council, but the Taliban rejected that proposal."Chronology 16 October 1995 – 15 January 1996." Middle East Journal, vol. 50, no. 2, 1996, pp. 243–65. JSTOR, http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328928. Accessed 6 Feb. 2025. On 20 May 1996, President [[Burhanuddin Rabbani]] met with ex-Prime Minister [[Gulbuddin Hekmatyar]] to discuss an alliance between Hekmatyar's [[Hezb-e Islami Gulbuddin|Hezb-e Islami]] and the Rabbani's government, and on 24 May they agreed to end hostilities between their two groups to move toward an elected government."Chronology 16 April 1996 – 15 July 1996." Middle East Journal, vol. 50, no. 4, 1996, pp. 573–93. JSTOR, http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328991. Accessed 6 Feb. 2025. On 26 June 1996, Hekmatyar was sworn in as Prime Minister for a second time, keeping the ministers of the old government as acting ministers until a new cabinet was elected.[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/06/27/world/world-news-briefs-afghans-get-new-leader-during-rebel-assault.html ''World News Briefs;Afghans Get New Leader During Rebel Assault''], [[The New York Times]], 27 June 1996. {| class="wikitable sortable" |+ Hekmatyar Cabinet*[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=uc1.c049366156&seq=11 ''Chiefs of State and Cabinet members of foreign governments / National Foreign Assessment Center July-Sept 1993'']

'''Name''' !! '''Year(s)''' !! colspan="2" | '''Affiliation''' !! '''Origin''' |- ! colspan="6" | '''Role''' |- | [[President of Afghanistan|President]] || [[Burhanuddin Rabbani]] || 17 June 1993 – 3 July 1996Although officially only the [[head of state]], Rabbani shared power in forming the cabinet with Prime Minister Hekmatyar, effectively also assuming the role of [[head of government]], especially after Hekmatyar's first tenure ''de facto'' ended. |- | rowspan="5" | [[Prime Minister of Afghanistan|Prime Minister]] || [[Gulbuddin Hekmatyar]]alternative name(s): ''(Engineer) Gulbuddin Hikmatyar''. || 17 June 1993 – 28 June 1994In December 1993, Hekmatyar's power as Prime Minister slowly started to deteriorate, when media outlets controlled by President [[Burhanuddin Rabbani]] spread rumors that Hekmatyar, who never set foot into the [[Kabul|city of Kabul]] during his entire tenure, has resigned. On 1 January 1994, Hekmatyar's [[Hezbi Islami]] switched alliances, joining [[Junbish-i-Milli]] leader [[Abdul Rashid Dostum]] and others to form the ''Shura-i Hamahangi''. This alliance controlled small, but significant parts of Kabul ([http://www.jstor.org/stable/4328694]), and tried to besiege the rest of the [[Jamiat-e Islami]] controlled parts. However, on 26 June 1994, Rabbani and [[Ahmad Shah Massoud|Massoud]] forces were able to push the alliance out of the city ([https://www.nytimes.com/1994/06/26/world/afghan-forces-battle-guerrillas-in-capital.html], [https://www.nytimes.com/1994/06/27/world/afghan-army-ousts-foes-from-capital.html]), gaining more and more influence while diminishing Hekmatyar's role. At least after 14 February 1995, when Hekmatyar was forced to flee from his headquarter in Chahar Asyab, he ''de facto'' lost control of the cabinet, definitively ending his first tenure as Prime Minister.
All in all, it was more of a gradual decline of Hekmatyar's power rather than an aprupt or clear-cut end. Therefore, there are differing views on when exactly one can consider his term to have effectively ended, however, most sources point to 28 June 1994 (right after the ''Shura-i Hamahangi'' were forced out of Kabul), when Rabbani's first term as President would regularly have ended ([https://www.rulers.org/rula1.html]). It is important to consider that Hekmatyar's tenure ''de jure'' did not end until November 1995 when he officially was remoed from the cabinet. |- | ''disputed'' (de facto)|| 28 June 1994 – before 14 February 1995While, if any exact date is mentioned at all ([https://www.jstor.org/stable/resrep05380.13?seq=2]), most sources say Prime Minister [[Gulbuddin Hekmatyar]]'s tenure ended on 28 June 1994, it is not clear who really controlled the Prime Ministry afterwards. Up until 14 February 1995, it could be argued that Hekmatyar still hat some sort of influence on the cabinet (before being forced to flee out of the [[Kabul Province]]). Many sources, however, point out that President [[Burhanuddin Rabbani|Rabbani]] and [[Ahmad Shah Massoud|Massoud]]'s troops forced Hekmatyar out of the city, Deputy Prime Minister [[Arsala Rahmani]] replaced him as acting Prime Minister, either right away ([https://www.jstor.org/stable/resrep05380.13?seq=2]) or in November 1994 ([https://www.rulers.org/rula1.html]). |- | [[Arsala Rahmani]] (caretaker; de facto)|| before 14 February – before 16 November 1995It is not clear when exactly [[Arsala Rahmani]] was replaced by [[Ahmad Shah Ahmadzai]] as ''de facto'' acting Prime Minister. Some sources say Ahmadzai took office in February 1995, right after [[Gulbuddin Hekmatyar]]'s last stronghold fell to the [[Taliban]], while others say Rahmani stood in office until 16 November 1995 before Ahmadzai replaced him ([https://www.rulers.org/rula1.html]). |- | [[Ahmad Shah Ahmadzai]] (acting; de facto)|| before 16 November 1995 – 26 June 1996 |- | Gulbuddin Hekmatyar || 26 June – 3 July 1996 |- | First Deputy Prime Minister || [[Qutbuddin Hilal]]alternative name(s): ''(Engineer) Qudbuddin Helal, Gulbuddin Helal''. || before 10 September 1993 – before 26 June 1996 |- | Deputy Prime Minister || Arsala Rahmani || before 10 September 1993 – before 26 June 1996 |- ! colspan="6" | '''Key ministries''' |- | rowspan="3"| [[Afghan Foreign Minister|Foreign Affairs]] || [[Hedayat Amin Arsala]]alternative name(s): ''Hidayat Amin Arsala''. || 17 June 1993 – after 1 November 1995 |- | [[Najibullah Lafraie]]alternative name(s): ''Najibollah Lefrahi''. (caretaker) || before 2 January – before 26 June 1996 |- | ''vacant'' || before 26 June – 3 July 1996 |- | [[Ministry of Defense (Afghanistan)|Defense]] || [[Ahmad Shah Massoud]] (de facto)Rabbani was named chairman of the ''Defense Commission'' for two months until a permanent Defense Minister was named. This status quo was officially continued after those two months (see: [https://www.refworld.org/reference/annualreport/hrw/1994/en/90848 ''Human Rights Watch World Report 1994 - Afghanistan''], [[United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees]], 1 January 1994). But during this entire time, [[Ahmad Shah Massoud]] ''de facto'' still was Defense Minister ([https://www.refworld.org/reference/annualreport/usdos/1996/en/25661 ''U.S. Department of State Country Report on Human Rights Practices 1995 - Afghanistan''], 30 January 1996) acting under the name of "military chief of Afghanistan" ([https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/national/1996/04/09/us-senator-in-afghanistan/d49a6c8c-4243-43df-a481-4781815b4e4f/ ''U.S. SENATOR IN AFGHANISTAN''], [[The Washington Post]], 8 April 1996), even though Deputy Defense Minister [[Yunus Qanuni]] was sometimes mentioned as acting Defense Minister since the turn of 1995/96. || 17 June 1993 – 3 July 1996 |- | rowspan="2"| [[Afghan Interior Minister|Interior]] || ''vacant''Hekmatyar was named chairman of the ''Interior Commission'' for two months until a permanent Interior Minister was named. This status quo was officially continued after those two months (see: [https://www.refworld.org/reference/annualreport/hrw/1994/en/90848 ''Human Rights Watch World Report 1994 - Afghanistan''], [[United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees]], 1 January 1994) until Hekmatyar was officially removed from the cabinet before 1 November 1995. || 17 June 1993 – 26 June 1996 |- | [[Munshi Abdul Majid]]alternative name(s): ''Munshi Majid''. ''Minister of State for State Affairs''also called the ''Minister Counselor for Cultural and Social Affairs''.|| [[Abdul Hai Elahi]] || before 5 January 1994 – 3 July 1996 |- | ''[[Ministry of Finance (Afghanistan)|Minister of State for Financial Affairs]]''also called the ''Minister Counselor for Economic Affairs''.|| [[Hamidullah Tarzi]] || before 10 September 1993 – 3 July 1996 |- | ''[[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Afghanistan)|Minister of State for Foreign Affairs]]''|| Najibullah Lafraie || before 10 September 1993 – 3 July 1996 |} === Second Hekmatyar cabinet (1996)=== On 26 September 1996, it was reported that the [[Taliban]] have captured [[Kabul]], although this was denied by Deputy Foreign Minister [[Abdul Rahim Ghafoorzai]].[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/09/26/world/kabul-falls-to-islamic-militia-afghans-accuse-pakistan.html ''Kabul Falls to Islamic Militia; Afghans Accuse Pakistan''], [[The New York Times]], 26 September 1996.[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/09/27/world/afghan-fundamentalists-sweep-into-kabul.html ''Afghan Fundamentalists Sweep Into Kabul''], [[The New York Times]], 27 September 1996. The next day, the government fled north of Kabul to [[Charikar]] and [[Jabal Saraj]] at the gateway to the [[Panjshir Valley]], considering the inevitable conquer of the city by the guerilla forces. The Rabbani government tried to reconquer the capital for a year, but where weakened and lost even more land,[https://www.nytimes.com/1997/05/25/world/afghan-north-last-holdout-falls-to-army-of-taliban.html ''Afghan North, Last Holdout, Falls to Army Of Taliban''], [[The New York Times]], 25 May 1997. so that on 21 August 1997, when Hekmatyar already fled to Iran and his successor Ghafoorzai died, the cabinet, which already ''de facto'' lost control over the country, was dissolved.[https://www.afghan-bios.info/index.php?option=com_afghanbios&id=668&task=view&total=79&start=28&Itemid=2 Afghan Biographies: Hekmatyar, Gulbuddin] {| class="wikitable sortable" |+ Hekmatyar Cabinet*[https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=uc1.c058577168&seq=3 ''Chiefs of State and Cabinet members of foreign governments / National Foreign Assessment Center July 1996'']

Disabled|Minister Counselor for Cultural
and Social Affairs]]''|| [[Abdul Hai Elahi]] || 3 July – 27 September 1996 |- | ''[[Ministry of Finance (Afghanistan)|Minister Counselor for Economic Affairs]]''|| [[Hamidullah Tarzi]] || 3 July – 27 September 1996 |- | ''[[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Afghanistan)|Minister of State for Foreign Affairs]]''|| Najibullah Lafraie || 3 July – 27 September 1996 |} ==Islamic Emirate (1996–2001)== {{see also|Rabbani Council of Ministers|Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan (1996–2001)#Government}} ===Rabbani Provisional Council of Ministers (1996–2001)=== {{More citations needed|date=February 2025}} On 27 September 1996, the [[Taliban]] captured Kabul, forcing the old government to flee from the city.[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/09/28/world/guerrillas-take-afghan-capital-as-troops-flee.html ''GUERRILLAS TAKE AFGHAN CAPITAL AS TROOPS FLEE''], [[The New York Times]], 28 September 1996. They also set up a six-member provisional council, among them Information and Culture Minister [[Amir Khan Muttaqi]], Foreign Minister [[Mohammad Ghaus Akhund]],[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/10/07/world/afghanistan-s-professional-class-flees-rule-by-ultra-strict-clerics.html ''Afghanistan's Professional Class Flees Rule by Ultra-Strict Clerics''], [[The New York Times]], 7 October 1996. Deputy Foreign Minister [[Sher Mohammad Abbas Stanikzai]],[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/10/02/world/afghanistan-s-new-rulers-soft-pedal-their-hard-line.html ''Afghanistan's New Rulers Soft-Pedal Their Hard Line''], [[The New York Times]], 2 October 1996. Health Minister [[Massoumi Afghan]][https://www.nytimes.com/1996/10/04/world/walled-in-shrouded-and-angry-in-afghanistan.html ''Walled In, Shrouded and Angry in Afghanistan''], [[The New York Times]], 4 October 1996. and others,[https://www.nytimes.com/1996/09/29/world/islamic-rebels-pursue-retreating-afghan-army.html ''Islamic Rebels Pursue Retreating Afghan Army''], [[The New York Times]], 29 September 1996. to govern the capital and named [[Mohammad Rabbani]], who is not related to [[Islamic State of Afghanistan|Islamic State]]'s president [[Burhanuddin Rabbani]], to lead the Council of Ministers. The Taliban declared the [[Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan]], which was only recognized by Pakistan, [[Saudi Arabia]] and the [[United Arab Emirates]], though Pakistan and the United Emirates later withdrew their recognition after the [[September 11 attacks]]. All other states continued to recognize the [[Islamic State of Afghanistan]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ Rabbani Provisional Council of Ministers[https://www.spiegel.de/wirtschaft/1-teil-personen-a-159428.html ''Personen''], [[Der Spiegel]], 26 September 2001 (German).[https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2002/881/oj/eng# ''Council Regulation (EC) No 881/2002 of 27 May 2002 imposing certain specific restrictive measures directed against certain persons and entities associated with Usama bin Laden, the Al-Qaida network and the Taliban, and repealing Council Regulation (EC) No 467/2001 prohibiting the export of certain goods and services to Afghanistan, strengthening the flight ban and extending the freeze of funds and other financial resources in respect of the Taliban of Afghanistan''][https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/ALL/?uri=CELEX:32007R0969 ''Commission Regulation (EC) No 969/2007 of 17 August 2007 amending for the 83rd time Council Regulation (EC) No 881/2002 imposing certain specific restrictive measures directed against certain persons and entities associated with Usama bin Laden, the Al-Qaida network and the Taliban, and repealing Council Regulation (EC) No 467/2001''][https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:32017R0404 ''COUNCIL IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) 2017/404 of 7 March 2017 implementing Article 11(4) of Regulation (EU) No 753/2011 concerning restrictive measures directed against certain individuals, groups, undertakings and entities in view of the situation in Afghanistan''][https://press.un.org/en/2001/afg169.doc.htm ''SECURITY COUNCIL COMMITTEE ESTABLISHED PURSUANT TO RESOLUTION 1267 (1999) CONCERNING AFGHANISTAN ISSUES A NEW CONSOLIDATED LIST'']Hong Kong Monetary Authority: ''Consolidated List. style="background:{{party color|Taliban}}"| | [[Taliban]] |- | Muhammad Taher Anwari | before 23 February – 13 November 2001 ! style="background:{{party color|Taliban}}"| | [[Taliban]] |} ==Transitional Islamic State (2001–2004)== ===Post-Taliban transitional phase (2001)=== When [[Fall of Kabul (2001)|Kabul fell to the Northern Alliance on 13 November 2001]], former President [[Burhanuddin Rabbani]] returned to the [[Arg (Kabul)|Arg]] on 17 November.{{cite web | url=https://www.washingtontimes.com/news/2001/nov/18/20011118-031220-5203r/?utm_source=chatgpt.com | title=Rabbani returns to Kabul, vows to back coalition | website=[[The Washington Times]]}} While the Taliban government [[Fall of Kandahar|still controlled Kandahar for several weeks]], around two dozen prominent Afghans convened at the [[Bonn Conference]] to establish an interim administration and set a timeline for adopting a new [[Constitution of Afghanistan|constitution]] and holding democratic elections.{{cite news| url=http://www.nps.edu/Academics/centers/ccc/publications/OnlineJournal/2006/Feb/johnsonFeb06.pdf| title=The Prospects for Post-Conflict Afghanistan: A Call of the Sirens to the Country's Troubled Past| volume=V| issue=2| date=February 2006| first=Thomas H.| last=Johnson| publisher=[[Strategic Insights]]| access-date=2009-06-29| archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100224025719/http://www.nps.edu/Academics/centers/ccc/publications/OnlineJournal/2006/Feb/johnsonFeb06.pdf| url-status=live| archive-date=2010-02-24}} Concerns arose that Rabbani might attempt to retain power and marginalize [[Pashtuns|Pashtun]] and [[Shia Islam|Shia]] opposition groups, but he ultimately cooperated and supported the formation of a representative government. On 6 December 2001, it was decided that Pashtun leader [[Hamid Karzai]] would assume leadership. Until the official transition on 22 December, Rabbani remained ''de jure'' president of Afghanistan, as the [[United Nations]] had never recognized the Taliban's legitimacy.https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/2001/12/23/new-afghan-leader-sworn-in/6e104b79-cf67-4851-aab3-7591f2c32945/ {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2025}} However, the ''de facto'' power rested with the [[Northern Alliance]], which established a "Supreme Military Council" to administer the newly captured territories.{{cite news|title=Factions Asked To Join Together in Post-Taliban Afghanistan|url=http://komonews.com/archive/factions-asked-to-join-together-in-post-taliban-afghanistan|access-date=22 July 2016|newspaper=KOMO News|date=13 November 2001}} The council, which ruled out a return of Rabbani, declared a three-month mandate.{{cite news|title=Ousted Afghan President won't go back yet|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=95kVAAAAIBAJ&sjid=mAsEAAAAIBAJ&pg=2366,1691316&dq=mohammad+fahim&hl=en|access-date=17 March 2012|newspaper=Manila Standard|date=16 November 2001}} Council positions were held by close [[Ahmad Shah Massoud|Massoud]]-allies: [[Mohammad Qasim Fahim]] as head of the council and Defense Minister, [[Yunus Qanuni]] as acting Interior Minister{{cite web | url=https://taz.de/Chaos-um-Kundus/!1139351/ | title=Chaos um Kundus | work=Die Tageszeitung: taz | date=23 November 2001}} and [[Abdullah Abdullah]] as acting Foreign Minister.{{cite web | url=https://www.tagesspiegel.de/politik/afghanistan-nordallianz-will-verhandeln-852265.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com | title=Politik: Afghanistan: Nordallianz will verhandeln | work=Der Tagesspiegel Online}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+ '''Supreme Military Council''' |- ! ([[Ghazni Province|Ghazni]]) |- |} ===Karzai transitional cabinet (2002–2004)=== {{Main|Afghan Transitional Administration}} The [[Bonn Agreement (Afghanistan)|Bonn Agreement]] of December 2001 had installed an interim government, the [[2002 Loya Jirga]] subsequently elected a transitional administration. ([[Balkh Province|Balkh]]) |- | rowspan="2" | [[Ministry of Counter Narcotics|Counter Narcotics]] | [[Habibullah Qaderi]]alternative name(s): ''(Engineer) Habibullah Qadiri''. | 24 December 2004 – March 2008 | | ''unknown'' | [[Pashtuns|Pashtun]] ([[Kandahar Province|Kandahar]]) |- | [[Khodaidad]]alternative name(s): ''(Lieutenant General) Khudaidad, Khodaidad Hazara''. | March 2008 – 18 January 2010 !style="background:#03913D;"| | *pro-[[Northern Alliance]] *[[Hezb-e Wahdat]] *pro-[[Karim Khalili|Khalili]] | [[Hazaras|Hazara]] ([[Uruzgan Province|Uruzgan]]) |} === Second Karzai cabinet (2010–2015) === {{Main|Second Karzai cabinet}} After winning a second term, President [[Hamid Karzai]] nominated 23 ministers in December 2009 to be part of his [[Presidency of Hamid Karzai|new administration]] but only 7 were approved by the [[National Assembly (Afghanistan)|National Assembly]]. All the other candidates that Karzai initially selected were rejected by members of the National Assembly.{{cite news |last=Rubin |first=Alissa J. |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2010/01/05/world/asia/05karzai.html |title=Standoff Builds Over Afghan Cabinet |location=Afghanistan |work=The New York Times |date=4 January 2010 |access-date=23 November 2011 |archive-date=25 November 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111125234103/http://www.nytimes.com/2010/01/05/world/asia/05karzai.html |url-status=live}} Karzai presented a second list of 18 candidates to the [[Wolesi Jirga]] on 9 January 2010. A week later, the Wolesi Jirga again approved only seven of the candidates.{{cite web |url=http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/2010-01/16/content_12821148.htm |title=Afghan parliament approves 7 new ministers |publisher=News.xinhuanet.com |date=16 January 2010 |access-date=23 November 2011 |archive-date=9 June 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110609150448/http://news.xinhuanet.com/english/2010-01/16/content_12821148.htm |url-status=dead}} Since then, part of the ministries have been governed by acting ministers who do not held approval of the Afghan legislature. The 14 approved ministers were sworn in on 18 January 2010.{{cite web | title=January 2010 | url=https://www.rulers.org/2010-01.html }}{{cite web | title=Taliban militants attack Afghan capital; 5 killed | website=[[Los Angeles Times]] | date=18 January 2010 | url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2010-jan-18-la-fg-afghan-kabul18-2010jan18-story.html }}{{cite web | title=Suicide bombers, gunmen attack central Kabul - Afghanistan | ReliefWeb | date=18 January 2010 | url=https://reliefweb.int/report/afghanistan/suicide-bombers-gunmen-attack-central-kabul }} Major changes to the cabinet were made when the Wolesi held votes of confidence for nominated ministers, particularly on 28 June 2010 were five ministers were approved, leaving only six of the 25 ministries left with an acting minister,{{cite web |url=http://www.afghan-web.com/politics/cabinet_members.html |title=Afghanistan Online: Cabinet of ministers |publisher=Afghan-web.com |access-date=23 November 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100330151322/http://www.afghan-web.com/politics/cabinet_members.html |archive-date=30 March 2010 |url-status=dead}} 5 March 2012, 15 September 2012,[https://www.afghanistan-analysts.org/en/reports/political-landscape/filling-the-power-ministries-3-three-security-bosses-voted-in/ ''Filling the Power Ministries (3): Three Security Bosses Voted In''], Afghanistan Analysts Network, 15 September 2012. 25 September 2013[https://tolonews.com/afghanistan/omer-daudzai-wins-vote-confidence-appointed-interior-minister ''Omer Daudzai Wins "Vote of Confidence," Appointed as Interior Minister''], [[Tolo News]], 25 September 2013. and 25 December 2013.[https://tolonews.com/index.php/afghanistan/mps-approve-five-new-ministers-cabinet ''MPs Approve Five New Ministers to Cabinet''], [[Tolo News]], 25 December 2013. When [[Ashraf Ghani]] was inaugurated as the new [[President of Afghanistan]] on 29 September 2014, he kept the ministers of the Karzai cabinet as acting ministers until the new [[National Unity Government]] could be formed with [[Abdullah Abdullah]]. However, after two months at the end of November 2014, he replaced all acting ministers with their respective deputy ministers to show that he would respect the constitution were it says a minister can only stay in office in an acting role for a maximum of two months. These new cabinet ministers stayed in office until the new government was formed in February 2015. {| class="wikitable sortable" |+ '''Karzai cabinet''' |- ! '''Portfolio''' !! '''Name''' !! ([[Paktia Province|Paktia]]) |- | Bismillah Khan Mohammadi | 30 June 2010 – 15 September 2012Was approved by the [[Wolesi Jirga]] on 28 June 2010 before taking the oath of office on 30 June 2010; see [https://www.securitycouncilreport.org/atf/cf/%7B65BFCF9B-6D27-4E9C-8CD3-CF6E4FF96FF9%7D/Afgh%20S%202010%20548.pdf ''Letter dated 15 October 2010 from the Secretary-General addressed to the President of the Security Council''], [[United Nations]], 15 October 2010.Was voted out by the [[Wolesi Jirga]] on 4 August 2012 but stayed in office as acting minister until 15 September 2012; see [https://www.afghanistan-analysts.org/en/reports/political-landscape/parliament-sacks-key-ministers-two-birds-with-one-stone/ ''Parliament sacks key ministers: Two birds with one stone?''], Afghanistan Analysts Network, 6 August 2012, and [https://www.bakhtarnews.af/en/unseated-ministers-to-continue-work-until-replacement/ ''Unseated Ministers To Continue Work Until Replacement''], [[Bakhtar News Agency]], 6 August 2012. !style="background:#03913D;"| | *[[Northern Alliance]] *[[Jamiat-e Islami]] *pro-[[Ahmad Shah Massoud|Massoud]] *pro-[[Mohammad Qasim Fahim|Fahim]] | [[Tajiks|Tajik]]
([[Panjshir Province|Panjshir]]) |- | [[Ghulam Mujtaba Patang]]alternative name(s): ''(Major General) Mujtaba Patang, Mushtaba Patang''. | 15 September 2012 – 1 September 2013Patang failed to get the vote of confidence by the [[Wolesi Jirga]] on 22 July 2013 ([https://pajhwok.com/2013/07/22/wolesi-jirga-sacks-interior-minister/ ''Wolesi Jirga sacks interior minister''], [[Pajhwok Afghan News]], 22 July 2013) but stayed in power as acting minister ([https://www.afghanistan-analysts.org/en/reports/political-landscape/too-cocky-for-parliaments-taste-interior-minister-voted-out/ ''Too Cocky for Parliament’s Taste? Interior Minister voted out''], Afghanistan Analysts Network, 22 July 2013, and [https://www.reuters.com/article/2013/07/22/us-afghanistan-minister-idUSBRE96L0D720130722/ ''Afghan parliament sacks interior minister over security issues''], [[Reuters]], 22 July 2013). ! style="background:{{party color|Independent politician}}"| | *pro-[[Khalq]] *pro-[[Atta Mohammad Noor|Noor]] *pro-[[Mohammad Qasim Fahim|Fahim]] | [[Pashtuns|Pashtun]]
([[Logar Province|Logar]]) |- | [[Mohammad Omar Daudzai]]alternative name(s): ''Omar Daudzai, Umer Dawoodzai, Omar Davodzi, Umar Daudzai, Mohammad Umer Daudzai, Muhammad Omar Daudzai''. | 1 September 2013 – 1 December 2014Daudzai was named acting Interior Minister on 1 September 2013 ([https://www.rferl.org/a/daudzai-afghanistan-interior-election-security/25092360.html ''Karzai Appoints Interim Interior Minister''], [[Radio Free Europe / Radio Liberty]], 1 September 2013) and was approved by the [[Wolesi Jirga]] on 25 September 2013 ([https://tolonews.com/afghanistan/omer-daudzai-wins-vote-confidence-appointed-interior-minister]). ! style="background:{{party color|Independent politician}}"| | *pro-[[Hezb-e Islami]][https://www.afghanistan-analysts.org/en/reports/political-landscape/daudzai-in-and-out-pre-election-cabinet-reshuffles/ ''Daudzai In – and Out: Pre-election cabinet reshuffles''], Afghanistan Analysts Network, 3 September 2013. | [[Pashtuns|Pashtun]]

([[Ghazni Province|Ghazni]]) |- |} ==Islamic Emirate (2021–present)== ===Post-Republic transitional government (2021)=== On the first days of the new regime, tentative nominations to the cabinet were announced until late August 2021, including the acting ministers of [[Ministry of Public Works (Afghanistan)|Public Works]],{{cite web | last1=Lalzoy | first1=Najibullah | title=Azizullah Fazli appointed as acting chairman of Afghanistan Cricket Board | work=Khaama Press | date=22 August 2021 | url=https://www.khaama.com/azizullah-fazli-appointed-as-acting-chairman-of-afghanistan-cricket-board-63464/ }} [[Ministry of Water and Energy (Afghanistan)|Water and Energy]], [[Ministry of Education (Afghanistan)|Education]] and the acting Head of the [[Da Afghanistan Bank|Central Bank]] among other high-ranking officials. Supreme Leader [[Hibatullah Akhundzada]] directly appointed the caretaking ministers. Additionally, [[Wahid Majrooh]], a minister of the old regime, stayed in office after cooperating with the Taliban until the end of September. {| class="wikitable" |+ '''Taliban transitional cabinet''' |- ! ([[Herat Province|Herat]]) |- | [[Ministry of Water and Energy (Afghanistan)|Water and Energy]] | ''unknown'' | 22 August – 7 September 2021{{cite web | last1=Lalzoy | first1=Najibullah | title=Taliban to hold grand gathering on new government in Kabul | work=Khaama Press | date=22 August 2021 | url=https://www.khaama.com/taliban-to-hold-grand-gathering-on-new-government-in-kabul-567456/ }} |- |} ===Akhund caretaker cabinet (2021–)=== {{Main|Akhund cabinet}} On 7 September 2021, a men-only "caretaker cabinet"{{cite web |title=Statement of Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan regarding cabinet announcement |url=https://alemarahenglish.af/?p=48478 |website=Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan – Voice of Jihad |access-date=20 April 2022 |date=7 September 2021 |quote=The Islamic Emirate has decided to appoint and announce a caretaker cabinet to undertake necessary governmental tasks. |archive-date=5 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211205154736/https://alemarahenglish.af/?p=48478 |url-status=live}} was appointed by Akhundzada, headed by [[Hasan Akhund]] as [[Prime Minister of Afghanistan|Prime Minister]]. The [[Ministry of Women's Affairs (Afghanistan)|Ministry of Women's Affairs]] was abolished. This was followed by three more major rounds of appointments on 21 September,{{Cite web|url=https://8am.af/eng/the-taliban-announce-remaining-cabinet-members/|title=The Taliban Announce Remaining Cabinet Members|date=21 September 2021|access-date=23 September 2021|archive-date=24 November 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211124145820/https://8am.af/eng/the-taliban-announce-remaining-cabinet-members/|url-status=dead}} 4 October[https://8am.media/eng/taliban-held-their-first-cabinet-meeting/ ''Taliban Held Their First Cabinet Meeting''], 8 AM Daily, 5 October 2021. and 23 November 2021.{{Cite news|url = https://pajhwok.com/2021/11/23/interim-cabinet-expanded-corps-commanders-named/|title = Interim cabinet expanded; corps commanders named|date = 23 November 2021|access-date = 25 November 2021|archive-date = 25 November 2021|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20211125012713/https://pajhwok.com/2021/11/23/interim-cabinet-expanded-corps-commanders-named/|url-status = live}} Among those were the nominations of two Taliban veterans as deputy ministers.{{cite web |last1=Gopalakrishnan |first1=Raju |last2=Mackenzie |first2=James |title=Taliban appoint hardline battlefield commanders to key Afghan posts |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/taliban-appoint-hardline-battlefield-commanders-key-afghan-posts-2021-09-21/ |website=Reuters |access-date=22 September 2021 |date=21 September 2021 |archive-date=28 September 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210928162047/https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/taliban-appoint-hardline-battlefield-commanders-key-afghan-posts-2021-09-21/ |url-status=live}} {| class="wikitable" |+ '''Akhund cabinet'''[https://ctrf.mf.gov.dz/pdf/presse-en/recap_eng.pdf]{{cite web | url=https://www.aa.com.tr/en/asia-pacific/whos-who-in-taliban-interim-government/2360424 | title=Who's who in Taliban interim government?}}{{cite web | url=https://www.afghanistan-analysts.org/en/reports/political-landscape/the-talebans-caretaker-cabinet-and-other-senior-appointments/ | title=The Taleban's Caretaker Cabinet and other Senior Appointments | date=7 October 2021}} |- ! notes= | sidebox= }} The '''Canada convoy protest''', known as the '''Freedom Convoy''' (French: ''Convoi de la liberté''), was a series of protests and blockades across [[Canada]] in early 2022, initially organized to oppose [[COVID-19 vaccination in Canada|COVID-19 vaccine mandates]] for cross-border truck drivers. The movement quickly expanded to protest all COVID-19 restrictions and mandates. Beginning on January 22, 2022, hundreds of vehicles departed from various locations across Canada, converging in [[Ottawa]] on January 29 for a rally at [[Parliament Hill]], joined by thousands of pedestrian protesters. Parallel demonstrations occurred in provincial capitals and at key border crossings with the United States. The protests followed the end of vaccine mandate exemptions for cross-border truckers, which had been in place to mitigate [[2021 supply chain crisis|supply chain disruptions]]. Approximately 85% of Canadian truck drivers serving cross-border routes were vaccinated, but the new restrictions potentially affected up to 16,000 drivers. Protesters called for the repeal of all COVID-19 mandates and restrictions, citing concerns over [[Civil liberties|personal freedoms]] and government overreach. While some officials and businesses raised concerns about the economic impact of the blockades, the demonstrations drew both domestic and international support, including from members of the [[Conservative Party of Canada]] and [[Republican Party (United States)|Republican]] politicians in the [[United States]]. The federal government responded by invoking the ''[[Emergencies Act]]'' on February 14, 2022, granting extraordinary powers to law enforcement. By February 21, most blockades and protests had been dismantled through large-scale police operations. As part of these measures, the government [[Asset freezing|froze bank accounts]] linked to protest organizers. Following the seizure, some supporters turned to [[Bitcoin]] to make donations to the convoy, citing its resistance to government control.{{Cite web |last=Sutton |first=Sam |date=2022-02-28 |title=Trucker crypto crackdown in Canada fuels GOP backlash in U.S. |url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/28/trucker-crypto-crackdown-in-canada-fuels-gop-backlash-in-u-s-00011401 |access-date=2025-06-12 |website=POLITICO |language=en}} The convoy drew a mixed response from the public and various organizations. Trucking groups criticized the protests, asserting most participants were not truckers. Allegations of involvement by [[Far-right politics|far-right]] groups and calls for the federal government to be overthrown were also raised, as were concerns about the seizure of weapons near a blockade in [[Coutts, Alberta]]. Supporters saw the movement as a grassroots stand for liberty against government overreach. ==Background== {{See also|Timeline of the Canada convoy protest}} ===Protest goals=== [[File:Freedom Convoy 2022 Ottawa February 4-23.jpg|thumb|left|Anti-vaccine protesters at the convoy in Ottawa]] The protest called for the end of [[Vaccine passports during the COVID-19 pandemic|vaccine mandates]] in Canada during the ongoing [[COVID-19 pandemic]].{{cite web|date=2022-01-28|title=Here's what you need to know about the truck convoy moving into Ottawa today|url=https://ottawa.ctvnews.ca/here-s-what-you-need-to-know-about-the-truck-convoy-moving-into-ottawa-today-1.5757761|access-date=2022-01-28|website=CTV News Ottawa|archive-date=January 28, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220128150858/https://ottawa.ctvnews.ca/here-s-what-you-need-to-know-about-the-truck-convoy-moving-into-ottawa-today-1.5757761|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|last=Seto|first=Chris|date=2022-01-27|title='Freedom Convoy' highlights frustrations over COVID-19 mandates|url=https://www.therecord.com/news/waterloo-region/2022/01/27/freedom-rally-to-roll-through-waterloo-region-thursday-morning.html|access-date=2022-01-28|website=therecord.com}} Freedom Convoy's spokesperson Ben Dichter stated on [[Fox News]] that, "we want to get rid of the vaccine mandates and the [vaccine] passports. And that passport, that's the really concerning one." The organizers of the original mid-January crowdsourced fundraising campaign on [[GoFundMe]], [[Tamara Lich]] and Dichter, both of whom are not long-haul truckers, described the goal of the truck convoy, from across Canada to the nation's capital, as a demonstration against the newly implemented January 15 federal cross-border COVID-19 vaccine mandate, targeting long-haul truck drivers, according to a January 28, 2022 [[CTV News]] article.{{cite news|last=Aiello|first=Rachel|title=Freedom Convoy: What is its aim?|work=CTV News|location=Ottawa, Ontario|access-date=2022-02-02|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/article/what-does-the-trucker-convoy-hope-to-accomplish/}} Several protesters voiced opposition to perceived authoritarianism and corruption by [[Justin Trudeau]], stating they wanted him "out of office", while others said, "this is not an anti-vaccination movement, this is a freedom movement."{{cite web|date=2022-01-29|title='Freedom Convoy' rolls into town, jams Parliamentary Precinct, thousands protest against COVID-19 mandates|url=https://www.hilltimes.com/2022/01/29/freedom-convoy-rolls-into-town-jams-parliamentary-precinct-thousands-protest-against-covid-19-mandates/341134|access-date=2022-01-30|website=The Hill Times|language=en-CA}}{{cite web|title=Big rigs, passenger vehicles snarl downtown Ottawa as trucker convoy parks by Parliament|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8580547/trucker-convoy-freedom-covid-vaccine-mandate-ottawa/|access-date=2022-01-30|website=Global News|language=en-US}} For example, [[Canada Unity]]'s Ontario organizer for the convoy, Jason LaFace, said that the intent of the Canada Unity protest was to dissolve the federal government. Many of the restrictions that the protesters objected to were under provincial jurisdiction.{{cite news|last1=Meyers|first1=Jeffrey B.|title=Candice Bergen's nod to Trump is a sign of Canada's descent, but the Charter may save us|url=https://theconversation.com/candice-bergens-nod-to-trump-is-a-sign-of-canadas-descent-but-the-charter-may-save-us-176785|access-date=2022-02-10|work=The Conversation|publisher=Academic Journalism Society|date=2022-02-09|location=Toronto ON}} ===Initial events and the start of the protest=== On November 16, 2021, [[Canada Border Services Agency]] officers refused truck driver [[Brigitte Belton]] entry to Canada at the [[Detroit–Windsor tunnel]] due to her failure to wear a face mask.{{cite web|last1=MacCharles|first1=Tonda|last2=Ballingall|first2=Alex|date=2022-10-13|title=How one trucker's TikTok video turned into the 'Freedom Convoy,' according to inquiry lawyers|url=https://www.thestar.com/politics/federal/2022/10/13/how-one-truckers-tiktok-video-turned-into-the-freedom-convoy-according-to-inquiry-lawyers.html|access-date=2022-11-05|website=thestar.com}} At the October 2022 [[Emergencies Act#Rouleau inquiry|Public Order Emergency Commission]], commission lawyer Stephen Armstrong stated that Belton vented her frustration via her [[TikTok]] feed before using the platform to connect with [[Christopher John Barber|Chris Barber]] and then later initiating the planning of the Canada convoy protest.{{cite web|last1=Osman|first1=Laura|last2=Taylor|first2=Stephanie|last3=Fraser|first3=David|date=2022-11-01|title='Power struggle' made it hard to manage 'Freedom Convoy,' inquiry hears|url=https://www.thepeterboroughexaminer.com/ts/politics/2022/11/01/freedom-convoy-leaders-to-testify-at-emergencies-act-inquiry.html|access-date=2022-11-05|website=thepeterboroughexaminer.com}} Armstrong said that Belton, Chris Barber, and [[James Bauder]] all took part in a [[Facebook live]] event on January 13, 2022, to plan the protest's route and logistics. The next day, [[Tamara Lich]] started the fundraiser for the protest. ====American influence==== [[File:Freedom Convoy 2022, Ottawa, February 12 (18).jpg|thumb|left|alt=In a dense crowd of people wearing winter clothing in front of a building, several hold flags bearing the National Flag of Canada. Standing shoulder-to-shoulder, the vast majority are not wearing face masks. A large Gadsden flag and a large sign with the words "We are the fringe" are visually prominent.|A large [[Gadsden flag]] and a large sign with the words "We are the fringe" are seen in a crowd in [[Ottawa]] on February 12, 2022. [[Justin Trudeau]] had called the convoy protesters a "fringe minority."]] Ottawa's police chief, [[Peter Sloly]], described a "significant element" of American involvement in the organization and funding of the convoys.{{cite news|last=Coletta|first=Amanda|date=2022-02-02|title='Significant element' from U.S. involved in self-described 'Freedom Convoy' in Canada, official says|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/02/freedom-convoy-alberta-blockade-vaccine-mandate-protests/|url-status=live|url-access=limited|access-date=2022-02-07|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220202130646/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/02/freedom-convoy-alberta-blockade-vaccine-mandate-protests/|archive-date=2022-02-02}} Some of the donors participating in the protest's [[GoFundMe]] campaign were from the United States, and many used false names or remained anonymous.{{cite news|last1=Elizabeth|first1=Thompson|last2=Rocha|first2=Roberto|date=2022-01-28|title=Large number of donations to support convoy came from aliases, unnamed donors|work=[[CBC News]]|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/vaccine-protest-convoy-gofundme-donations-1.6330594|access-date=2022-05-22}} ''CBC News'' and ''The New York Times'' described the protests as a "''[[cause célèbre]]''" for the [[Right-wing politics in the United States|American right]].{{cite news|last=Panetta|first=Alexander|date=2022-02-08|title=Ottawa protest inspires talk of copycat convoys in U.S. and beyond|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/ottawa-truck-protest-convoy-us-1.6343784|work=[[CBC News]]|access-date=2022-02-08}} The convoys and protests were widely praised and promoted by American conservative political figures,{{cite news|last=Lithwick|first=Dahlia|date=2022-02-07|title=Canadians Aren't Actually on the Brink of Civil War|url=https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2022/02/why-ottawas-freedom-convoy-is-a-fringe-political-demonstration.html|work=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]|access-date=2022-02-08}}{{cite news|last=McCarten|first=James|date=2022-02-07|title=Trump backers seize on GoFundMe controversy as truckers linger in U.S. headlines|url=https://www.cp24.com/news/trump-backers-seize-on-gofundme-controversy-as-truckers-linger-in-u-s-headlines-1.5771639|work=[[The Canadian Press]]|access-date=2022-02-07}} who have been accused of propagating exaggerated claims about the protest, or making unfounded claims about the roles and actions of Canadian authorities and politicians with respect to the protest.{{cite news|last=Garossino|first=Sandy|date=2022-02-04|title=Startling Trump interference points to GoFundMe convoy campaign manipulation|url=https://www.nationalobserver.com/2022/02/04/opinion/startling-trump-interference-points-gofundme-convoy-campaign-manipulation|work=[[Canada's National Observer]]|access-date=2022-02-07}} The protest was endorsed by Trump, who, during his time in power, had regularly clashed with Trudeau over policy differences.{{cite news|last1=Dale|first1=Daniel|last2=Ballingall|first2=Alex|date=2018-06-12|title=Trump says he'll punish 'the people of Canada' because of Trudeau's news conference|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/world/2018/06/12/we-just-shook-hands-trump-confused-by-trudeaus-pushed-around-comment-after-g7-summit.html|work=[[Toronto Star]]|access-date=2022-02-10}} In response to the convoy, Trump referred to Trudeau as a "far-left lunatic" who "has destroyed Canada", and invited protesters to organize on [[Truth Social]], his proposed social network.{{cite news|last=Lum|first=Zi-Ann|date=2022-02-04|title=Trump calls Trudeau "far left lunatic" as siege continues in Ottawa|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/04/trump-trudeau-ottawa-protests-00005944|work=[[Politico]]|access-date=2022-02-09}} Various commentators have called the Freedom Convoy an example of Canadian Trumpism.{{cite news|last1=Maher|first1=Stephen|date=2022-02-01|title=The Conservative revolt has begun|work=Maclean's|publisher=St. Joseph Communications|location=Toronto ON|url=https://www.macleans.ca/politics/the-conservative-revolt-has-begun/|access-date=2022-02-10|quote=Wells gives us a quick history lesson, reviewing the "east/west, insider/outsider shimmy" of Conservative politics in recent decades, although an infusion of Trumpism makes it hard to figure out what will happen this time.}} *{{cite news|last1=Nasrallah|first1=Elie Mikhael|date=2022-02-09|title=Nasrallah: Trucker protest marks Ottawa's loss of innocence|work=Ottawa Citizen|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/opinion/nasrallah-trucker-protest-marks-ottawas-loss-of-innocence|access-date=2022-02-10|quote=The loss of innocence is making Canadians into mini-Americans in the era of nativism, Trumpism and right-wing uprisings. The American disease has arrived in the winter in 2022. Canada and Ottawa may never be the same.}} *{{cite news|last1=DiMatteo|first1=Enzo|date=2022-01-31|title=#FreedomConvoy2022 and Canada's descent into Trumpism|work=NOW Toronto|location=Toronto ON|url=https://nowtoronto.com/news/freedomconvoy2022-the-end-of-erin-otoole-and-canadas-descent-into-trumpism|access-date=2022-02-10}} *{{cite news|last1=Kilian|first1=Crawford|date=2022-01-31|title=Where the Convoy Is Trying to Drive Canada|work=The Tyee|location=Vancouver BC|url=https://thetyee.ca/Analysis/2022/01/31/Where-Convoy-Trying-To-Drive-Canada/|access-date=2022-02-10|quote=The destination is the ditch of Trumpism, our democratic institutions undercut by paranoid illiberalism.}} *{{cite news|last1=Kay|first1=Jonathan|date=2022-02-07|title=Canada's truck convoy is just a stunt in a country where populism is still taboo|newspaper=The Washington Post|location=Washington DC|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/outlook/2022/02/07/canada-truck-convoy-ottawa/|access-date=2022-02-10|quote=Each faces the same single existential decision in regard to the brand of outlying conservative populism that announces itself with a horn: (a) Let the outliers into the big tent, and watch the Conservatives be excoriated in the media as Canada's answer to Trumpism, or (b) cast them out, and send them fleeing to the People's Party, which will then siphon off support on the Conservatives' right flank.}} *{{cite news|last1=Poling|first1=Jim Sr.|date=2022-02-02|title=Following a convoy of courage|work=The Minden Times|publisher=White Pine Media Corp|location=Minden ON|url=https://mindentimes.ca/opinion/following-a-convoy-of-courage/|access-date=2022-02-10|quote=The truckers' convoy didn't bring dreams to Ottawa. They brought Nazi banners, Confederate flags, anger, hatred and other relics of American Trumpism. This is a country that neither needs, nor wants Trumpism. It's a country that needs respectful protests, positive actions, much better leadership and appreciation of its heroes.}} *{{cite tweet|last1=Singh|first1=Jagmeet|date=2022-02-08|title=The spread of Trumpism into Canada must be stopped. Foreign actors and money cannot be allowed to sow division in Canada. US interference from the extreme right and millions of dollars via anonymous foreign sources must be shut down.|user=thejagmeetsingh|number=1491098260039208970}} [[Mark Carney]], former [[Governor of the Bank of Canada]] and future prime minister, strongly criticized the foreign involvement in "the sedition in Ottawa," describing donors as "foreign funders of an insurrection" who had "interfered in our domestic affairs."{{cite news|last=Carney|first=Mark|date=2022-02-07|title=It's time to end the sedition in Ottawa by enforcing the law and following the money|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/opinion/article-mark-carney-end-freedom-convoy-ottawa-state-of-emergency/|work=[[The Globe and Mail]]|access-date=2022-02-07}} ====Fundraising==== Fundraising started on January 14, 2022, through the crowdsourced fundraising platform [[GoFundMe]]. Listed as organizers were [[Tamara Lich]], secretary of the [[Alberta separatism|western separatist]] [[Maverick Party]], and B. J. Dichter, a 2015 federal Conservative Party candidate now associated with the People's Party of Canada.{{cite web|last=Gilmore|first=Rachel|title=Some trucker convoy organizers have history of white nationalism, racism|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8543281/covid-trucker-convoy-organizers-hate/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=Global News|language=en-US}} The Maverick Party has denied involvement in the convoy and said it was not the recipient of any funds raised. The convoy's GoFundMe raised over {{CAD|5 million}} by January 25. On January 24, GoFundMe stated funds raised would not be released until the fundraising organizers could demonstrate how the funds would be properly distributed.{{cite tweet|user=glen_mcgregor|number=1485363882151579651|title=Statement from GoFundMe to CTV News about the fundraiser for #TruckersForFreedom Freedom Convoy 2022 that has raised $2.5 million.}} On January 27, GoFundMe released the initial CA$1 million of the funds after the organizers had provided a distribution plan.{{cite news|title=GoFundMe has released $1M of more than $6M raised for protest convoy|first=Catharine|last=Tunney|date=2022-01-27|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/gofundme-money-released-convoy-1.6328029|access-date=2022-02-02}} On February 2, GoFundMe flagged the fundraiser for review and paused donations, saying it was investigating to ensure the fundraiser was in compliance with the platform's [[terms of service]].{{cite news|title=GoFundMe pauses donations to 'Freedom convoy' as fundraiser surpasses $10 million|first=Lex|last=Harvey|newspaper=Toronto Star|date=2022-02-03|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/canada/2022/02/03/gofundme-pauses-donations-to-freedom-convoy-as-fundraiser-surpasses-10-million.html|access-date=2022-02-04}} The fundraiser had raised {{CAD|10,096,500}} from approximately 120,000 donors.{{cite news|title=GoFundMe pauses anti-vaccine mandate protest fundraiser|first=Elizabeth|last=Thompson|date=2022-02-02|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/convoy-fundraiser-pause-donors-1.6337375|access-date=2022-02-04}} On February 3, the [[Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Public Safety and National Security]] (SECU) called on GoFundMe officials to testify before it, to respond to concerns about the origin of the funds raised and to ensure they would not be used to "promote extremism".{{cite news|title=MPs vote to call GoFundMe to testify at Commons committee on convoy protest|first=Elizabeth|last=Thompson|date=2022-02-03|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/convoy-protest-mps-gofundme-1.6338433|access-date=2022-02-04}} [[Member of Parliament (Canada)|Members of Parliament]] also called on the [[Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada]] (FINTRAC), Canada's [[financial intelligence]] agency, to appear before the committee. [[CBC News]] reported that a third of the donations were anonymous or used false names for the publicly visible part of the campaign, attempting to prevent their [[doxxing]], and that commenters on the fundraiser claimed to be from the United States, United Kingdom, and other countries. On the evening of February 4, GoFundMe removed the campaign permanently for violating their terms of service,{{cite news|last1=Judd|first1=Amy|title=GoFundMe for Canada's trucker convoy removed for violating 'terms of service'|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8596591/gofundme-trucker-convoy-removed/|access-date=2022-02-04|work=Global News|date=2022-02-04|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite news|title='An occuption': GoFundMe pulls plug on fundraiser for convoy protesters|url=https://nationalpost.com/news/an-occuption-gofundme-pulls-plug-on-fundraiser-for-convoy-protesters|access-date=2022-02-04|agency=The Canadian Press|date=2022-02-04|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite news|last1=Otis|first1=Daniel|title=Trucker convoy fundraiser removed from GoFundMe|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/trucker-convoy-fundraiser-removed-from-gofundme/|access-date=2022-02-04|work=CTV News|date=2022-02-04|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite news|last1=Boisvert|first1=Nick|title=GoFundMe ends payments to convoy protest, citing reports of violence and harassment|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/gofundme-stops-payments-1.6340526|access-date=2022-02-04|work=CBC News|date=2022-02-04|location=Toronto ON}} specifically, "because the protest violates its rules on violence and harassment." A convoy fundraiser on the fundraising website [[GiveSendGo]], launched after the GoFundMe removal, attracted over $8.2 million as of February 10.{{cite news|last1=Anglesey|first1=Anders|title=Freedom Convoy Raises $2M on GiveSendGo After GoFundMe Removes Campaign|url=https://www.newsweek.com/freedom-convoy-raises-2m-givesendgo-after-gofundme-removes-campaign-canada-truckers-1676591|access-date=2022-02-07|work=Newsweek|date=2022-02-06}}{{cite news|last1=Villegas|first1=Paulina|last2=Thebault|first2=Reis|date=February 7, 2022|title=Frozen out of GoFundMe, Canadian protest convoy raises millions on Christian site|newspaper=[[The Washington Post]]|location=Washington DC|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/07/canada-protesters-fundraising-platform/|url-status=live|url-access=limited|access-date=2022-02-07|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220207061025/https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/07/canada-protesters-fundraising-platform/|archive-date=2022-02-07}}{{cite news|last1=Taylor|first1=Stephanie|title=Ontario freezes funds from GiveSendGo trucker convoy fundraiser|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8610512/givesendgo-fundraiser-trucker-convoy-frozen/|access-date=2022-02-11|work=Global News|agency=The Canadian Press|date=2022-02-10|location=Toronto ON}} The [[Attorney General of Ontario]] sought and was granted an [[Ontario Superior Court of Justice]] court order under Section 490.8 of the [[Criminal Code of Canada]] against GiveSendGo, to [[Asset freezing|freeze the funds]] collected from two campaigns, "Freedom Convoy 2022"{{emdash}}US$8.4 million and "Adopt-a-Trucker"{{emdash}}over $686,000,{{cite news|last=Taylor|first=Stephanie|title=Ontario court freezes donations to GiveSendGo, halting millions collected for truckers' protests|work=Canada's National Observer|access-date=2022-02-13|date=2022-02-11|url=https://www.nationalobserver.com/2022/02/11/news/ontario-court-freezes-donations-givesendgo-halting-millions-collected-truckers}}{{cite news|last=Woodward|first=Jon|title=Anonymous donations to convoy as high as $215,000 concern Canadian MPs|work=CTV News|location=Toronto, Ontario|access-date=2022-02-13|date=2022-02-13|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/toronto/article/anonymous-donations-to-convoy-as-high-as-215000-concern-canadian-mps/|archive-date=February 14, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220214040208/https://toronto.ctvnews.ca/anonymous-donations-to-convoy-as-high-as-215-000-concern-canadian-mps-1.5777497|url-status=live}} and prohibit their distribution.{{cite news|title=Ontario court freezes access to funds raised for protest convoy on GiveSendGo platform|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/toronto/freedom-convoy-2022-donations-frozen-give-send-go-1.6347345|access-date=2022-02-11|work=CBC News|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-10|location=Toronto ON}} The court order binds "any and all parties with possession or control over these donations". The [[Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance]] (FINA) voted on February 10 to include a study of the "rise of ideologically motivated extremism". The FINA Committee invited GiveSendGo to testify. By February 19, at least 76 bank accounts linked to the protests totalling CA$3.2 million were frozen under the ''Emergencies Act''.{{cite news|title=Canada protests: Police push back demonstrators in Ottawa|work=BBC News|access-date=2022-02-19|date=2022-02-19|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-60420469}} Most accounts had been unfrozen by February 23. The RCMP stated that it has not shared donor lists with financial institutions, only the names of those who were "influencers in the illegal protest in Ottawa, and owners and/or drivers of vehicles who did not want to leave the area impacted by the protest."{{cite news|last1=Connolly|first1=Amanda|title=Canada must work on 'healing' from pandemic, convoy unrest, says Trudeau ahead of key vote|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8635451/ottawa-convoy-blockade-pandemic-divisions-justin-trudeau/|access-date=2022-02-21|work=Global News|date=2022-02-21|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite web|title=Statement on the freezing of financial accounts|url=https://blockade.rcmp.ca/news-nouvelles/ncr-rcn211130-s-d-en.html|website=Royal Canadian Mounted Police|publisher=Government of Canada|access-date=2022-02-21|location=Ottawa ON|date=2022-02-21}} In response to the efforts taken to block protestors from accessing funds via GoFundMe and other methods, reports surfaced of [[Bitcoin]] being used as an alternative source for fundraising.{{cite web|last1=O'Sullivan|first1=Andrea|title=Bitcoin Can Fix Financial Deplatforming of Canada's Truckers—But It Won't Be Easy|url=https://reason.com/2022/02/22/bitcoin-can-fix-financial-deplatforming-of-canadas-truckers-but-it-wont-be-easy/|website=reason.com|date=2022-02-22|publisher=Reason Magazine|access-date=2022-02-23}} Individual protestors have been reported as receiving bitcoin, sometimes as much as US$8,000 equivalent. Of the protestors and the new interest in bitcoin among average Canadians, the journalist stated, "...institutions can be directed to shut off financial access to enemies of the state. This has traditionally been 'rogue' nations and terrorist outfits, but Canada decided to expand this net to include the hundreds of thousands of normal Canadians who oppose government lockdowns and mandates." An Ontario Superior Court judge extended the freeze on funds until at least March 9, as of February 28.{{cite news|last1=Ki Sun Hwang|first1=Priscilla|title=Court extends rare order to freeze up to $20M in crypto, cash donations to 'Freedom Convoy'|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/mareva-injunction-order-extended-freedom-convoy-crypto-financial-donations-frozen-1.6366975|access-date=2022-02-28|work=CBC News Ottawa|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-28|location=Ottawa ON}} ====Donor list hacked==== On February 13, 2022, the GiveSendGo website was hacked and its data on donations was released to journalists and researchers through [[Distributed Denial of Secrets]].{{cite news|last=Faife|first=Corin|url=https://www.theverge.com/2022/2/14/22933772/givesendgo-funding-freedom-convoy-hacked-donor-leaked|title=Funding site linked to Canadian trucker protest hacked, donor info leaked online|work=[[The Verge]]|date=2022-02-14|access-date=2022-02-16}}{{Cite web|last=Thalen|first=Mikael|date=2022-02-15|title=Donation site used by Freedom Convoy suffers 3rd data leak in two weeks|url=https://www.dailydot.com/debug/givesendgo-trucker-convoy-hack-leak/|access-date=2022-03-05|website=[[The Daily Dot]]|language=en-US}} Four days later, it was revealed that the hacker who had claimed credit, [[Aubrey Cottle]], had been [[Death threat|threatened with murder]]. In the meantime, GiveSendGo had contacted the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation]] to see if he could be prosecuted.{{cite news|last1=Gilbert|first1=David|title=GiveSendGo Hacker Faces Death Threats for Leaking 'Freedom Convoy' Donor Info|url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/givesendgo-hacker-aubrey-cottle-freedom-convoy/|agency=Vice News|publisher=Vice Media Group|date=2022-02-17}} Of the 92,845 donations, 55.7 per cent of donors were from the United States, and 39 per cent from Canada. Many of the American donors' names correspond to the names of Donald Trump donors.{{cite news|author1=Elizabeth Thompson|author2=Roberto Rocha|author3=Albert Leung|date=2022-02-14|title=Hacked convoy data shows more than half of donations came from U.S.|work=CBC News|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|location=Toronto ON|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/convoy-protest-donations-data-1.6351292|access-date=2022-02-14}} The hack also revealed that one $90,000 donation allegedly came from American software billionaire [[Thomas Siebel]].{{cite web|title=Hackers Just Leaked the Names of 92,000 'Freedom Convoy' Donors|url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/freedom-convoy-givesendgo-donors-leaked/|access-date=2022-02-14|website=Vice News|date=2022-02-14}} Some members of the [[Ontario Provincial Police]] were revealed to have donated to the convoy on GiveSendGo, prompting the OPP to launch an internal conduct investigation.{{cite web|last=Jackson|first=Hannah|date=2022-02-22|title=OPP launches internal conduct investigation after members appear to have donated to convoy|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8638298/opp-launches-internal-conduct-investigation-freedom-convoy/|access-date=2022-02-24|website=Global News|language=en-US}} The American publication ''[[Politico]]'' reported that American right-wing commentators [[Dan Bongino]] and [[Ben Shapiro]] were among personalities directing support to the Canadian movement's crowdfunding websites.{{cite web|last=Scott|first=Mark|title=Ottawa truckers' convoy galvanizes far-right worldwide|work=Politico|access-date=2022-02-10|date=2022-02-06|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/06/ottawa-truckers-convoy-galvanizes-far-right-worldwide-00006080}} Based on research by the [[Institute for Strategic Dialogue]], from January 28 to February 5, a link to the original Freedom Convoy GoFundMe campaign was posted at least two dozen times on [[4chan]]. According to ''Politico'', white supremacist channels on [[Telegram (software)|Telegram]] also repeatedly shared the link. Once the ''Emergencies Act'' had been invoked, all crowdfunding platforms or payment providers hosting fundraising related to the convoy were required to register with the [[Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada]]. Banks were given the power to freeze accounts of those suspected to be involved in the blockades.{{cite news|last1=Shecter|first1=Barbara|title=Trudeau gives banks power to freeze funds without court order in bid to choke off protest funding|url=https://financialpost.com/fp-finance/banking/trudeau-gives-banks-power-to-freeze-funds-without-court-order-in-bid-to-choke-off-protest-funding|access-date=2022-02-14|work=Financial Post|publisher=Postmedia|date=2022-02-14|location=Toronto ON}} On March 3, GoFundMe executive Juan Benitez testified to the [[House of Commons of Canada|House of Commons]] that 86 per cent of the donors to the original GoFundMe campaign were Canadian, and 88 per cent of the donated funds to the original campaign were from Canadians. Benitez added that GoFundMe did not identify any donors to the campaign that were affiliated with terrorist or organized crime groups.{{cite web|last=Turnbull|first=Sarah|date=2022-03-03|title=GoFundMe head testifies over Freedom Convoy fundraising, says most donors were Canadian|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/article/gofundme-head-testifies-over-freedom-convoy-fundraising-says-most-donors-were-canadian/|access-date=2022-03-22|website=CTV News}} ===Vaccine mandates and passports=== During the [[COVID-19 pandemic]], vaccine mandates targeting federal public servants in the Core Public Administration were announced by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in October 2021.{{cite web|date=2021-10-06|title=Prime Minister announces mandatory vaccination for the federal workforce and federally regulated transportation sectors|url=http://www.pm.gc.ca/en/news/news-releases/2021/10/06/prime-minister-announces-mandatory-vaccination-federal-workforce-and|access-date=2023-10-10|website=Prime Minister of Canada|language=en}} Many government departments and organizations faced forced administrative leaves without pay if they failed to ensure employees were vaccinated by October 30, 2021. Many of the Provinces implemented their own vaccine mandates and passports at their own discretion, separate from the Federal mandates.{{cite web|date=2022-01-27|title=A look at Canada's vaccine mandates and public health restrictions|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/health/coronavirus/a-look-at-canada-s-vaccine-mandates-and-public-health-restrictions-1.5757218|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220127203933/https://www.ctvnews.ca/health/coronavirus/a-look-at-canada-s-vaccine-mandates-and-public-health-restrictions-1.5757218|url-status=dead|archive-date=January 27, 2022|access-date=2023-10-10|website=CTV News}} In a February 11 [[Bloomberg News]] interview with Canadian Labour Minister, [[Seamus O'Regan]], [[Amanda Lang]] said that "a lot of the mandates being protested here are [not federal but] provincial mandates".{{cite news|title=Trucker protests are causing companies to shut down: Canadian Manufacturers and Exporters CEO|work=Bloomberg News|url=https://www.bnnbloomberg.ca/video/taking-stock---federal-labour-minister-on-blockades-and-protests~2380729|date=2022-02-11|access-date=2022-02-12}} O'Regan clarified that "jurisdiction is clearly laid out in our Constitution" which describes the separation of powers at federal, provincial and territorial levels.{{cite report|url=https://lop.parl.ca/staticfiles/PublicWebsite/Home/ResearchPublications/InBriefs/PDF/2011-91-e.pdf|title=The Federal Role in Health and Health Care|last1=Butler|first1=Martha|last2=Tiedemann|first2=Marlisa|date=2011-09-22|pages=11 Publication No. 2011–91-E}} Under the ''[[Constitution Act, 1867|Constitution Act]]'', health is the responsibility of the provinces and territories.{{cite web|title=The Canada Health Act: An Overview|access-date=2021-01-08|url=https://lop.parl.ca/sites/PublicWebsite/default/en_CA/ResearchPublications/201954E?|first=Marlisa|last=Tiedemann|work=Library of Parliament|series=Research publications|number=2019–54–E|pages=18|date=2019-12-17}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Jvs1DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA128|title=Researching Quality in Care Transitions: International Perspectives|author1=Karina Aase|author2=Justin Waring|author3=Lene Schibevaag|publisher=Springer|year=2017|isbn=978-3-319-62346-7|pages=128–129}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=2_y6J647QFoC&pg=PA81|title=Government Relations in the Health Care Industry|author1=Peggy Leatt|author2=Joseph Mapa|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|year=2003|isbn=978-1-56720-513-8}}{{rp|81}} COVID-19-related responses, including those related to vaccinations are the responsibility of provinces and territories who provide free health services in their own jurisdictions.{{cite book |author=Monique Bégin |title=Medicare: Canada's Right to Health |publisher=Optimum Pub. International |year=1988 |isbn=978-0-88890-219-1 |page=Intro}} At the federal level, dialogues were undertaken with other [[Group of Seven]] nations, the [[World Health Organization]], and the [[International Civil Aviation Organization]] regarding passports{{emdash}}"reliable and secure" "proof of vaccination credentials".{{cite web|work=[[Transport Canada]]|title=Mandatory COVID-19 vaccination requirements for federally regulated transportation employees and travellers|access-date=2022-02-04|date=2021-10-06|url=https://www.canada.ca/en/transport-canada/news/2021/10/mandatory-covid-19-vaccination-requirements-for-federally-regulated-transportation-employees-and-travellers.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220129205800/https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/news/2021/08/canada-moving-forward-with-a-proof-of-vaccination-for-international-travel.html|archive-date=2022-01-29}} Regulations regarding international borders are reciprocal; they are enacted and enforced by both countries who share the border, involving federal agencies including [[Transport Canada]], and in the United States, Homeland Security and CDC. For example, some provincial and territorial governments imposed "tougher lockdowns" to attempt to slow the spread of Covid-19. ====Vaccination requirements for US–Canada cross-border travel==== {{main|COVID-19 vaccination mandates in Canada}} These new [[United States Department of Homeland Security]] regulations, released in October 2021, were regarding cross-border travel between the Canada and the United States and were based on guidance from the [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention]]. To prevent supply chain disruptions, the DHS allowed for a window of four months{{emdash}}until January 22, 2022{{emdash}}for Canadian truckers to get fully vaccinated against COVID-19.{{cite news|title=DHS to Require Non-U.S. Individual Travelers Entering the United States at Land Ports of Entry and Ferry Terminals to be Fully Vaccinated Against COVID-19|work=Homeland Security|url=https://www.dhs.gov/news/2022/01/20/dhs-require-non-us-individual-travelers-entering-united-states-land-ports-entry-and|access-date=2022-02-02}}{{cite web|title=DHS Releases Details for Fully Vaccinated, Non-Citizen Travelers to Enter the U.S. at Land and Ferry Border Crossings|url=https://www.dhs.gov/news/2021/10/29/dhs-releases-details-fully-vaccinated-non-citizen-travelers-enter-us-land-and-ferry|website=US Department of Homeland Security|publisher=US Gov|access-date=2022-01-26}} On November 19, 2021, the [[Public Health Agency of Canada]] announced upcoming adjustments to Canada's border measures.{{cite web|title=Government of Canada announces adjustments to Canada's border measures|url=https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/news/2021/11/government-of-canada-announces-adjustments-to-canadas-border-measures.html|website=Government of Canada|date=2021-11-19|publisher=Public Health Agency of Canada|access-date=2022-01-26}} This would include the requirement for essential service providers, including truck drivers, to be fully vaccinated after January 15, 2022. The announcement clarified that unvaccinated or partially vaccinated foreign national truck drivers would be prohibited from entering Canada after that date. Unvaccinated Canadian truck drivers could enter Canada but would have to quarantine for two weeks. According to the Canadian Press and CBC, as of January 22, the mandates would impact an estimated 26,000 unvaccinated truckers of the 160,000 truck drivers who regularly cross the border in both the United States and Canada. When asked in the [[House of Commons of Canada|House of Commons]] to produce data linking truckers to COVID-19 infections in Canada, neither the [[Minister of Health (Canada)|minister of health]] [[Jean-Yves Duclos]] nor the [[Chief Public Health Officer of Canada|chief public health officer]] [[Theresa Tam]] were able to do so.{{cite news|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8535295/truckers-vaccine-mandate-business-groups/|title=Business groups urge feds to reverse vaccine mandate for cross-border truckers|last1=Blanchfield|first1=Mike|last2=Taylor|first2=Stephanie|date=2022-01-24|website=[[Global News]]|access-date=2022-01-26}} An October 21, 2021, federal briefing said that the provinces and territories{{emdash}}who hold all of the vaccination information{{emdash}}are responsible for providing the vaccine passport that Canadians needed to travel internationally, using "existing provincial proof of vaccination systems".{{cite news|last=Aiello|first=Rachel|title=Vaccine passport: Provinces issuing proof for travel|work=CTV News|access-date=2022-02-02|date=2021-10-21|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/health/coronavirus/feds-say-provinces-will-issue-standardized-proof-of-vaccination-for-travel-1.5632390|archive-date=January 27, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220127174701/https://www.ctvnews.ca/health/coronavirus/feds-say-provinces-will-issue-standardized-proof-of-vaccination-for-travel-1.5632390|url-status=dead}} By 2022, all provinces and territories had "vaccine passports with the QR code that meets the recommended Canadian standard for domestic and international travel".{{cite web|work=Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada |title=How to get your Canadian COVID-19 proof of vaccination|access-date=2022-02-02|date=2021-10-21|url=https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/services/canadian-covid-19-proof-vaccination/get-proof.html}} There is no [[COVID-19 vaccination mandates in Canada|federal mandate for COVID-19 vaccines in Canada]]. With healthcare capacity spread "too thin" during the "rising fifth wave driven by the Omicron variant" in early January 2022, federal Health Minister [[Jean-Yves Duclos]]'s suggestion of mandatory COVID-19 vaccines was rejected by Alberta Premier [[Jason Kenney]], whose government had removed the power of mandatory vaccination from the province's Public Health Act, to ensure there could not be a vaccine mandate.{{cite web|last1=Amato|first1=Sean|last2=Edmonton|first2=CTV News|last3=Contact|first3=Follow|title=Alberta premier outright refuses federal suggestion of mandatory COVID-19 vaccines|work=CTV News Edmonton|access-date=2022-02-02|date=2022-01-07|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/edmonton/article/alberta-premier-outright-refuses-federal-suggestion-of-mandatory-covid-19-vaccines/}} Since October 29, proof of vaccination has been mandatory for employees of federal public services and federally-regulated industries including banking.{{cite news|last=Bensadoun|first=Emerald|title=Is mandatory COVID-19 vaccination coming to Canada? A look at each province and territory's restrictions and vaccine mandate policies|work=The Globe and Mail|access-date=2022-02-02|date=2022-01-09|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/canada/article-mandatory-vaccination-canada-province-territory-policies-guide/}} By early January, in these public sectors, those "without proof, or an exemption on medical or religious grounds, has been put on unpaid leave". Since the end of October, Canadians who wish to travel domestically on cruise ships or by VIA Rail trains have been required to prove they are fully vaccinated or have a negative COVID-19 test. The January 15 enforcement of the vaccination requirement applies to truckers who are entering Canada at international border crossings.{{cite news|last=Smith|first=John|title=Truck driver vaccine mandates loom for cross-border operations|date=2022-01-05|access-date=2022-02-12|url=https://www.trucknews.com/transportation/truck-driver-vaccine-mandates-loom-for-cross-border-operations/1003156144/|work=Truck News}} Since January 15, unvaccinated American cross-border truckers have been denied entry into Canada. Canadian truckers who are not fully vaccinated "have to show proof of a negative PCR test collected within 72 hours of arriving at the border"; they also "need to quarantine after arrival".{{cite news|last=Weisgarber|first=Maria|title=Vaccine mandate now in effect for cross-border truckers re-entering Canada|work=CTV News|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/vancouver/article/vaccine-mandate-now-in-effect-for-cross-border-truckers-re-entering-canada/|date=2022-01-15|access-date=2022-02-12}} Unvaccinated Canadian truckers can still work in Canada, unless their individual employer requires vaccination. As of January 5, the federal government had announced plans for a vaccine mandate for federally-regulated domestic fleets that cross provincial borders. At that time, no final date was set for those rules. A widely-shared January 12 miscommunication sent to the media from a [[Canada Border Services Agency]] spokesperson, that erroneously announced that the anticipated January 15 cross-border trucker vaccine mandate would not come into effect, was immediately retracted as an error.{{cite news|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/phac-truck-vaccine-mandate-communications-error-1.6322988|title=Public Health Agency of Canada involved in 'error' on trucker vaccine rules: CP sources|work=CBC News|date=2022-01-21|access-date=2022-02-14}} The "botched messaging" created "chaos and confusion."
