[[Tamara Lich]], the protest's fundraiser, is Secretary for the [[Maverick Party]], a Western Canadian separatist group formerly known as Wexit Canada.{{cite web|title=Tamara Lich|url=https://www.maverickparty.ca/tamara-lich|website=Maverick Party|access-date=2022-01-26|archive-date=2022-01-29|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220129081244/https://www.maverickparty.ca/tamara-lich|url-status=dead}} Lich was previously the regional co-ordinator for Wexit in southeastern Alberta and a board member for Wexit Alberta.{{cite web|last1=Climenhaga|first1=David|title=Who's Fuelling the Truckers Protesting Vaccine Mandates?|url=https://thetyee.ca/Analysis/2022/01/24/Truckers-Protesting-Vaccine-Mandates/|website=The Tyee|date=2022-01-24|access-date=2022-01-26}} The Maverick Party denied involvement with the convoy on January 24.{{cite web|title=Alberta-based Maverick Party denies involvement in fund raising for 'freedom convoy'|first=Dan|last=Ferguson|website=Red Deer Advocate|date=2022-01-25|url=https://www.reddeeradvocate.com/news/alberta-based-maverick-party-denies-involvement-in-fund-raising-for-freedom-convoy/|access-date=2022-01-26}} Action 4 Canada, which is associated with the Canada Unity group inside the Freedom Convoy, has been called an [[Islamophobia|Islamophobic]] and [[Anti-LGBT rhetoric|anti-LGBTQ]] hate and conspiracy group; it hosts webpages about the dangers of [[political Islam]], [[Misinformation related to 5G technology|health consequences of 5G technology]], and the alleged underreporting by the mainstream media of adverse reactions to the vaccine.{{cite news|last1=Reynolds|first1=Christopher|last2=Ibrahim|first2=Erika|title=Trucker convoy raises millions in funds as vaccine-hesitant supporters flock to cause|url=https://www.thestar.com/business/2022/01/24/trucker-convoy-raises-millions-in-funds-as-vaccine-hesitant-supporters-flock-to-cause.html|access-date=2022-01-26|work=The Toronto Star|agency=The Canada Press|date=2022-01-24|language=en-CA|publisher=Toronto Star Newspapers}} Action 4 Canada appears to have been founded by Tanya Gaw, who actively supported the Yellow Vests protests of 2019.{{cite web|last1=Smith|first1=Peter|last2=Simons|first2=Elizabeth|title=M-103 to the pandemic: evolution of Canadian Islamophobic activists shows how hate movements adapt|url=https://www.antihate.ca/m_103_pandemic_evolution_canadian_islamophobic_activists_shows_how_hate_movements_adapt|website=Antihate.ca|date=2021-10-05|access-date=2022-01-26}} Jason LaFace, Canada Unity's Ontario organizer and official representative to the Freedom Convoy, is also a main organizer for No More Lockdowns Canada, an anti-lockdown and anti-vaccine mandate organization primarily associated with expelled Ontario MPP [[Randy Hillier (politician)|Randy Hillier]]. No More Lockdowns Canada holds anti-lockdown rallies across Ontario.{{cite news|last1=Taylor|first1=Casey|title=Truck convoy's message muddies the closer it gets to capital|url=https://www.baytoday.ca/local-news/truck-convoys-message-muddies-the-closer-it-gets-to-capital-4994947|access-date=2022-01-27|work=baytoday.ca|publisher=Village Media|date=2022-01-26|archive-date=2022-02-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220222191955/https://www.baytoday.ca/local-news/truck-convoys-message-muddies-the-closer-it-gets-to-capital-4994947|url-status=dead}} Benjamin Dichter, who is listed as an organizer on the Freedom Convoy's GoFundMe page, was a speaker at the inaugural 2019 [[People's Party of Canada]] national convention, and has claimed [[political Islam]] has infiltrated the [[Conservative Party of Canada]] and is "rotting away at our society like syphilis".{{cite news|last1=Boutilier|first1=Alex|title=FEDERAL ELECTION Bernier tries to walk line between libertarianism and identity politics at People's Party's first national convention|url=https://www.thespec.com/news/federal-election/2019/08/19/bernier-tries-to-walk-line-between-libertarianism-and-identity-politics-at-people-s-party-s-first-national-convention.html|access-date=2022-01-27|work=Hamilton Spectator|publisher=Metroland Media Group|date=2019-08-19}} Convoy leader Dave Steenburg has posted material by [[Soldiers of Odin]], a [[Neo-Nazism|neo-Nazi]] vigilante group, on his Facebook page; Jason LaFace, an administrator of the Convoy Facebook page, has done the same thing. LaFace has also posted a photo of himself wearing a Soldiers of Odin jacket.{{cite web|last=Gilmore|first=Rachel|title=Some trucker convoy organizers have history of white nationalism, racism|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8543281/covid-trucker-convoy-organizers-hate/|access-date=2022-02-02|website=Global News|quote='One of the admins on their website is actually somebody who's like the vice president of the Soldiers of Odin, a skinhead group in Sudbury, Ont.,' said Dr. Carmen Celestini, a post-doctoral fellow with the Disinformation Project at Simon Fraser University. 'His name is Jason LaFace. He also uses other names, but he is a vice president of this group, which organize events that will try to stop immigration, people who are BIPOC or people who are in LGBTQ communities.'}}{{cite news|title='Freedom convoy' rolls through Ottawa encouraging the participation of Canada's far-right|url=https://theconversation.com/freedom-convoy-rolls-through-ottawa-encouraging-the-participation-of-canadas-far-right-175902|work=[[The Conversation (website)|The Conversation]]|date=2022-02-03|quote="Steenburg made headlines when he posted a Soldiers of Odin logo (a known far-right hate group) on his social media page with captions encouraging Canadians to stand up for their rights."}} Following the seizure of weapons, ammunition and body armour at the blockade in Coutts, one piece of body armour was identified to have patches signifying relation to [[accelerationist]] right-wing [[militia]] [[Diagolon]].{{cite web|date=2022-02-15|title=Analysis of guns and ammunition seized near Coutts, Alta. blockade|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/calgary/article/analysis-of-guns-and-ammunition-seized-near-coutts-alta-blockade/|access-date=2022-02-16|website=CTV News Calgary}} ===Counter-protests=== Counter-protests in favour of public health measures were held throughout the convoy's duration. In [[Ottawa]], with protestors in the parliament area spilling into residential neighbourhoods nearby, lawsuits and injunction requests were filed by residents to silence the trucks that were honking their horns at all hours.{{cite web|date=2022-02-09|title=Meet the 21-year-old Ottawa woman behind the injunction that silenced the honking|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/zexi-li-ottawa-injunction-trucker-protest-convoy-1.6344503|access-date=2023-02-09|website=CBC News}} [[Counter-protest]]s were held in several cities on February 5. Counter-protestors forced a convoy of vehicles to split-up in [[Vancouver]] after blocking the roadway by standing on foot or with a bicycle on Terminal Avenue. There were few side streets or exit options after the trucks travelled more than 2 kilometres down the roadway, which required the trucks to reverse the length of Terminal Avenue in order to exit the street.{{cite web|last=Holliday|first=Ian|date=2022-02-05|title=Counter-protesters say 'Go Home' as Vancouver convoy disrupted|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/vancouver/article/counter-protesters-say-go-home-as-vancouver-convoy-disrupted/|access-date=2023-02-09|website=CTV News Vancouver}}{{cite web|title=Five arrested as anti-COVID-19 mandate convoys, rallies staged in British Columbia |url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8597602/anti-covid-19-mandate-convoys-rallies-b-c/|access-date=2023-02-09|website=Global News|language=en-US}} Counter-protestors in [[Victoria, British Columbia|Victoria]] spread out by the [[British Columbia Parliament Buildings|BC Legislature]], to be near and far weekend convoy protestors.{{cite web|title=Trucker convoy, vaccine supporters face off at legislature protest|url=https://www.timescolonist.com/local-news/truckers-and-counter-protesters-both-show-up-at-legislature-protest-5032968|access-date=2023-02-09|website=Victoria Times Colonist|date=2022-02-06}} Some 200 people gathered in front of Ottawa City Hall to demand the end of disruptions caused by trucks downtown. People held pro-vaccination signs in [[Whitehorse]] while anti-mandate protestors passed them in vehicles.{{cite news|date=2022-02-05|title=5 arrested in Vancouver as rocks thrown, cars kicked during pandemic rallies, police say|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/british-columbia/bc-vaccine-protests-feb-5-1.6341060|access-date=2022-02-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220213201636/https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/british-columbia/bc-vaccine-protests-feb-5-1.6341060|archive-date=2022-02-13}}{{cite news|last=de Flaviis|first=Sissi|date=2022-02-07|title=Another weekend of COVID-19 protests sparks counter-protest in Whitehorse|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/north/whitehorse-trucker-protest-feb-5-1.6341894|access-date=2022-02-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220211153602/https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/north/whitehorse-trucker-protest-feb-5-1.6341894|archive-date=2022-02-11}}{{cite news|author1=Andrew Duffy|author2=Elizabeth Payne|title=Counter protest to take to the streets this weekend|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/local-news/counter-protest-to-take-to-the-streets-this-weekend|access-date=2022-02-04|work=Ottawa Citizen|date=2022-02-04|location=Ottawa ON}}{{cite news|last1=Kestler-D'Amours|first1=Jillian|title=Ottawa residents decry anti-vaccine trucker 'occupation'|url=https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2022/2/4/ottawa-residents-decry-anti-vaccine-trucker-occupation|access-date=2022-02-04|work=Al Jazeera|date=2022-02-04}}{{cite news|last=Porter|first=Kate|title=Avoid counter-protests this weekend, urge seasoned activists and police|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/avoid-counter-protest-ottawa-second-weekend-safety-1.6340103|access-date=2022-02-06|work=CBC News Ottawa|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-05|location=Ottawa ON}}{{cite news|last=Farooqui|first=Salmaan|date=2022-02-05|title=Trucker convoy demonstrations spread across Canada as counter-protests call for an end to disruptions|work=The Globe and Mail|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/canada/article-trucker-convoy-demonstrators-met-with-counter-protesters-calling-for/|access-date=2022-02-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220213115628/https://www.theglobeandmail.com/canada/article-trucker-convoy-demonstrators-met-with-counter-protesters-calling-for/|archive-date=2022-02-13}} A counter-protest organized by health care workers was planned in Toronto for February 12, but was called off when the provincial government instituted a state of emergency.{{cite news|last=Lavoie|first=Joanna|date=2022-02-11|title=Toronto health-care workers organize – then call off – second Freedom Convoy counterprotest|work=Toronto Star|url=https://www.thestar.com/local-toronto-city-centre/news/2022/02/11/toronto-health-care-workers-organize-then-call-off-second-freedom-convoy-counterprotest.html|access-date=2022-02-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220212003139/https://www.thestar.com/local-toronto-city-centre/news/2022/02/11/toronto-health-care-workers-organize-then-call-off-second-freedom-convoy-counterprotest.html|archive-date=2022-02-12}} In counter-protests on February 12, thousands of people joined the "Community Solidarity March" through Ottawa to demand the end of the protests.{{cite news|last=Pringle|first=Josh|title=Ottawa mayor says truckers have agreed to leave residential neighbourhoods|work=CTV News|location=Ottawa, ON|access-date=2022-02-13|date=2022-02-13|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/ottawa/article/ottawa-mayor-says-truckers-have-agreed-to-leave-residential-neighbourhoods/}}{{cite news|title=Counter-protest in Ottawa|work=CTV News|location=Ottawa, ON|access-date=2022-02-12|date=2022-02-12|url=https://ottawa.ctvnews.ca/video?clipId=2380858|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220213001433/https://ottawa.ctvnews.ca/video?clipId=2380858|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 13, 2022}}{{cite web|date=2022-02-13|title=Counter-protests grow, block convoys on 3rd weekend of downtown demonstrations|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/covid-19-counter-protest-trucker-protest-1.6349936|access-date=2023-02-10|website=CBC News}} On February 13, a group of Ottawa residents blocked the intersection of Riverside Drive and Bank Street to prevent a newly-arrived contingent of vehicles from joining the main protest.{{cite web|last=Duffy|first=Andrew|title=Counter-protesters blockade 'Freedom Convoy' on Riverside Drive|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/local-news/counter-protesters-blockade-freedom-convoy-on-riverside-drive|access-date=2023-02-10|website=Ottawa Citizen|language=en-CA}} The original group of 20 grew to more than 1,000 people and successfully [[kettled]] the eastern artery of the "Blue Collar Convoy" for eight hours and were joined by groups in other neighbourhoods. The convoy vehicles left one at a time under a heavy police escort, after the vehicle owners took down their signs and flags.{{cite news|last1=Off|first1=Carol|last2=Howden|first2=Chris|date=2022-02-15|title=As it Happens February 14, 2022 Episode Transcript|work=CBC|url=https://www.cbc.ca/radio/asithappens/as-it-happens-the-monday-edition-1.6350961/february-14-2022-episode-transcript-1.6353028|access-date=2022-02-16|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220216111655/https://www.cbc.ca/radio/asithappens/as-it-happens-the-monday-edition-1.6350961/february-14-2022-episode-transcript-1.6353028|archive-date=2022-02-16}}{{cite news|date=2022-02-14|title=Trucker convoy: Counter-protesters block convoy traffic; Convoy organizers plan to 'consolidate' efforts around Parliament Hill; Arrests in Windsor as Ambassador Bridge cleared|work=Ottawa Citizen|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/trucker-convoy-protest-enters-day-17-in-ottawa-arrests-in-windsor|access-date=2022-02-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220214072003/https://ottawacitizen.com/news/trucker-convoy-protest-enters-day-17-in-ottawa-arrests-in-windsor|archive-date=2022-02-14}}{{cite news|date=2022-02-13|title=Counter-protests grow, block convoys on 3rd weekend of downtown demonstrations|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/covid-19-counter-protest-trucker-protest-1.6349936|access-date=2022-02-14}} The standoff between Ottawa residents and the kettled convoy participants was colloquially called the 'Battle of Billings Bridge' and was commemorated a year later with an unofficial plaque installed in the location of the standoff.{{cite web|last=Gillis|first=Megan|date=2023-02-09|title=Unofficial plaque marks historic site of 'Battle of Billings Bridge,' almost one year later|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/local-news/unofficial-plaque-marks-historic-site-of-battle-of-billings-bridge-almost-one-year-later|access-date=2023-02-09|website=Ottawa Citizen|language=en-CA}}{{cite web|last=Woods|first=Michael|date=2023-02-09|title=Plaque commemorates 'Battle of Billings Bridge' during 'Freedom Convoy'|url=https://ottawa.ctvnews.ca/plaque-commemorates-battle-of-billings-bridge-during-freedom-convoy-1.6266844|archive-url=https://archive.today/20230209172627/https://ottawa.ctvnews.ca/plaque-commemorates-battle-of-billings-bridge-during-freedom-convoy-1.6266844|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 9, 2023|access-date=2023-02-09|website=CTV News Ottawa}} During the convoy occupation of Ottawa, various individuals gained public note for their counter-protesting.{{cite news|title=These Ottawans became internet famous countering convoy protesters|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/counter-protesters-who-are-they-ottawa-freedom-convoy-occupation-1.6364155|access-date=2022-02-28|work=CBC News Ottawa|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-27|location=Ottawa ON}} The protestors' [[Zello]] [[push-to-talk]]-style voice channels were the subject of coordinated disruption from counter-protestors, including users playing the [[Homoeroticism|homoerotic]] [[country rock]] song "[[Ram Ranch]]" to "[[Internet troll|troll]]" participants and listeners (an effort coordinated under the [[hashtag]] "#RamRanchResistance").{{cite magazine|last=Dickson|first=E. J.|date=2022-02-10|title=A Porno-Metal Song About Gay Cowboys Is Disrupting the Anti-Vax Trucker Convoy|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/culture-news/anti-vax-trucker-convoy-porno-metal-ram-ranch-1297926/|access-date=2022-02-12|magazine=Rolling Stone|language=en-US}}{{cite web|last=Maher|first=Stephen|date=2022-01-26|title=The grassroots action against Erin O'Toole's leadership has begun|url=https://www.macleans.ca/politics/the-grassroots-action-against-erin-otooles-leadership-has-begun/|access-date=2022-01-28|website=Macleans}}{{cite news|last=Grant|first=Demar|date=2022-02-11|title=What is Zello? Inside the app that helped organize "freedom convoy" blockades|language=en-CA|work=The Toronto Star|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/canada/2022/02/11/what-is-zello-inside-the-app-that-helped-organize-freedom-convoy-blockades.html|access-date=2022-02-12|issn=0319-0781}} === Ottawa Police Service response === Ottawa Police Service faced criticism for how it responded to the convoy during the first three weeks, culminating with the resignation of Chief Peter Sloly. At a January 31 press conference, [[Ottawa Police Service]] (OPS) Chief [[Peter Sloly]] described the demonstration as "unique in nature, massive in scale, polarizing in context and dangerous in literally every other aspect of the event itself".{{cite web|title=Chief Peter Sloly remarks during Media availability|work=Ottawa Police Service|access-date=2022-02-02|date=2022-01-31|url=https://www.ottawapolice.ca//Modules/News/index.aspx?newsId=4e505c6b-0e2e-4a69-a9f0-cfccdcb3e542&lang=en|archive-date=2022-02-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220202214635/https://www.ottawapolice.ca//Modules/News/index.aspx?newsId=4e505c6b-0e2e-4a69-a9f0-cfccdcb3e542&lang=en|url-status=dead}} He said that starting on January 28, there was a "traffic gridlock" which turned into "traffic chaos" over the next two days, as "thousands of vehicles, particularly heavy trucks" arrived in Ottawa. Security forces included police forces from the federal, provincial and municipal levels: the [[RCMP]], [[Ontario Provincial Police]] (OPP), [[Gatineau Police]], [[Sûreté du Québec]], [[London Police Service]], [[Hamilton Police Service]], [[Peel Regional Police]], [[Toronto Police Service]], [[York Regional Police]], and [[Durham Regional Police]] who worked with the OPS Incident Command System. OPS Chief Sloly advised people to avoid downtown Ottawa during the weekend protest, adding that "we are prepared to investigate, arrest if necessary, charge and prosecute anyone who acts violently or breaks the law in the demonstrations, or in association with the demonstrations". Chief Sloly said that since the scaling down of the demonstration on January 31, the crime prevention teams normally providing support in around the core, but who were deployed elsewhere during the height of the protests, have returned to their neighbourhoods. By January 31, with the cost of police service per day estimated at CA$800,000 a day, the Mayor of Ottawa was considering legal action to cover costs.{{cite news|title=City of Ottawa considering legal action to recoup protest costs|work=CBC|access-date=2022-02-01|date=2022-01-31|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/ottawa-mayor-convoy-protest-1.6335645}} [[Patrick McDonell]], the [[List of Sergeants-at-Arms of the Canadian House of Commons|Sergeant-at-Arms of the House of Commons]] and Director General of [[Parliamentary Protective Service]] (PPS) warned members of parliament about possible [[doxing]] attempts to discover the addresses of their residences in the [[National Capital Region (Canada)|Capital Region]]. The letter further warned MPs not to get involved in any demonstrations, to "go somewhere safe", and to keep all doors locked.{{cite news|title=Letter sent to parliamentarians warning of doxing ahead of trucker convoy: 'Go somewhere safe'|first=Lucas|last=Casaletto|date=2022-01-27|url=https://toronto.citynews.ca/2022/01/27/ottawa-freedom-convoy-security/|access-date=2022-01-28}}{{cite news|last=Aiello|first=Rachel|title=MPs warned about security risks related to convoy, O'Toole plans to meet truckers|work=CTV News|access-date=2022-02-01|date=2022-01-27|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/mps-warned-about-security-risks-related-to-convoy-o-toole-plans-to-meet-truckers-1.5757134|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220128003901/https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/mps-warned-about-security-risks-related-to-convoy-o-toole-plans-to-meet-truckers-1.5757134|url-status=dead|archive-date=January 28, 2022}} McDonell told reporters that Canada has never been so divided—the thousands of people arriving on Parliament Hill in the trucker convoy represented a "symbol of the fatigue" that Canadians were experiencing after two years of COVID. PPS prepared for approximately 10,000 protesters; Wellington Street, which is in front of Parliament Hill was closed to most traffic; some lanes had been "designated specifically" for truckers; and others for emergency vehicles. On January 29, the first day of protest at [[Parliament Hill]], Prime Minister [[Justin Trudeau]] was "moved to an undisclosed location due to security concerns".{{cite news|last=Tasker|first=John Paul|title=Thousands opposed to COVID-19 rules converge on Parliament Hill|work=CBC|access-date=2022-02-01|date=2022-01-29|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/truck-convoy-protest-some-key-players-1.6332312}}{{cite news|date=2022-01-29|title=Freedom Convoy 2022 live updates: Trudeau relocated due to security concerns|work=National Post|url=https://nationalpost.com/news/politics/covid-19-freedom-convoy-2022-news|access-date=2022-01-29}} The OPS had called in reinforcements from the RCMP and the [[Canadian Security Intelligence Service]] (CSIS){{emdash}}Canada's main intelligence service{{emdash}}and were working to identify threats in the convoy. Intelligence reports by the Integrated Terrorism Assessment Centre (ITAC), which is part of CSIS, said that some convoy supporters "advocated civil war", had "called for violence against prime minister Justin Trudeau", and said the protest should be "used as Canada's 'January 6'", referencing the [[January 6 United States Capitol attack]].{{cite news|issn=0261-3077|last=Ling|first=Justin|title=Canada was warned before protests that violent extremists infiltrated convoy|work=The Guardian|access-date=2022-02-17|date=2022-02-17|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/feb/17/ottawa-protests-anti-terror-agency-warned-violent-extremists}} The ITAC reports, which were seen by ''[[The Guardian]]'', warned in late January that "extremists" were "likely involved" and the "potential for violence remained real". The February 8 ITAC report raised concerns that the "online rhetoric" was "violent", and that there were "ideological extremists" who were "physically present" at "some gatherings". As protesters headed to Ottawa on January 28, the OPP tweeted "OPP advises motorists to avoid travel on [[Ontario Highway 417|Highway 417]] and [[Ontario Highway 416|Highway 416]] in the Ottawa area, beginning Friday afternoon and on Saturday."{{cite web|last1=Woods|first1=Michael|last2=Pringle|first2=Josh|date=2022-01-28|title=Heavy police presence as truckers arrive in downtown Ottawa|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/ottawa/article/heavy-police-presence-as-truckers-arrive-in-downtown-ottawa/|access-date=2022-01-28|website=CTV News Ottawa}} They asked that emergency vehicle access be assured throughout. An article in ''[[The New York Times]]'' described the Ottawa Police Service as "vastly outnumbered", and "unprepared for the sophisticated tactics, discipline and logistical abilities of the protesters".{{cite news|last=Porter|first=Catherine|title=In Ottawa Trucker Protests, a Pressing Question: Where Were the Police?|work=The New York Times|access-date=2022-02-12|date=2022-02-12|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/02/12/world/americas/canada-truck-protests-police.html}} The Ottawa Police Service has 1,500 officers to serve the million residents of the city of Ottawa. During the three weeks of the protest and occupation, only 150 officers were on the streets in "three of the most affected neighbourhoods over the course of a day". Chief Sloly requested an additional 1,800 early on, but by February 13, they had not arrived. Police experts said that Sloly, who has been criticized for his response to the protests, had a legitimate problem of "lack of resources...given the unprecedented situation facing Ottawa."{{cite news|last=Carbert|first=Michelle|title=For Ottawa's Chief Peter Sloly, police response to protests pits his progressive views against a complicated threat|work=The Globe and Mail|access-date=2022-02-13|date=2022-02-12|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/politics/article-for-ottawas-chief-peter-sloly-police-response-to-protests-pits-his}} Some officers were "swarmed" while attempting to make an arrest. On the first weekend, crowds were estimated at 8,000 to 18,000. On weekdays, the number of protesters somewhat declined. On the second weekend, with 500 heavy trucks and 7,000 demonstrators in the downtown core "red zone" as of Saturday evening, Sloly reported to an emergency meeting Ottawa Police Services board meeting, that even with all OPS officers on active duty and the hundreds of law-enforcement officials who came to help, he did not have the resources to end what he called the "turbulent protests".{{cite news|last=Chianello|first=Joanne|title=No end in sight to Ottawa protests, not enough resources, says police chief|work=CBC News|date=2022-02-05|access-date=2022-02-14|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/ottawa-protests-police-chief-no-end-in-sight-1.6341192}} On the third weekend of February 11 and 12, there were about 4,000 or 5,000 protesters and partyers in the city's core, on Parliament Hill. Even after a state of emergency was declared by the mayor, officers were outnumbered and unable to enforce injunctions or regulations. According to the ''Times'', many analysts have cautioned that some convoy's leaders with military or policing backgrounds helped demonstrators strategize and plan under the guidance of people like Tom Quiggin, "proclaimed head of protective intelligence", who had formerly served as an "intelligence officer for the Canadian military, cabinet office and federal police." and has been described as "one of the country's top counter-terrorism experts." By February 10, Chief Sloly said 250 RCMP officers were sworn in and deployed to reinforce the 1,500 OPS officers. The response of local police forces to these blockades has erred on the side of caution, with police at each site initially trying to negotiate with the protesters.{{cite web|last=Gervais|first=Brittany|date=2022-02-01|title=Negotiation usually comes first when dealing with large protests, criminal law experts explain|url=https://calgaryherald.com/news/local-news/negotiation-usually-comes-first-when-dealing-with-large-protests-criminal-law-experts-explain|access-date=2022-02-18|website=Calgary Herald|language=en-CA}} === Integrated Command Centre response=== Plans for the creation of the new Integrated Command Centre were in place by February 12. The Integrated Command Centre was created to help Ottawa Police Services (OPS) coordinate with Ontario Provincial Police (OPP) and RCMP when the anticipated reinforcements would arrive.{{cite news|last1=Pringle|first1=Josh|last2=Raymond|first2=Ted|title=Trucker protest: Ottawa police form new command centre with RCMP and OPP|work=CTV News|location=Ottawa, ON|access-date=2022-02-13|date=2022-02-12|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/ottawa/article/ottawa-police-form-new-command-centre-with-rcmp-opp-to-respond-to-downtown-protest/}} As an estimated 4,000 protesters converged on Parliament Hill on February 12, the extra reinforcements requested by Chief Sloly a week earlier had not yet arrived. Emergency Preparedness Minister [[Bill Blair (politician)|Bill Blair]] said on February 13 that they are in constant contact with provincial and municipal authorities. Because the situation is "critical" for Canada with the "closing of our borders, the targeting on critical infrastructure, particularly our points of entry by the people behind these protests", it poses a "significant national security threat" to Canada, and we have to "end it".{{cite news|last=Aiello|first=Rachel|title=Feds prepared to use emergency powers to end blockades, but police must 'do their job': Blair|work=CTV News|access-date=2022-02-13|date=2022-02-13|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/article/feds-prepared-to-use-emergency-powers-to-end-blockades-but-police-must-do-their-job-blair/|location=Ottawa, ON}} On Day 19 of the protests, with Ottawa under three states of emergency invoked by the three levels of government, Chief Sloly resigned.{{cite news|last1=Kirkup|first1=Kristy|first2=Marieke|last2=Walsh|first3=Michelle|last3=Carbert|date=2022-02-15|access-date=2022-02-15|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/politics/article-ottawa-police-chief-peter-sloly-resigns-amid-criticism-of-how-vaccine/|title=Ottawa Police Chief Peter Sloly resigns amid criticism of how vaccine-mandate protests were handled}} At the February 15 Ottawa Police Board meeting, Deputy Chief Steve Bell, who was appointed Interim Chief of Police, told the board that the OPS force had not "yet received the 1,800 extra officers requested". At the height of the protests, there were about 8,000 protesters. Overnight, on February 14, there were "less than 150 people in the blockade" and approximately 360 vehicles still on the streets, in contrast to the estimated 4,000 vehicles at the end of January.{{cite news|last=Aiello|first=Rachel|title=Emergencies Act: Feds outline next steps|work=CTV News|access-date=2022-02-16|date=2022-02-15|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/article/bringing-minors-to-protests-supplying-fuel-can-be-prohibited-under-the-emergencies-act/}} The OPS had made a total of "33 arrests and charged 18 people" in the 19 day period. [[Canadian Police Association]] president, Tom Stamatakis, cautioned that with Sloly's resignation, politicians "should not 'scapegoat' him for other failures that have allowed this occupation to drag on." On February 18, Day 22, hundreds{{cite news|issn=0362-4331|last1=Bilefsky|first1=Dan|last2=Nir|first2=Sarah Maslin|last3=Kitroeff|first3=Natalie|last4=Austen|first4=Ian|title=Canada Live Updates: Police Clamp Down on Ottawa Protesters|work=The New York Times|access-date=2022-02-18|date=2022-02-18|url=https://www.nytimes.com/live/2022/02/18/world/canada-ottawa-protests-news}} of OPS, York, Toronto, Gatineau, Durham Region, London, Hamilton forces, the [[Sûreté du Québec|Sureté du Québec]], the OPP, and the RCMP, worked on a coordinated police action in and around Parliament Hill. By late February 18 morning, CTV News and other mainstream media reporting in the red zone, showed police steadily, very slowly sandwiching groups of dozens of the remaining protesters on all sides on separate streets. There were reports of a flank of police on horseback behind those in the front lines.{{cite news|date=2022-02-19|title=A timelapse of police efforts to clear convoy protesters on Friday|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/player/play/2004738627607|access-date=2023-02-10}} Families with children were seen in these final groups of protesters, with police reporting that protesters were "putting children between their operations and the protest site." The police assured that "children will be brought to a place of safety." In a CBC interview, former OPS chief [[Charles Bordeleau]] said he had never seen children used before in other protests. He said that the CAS did not have to step in. [[Pat King (activist)|Pat King]] was arrested near noon. King is described as the final of three major online influencers continuing the protest to be arrested. He was later charged of mischief, counselling to commit the offence of mischief, counselling to commit the offence of disobey court order (s.127), and counselling to commit the offence of obstruct police.{{cite web|title=Charges laid in relation to Unlawful Protests in Ottawa|url=https://www.ottawapolice.ca/Modules/News/index.aspx?lang=en&newsId=a37ddc2f-6cdc-4107-a4fa-faa57d60847e|publisher=Ottawa Police Service|access-date=2022-02-19|location=Ottawa ON|date=2022-02-19|archive-date=2022-02-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220219131631/https://www.ottawapolice.ca/Modules/News/index.aspx?lang=en&newsId=a37ddc2f-6cdc-4107-a4fa-faa57d60847e|url-status=dead}} By early afternoon, tactical officers began slowly entering the cabins of large rigs that had been parked in front of Parliament since the end of January. Two large campers placed by protesters to prevent large trucks blocking Sussex from moving were finally cleared early in the afternoon, with the arrest of a protester in the motor home by five officers wearing full security gear. Police allowed mainstream media close access to the frontlines of the tactical method.{{cite news|title=Convoy leader arrested as police and protesters clash at downtown Ottawa occupation|work=CBC|location=Ottawa, ON|access-date=2022-02-18|date=2022-02-18|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/police-action-convoy-protest-1.6356444}} In the afternoon, as some of the remaining protesters accepted the inevitable, they began to clear {{convert|30|cm|ft|abbr=on}} of snow from their trucks and trailers in preparation for their departure. Frustrated protesters interviewed by CBC and CTV who were earlier calling "Hold the line", asked for their "voice to be heard". The last few protesters said, without evidence, that the protest was legal, citing, but misunderstanding Canadian law. CBC News on the front lines reported protesters in the red zone, who could leave, chose to stay and be arrested. There were 70 arrests and 21 vehicles were removed by mid-afternoon. CBC's senior defence writer, Murray Brewster, described the police operation as "professional, methodical, and tactical". Police did not prevent truckers or individual protesters from driving or walking away for most of the day. They may face charges later. Police used a minimal amount of force, even late in the afternoon, as members of different forces began "squeezing" the group of protesters "even tighter" on Wellington Street in front of Parliament. They were not wearing shields or using pepper spray or tear gas, as they wanted protesters to leave, as opposed to arresting them, according to Bordeleau. As waiting long lines tow trucks, brought in by the police, replaced the heavy rigs that had been grid-locking Ottawa downtown, some of the tow trucks' company names were hidden. CBC reported that tow truck companies who had previously refused to tow protesters' vehicles, have been forced through the ''Emergencies Act'' to comply with orders. Bordeleau also said that the Emergencies Act had allowed police to set up 100 checkpoints around the city of Ottawa to prevent protester back-filling and to prevent pop-up protests. In response to those who contrasted the February 18 coordinated successful response to the OPS inaction in previous weeks, [[Carol Anne Meehan]], an Ottawa City Councillor, said that the OPS had been severely under-resourced until the ''Emergencies Act'' was invoked. On February 19, police began with a "hard, fast push" towards to the encampment at Parliament Hill. Due to the resistance of protesters, police wore helmets and carried batons for protection.{{cite news|last1=Aziz|first1=Saba|first2=Amanda|last2=Connolly|title=Ottawa police say convoy blockade 'aggressive;' some arrested with body armour, smoke grenades|work=Global News|access-date=2022-02-19|date=2022-02-19|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8632832/ottawa-police-freedom-convoy-blockade/}} At one point, a smoke device was released by protesters. By early afternoon, more than 140 people had been arrested and dozens of vehicles had been seized. During the question period of the OPS press conference, Chief Bell confirmed that security forces have been gathering intelligence using videos, and that they will be actively pursuing people who have been filmed. The police will actively follow up with identifications, collect evidence, seek criminal charges with sanctions that can include the financial level and licensing level.{{cite news|last1=Tunney|first1=Catharine|last2=Paas-Lang|first2=Christian|title=Ottawa police now using a chemical irritant as they push back protest crowd|work=CBC|access-date=2022-02-19|date=2022-02-19|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/ottawa-convoy-protest-fourth-weekend-1.6358094}}{{cite AV media|people=Ottawa Police Chief Bell|title=Media Availability|date=2022-02-19|series=Media availability|access-date=2022-02-19|time=32:00|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tJe9gbDiWvY}} ==== Clearance ==== As of February 10, there was general agreement by government and police that vigorous efforts to clear the blockades were dangerous, possibly leading to expanded or violent reactions. A variety of options were legally possible and some, such as issuing tickets, were employed. Tow-truck drivers in several locations declined to tow trucks.{{cite news|author1=Amy Cheng|author2=Jennifer Hassan|title='Freedom Convoy' protesters who block streets could be 'arrested without a warrant,' Ottawa police warn|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/10/canada-freedom-convoy-alberta-ottawa-protests/|access-date=2022-02-10|newspaper=The Washington Post|date=2022-02-10}}{{cite news|author1=Amanda Coletta|author2=Amy Cheng|author3=Jennifer Hassan|title=Canada's capital is jammed, its border crossings are blockaded, and there's no end in sight|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/09/canada-freedom-convoy-alberta-ambassador-bridge/|access-date=2022-02-10|newspaper=The Washington Post|date=2022-02-09}} Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, on February 3, declared his reluctance to employ the military to clear the protesters, saying he had received no formal request for military assistance and added, "One has to be very, very cautious before deploying military in situations engaging Canadians," saying it's not something to "enter into lightly". On February 11, the court injunction to clear protesters from the Ambassador Bridge, secured by the City of Windsor, went into effect at 7 pm.{{cite news|last=Tumilty|first=Ryan|title=The consequences are becoming more and more severe,' Freedom convoy warned|url=|date=2022-02-11|work=National Post}}{{failed verification|date=February 2024}} Prime Minister Trudeau and United States President [[Joe Biden]] met virtually to discuss the dissolution of cross-border blockades.{{cite news|last=Blatchford|first=Andy|title=Biden, Trudeau talk convoys as U.S. braces for Canadian-style protests|date=2022-02-11|access-date=2022-02-14|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/11/biden-trudeau-convoy-protest-00008358}} Ontario Premier [[Doug Ford]] declared a [[States of emergency in Canada|state of emergency]] in the province. Heavy fines of up to $100,000 and up to a year of imprisonment were introduced for offences such as blocking or impeding the movement of goods, people, and services along critical roads and bridges in the province, including "international border crossings, [[400-series highways]], airports, ports, bridges and railways". Penalties include revoking personal and commercial drivers' licences.{{cite web|last1=Jones|first1=Allison|last2=Alhmidi|first2=Maan|date=2022-02-11|title=Ontario premier declares state of emergency in response to protests|url=https://www.thestar.com/politics/provincial/2022/02/11/ford-declares-state-of-emergency-in-ontario-over-truck-blockades.html|access-date=2022-02-12|website=[[The Toronto Star]]}}{{cite web|last=Cecco|first=Leyland|date=2022-02-11|title=Ontario declares state of emergency, threatening fines and jail time to end blockade|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/feb/11/ontario-state-emergency-threatens-fines-jail-time-end-blockade|access-date=2022-02-11|website=[[The Guardian]]}} On February 17, police massed at a staging area in Ottawa. A {{convert|12|ft|m}}-high fence was constructed around the Parliament building and over 100 checkpoints limited access to the protest area.{{cite news|author1=Miriam Berger|author2=Amanda Coletta|author3=Maite Fernández Simon|author4=Ellen Francis|date=2022-02-17|title=Ottawa police presence grows as interim chief vows 'imminent' action to 'take back' city from 'Freedom Convoy' protesters|newspaper=The Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/17/canada-freedom-convoy-ottawa-protest/|access-date=2022-02-17}} [[OC Transpo]] transit stations in the downtown were also closed.{{cite news|last1=Stoodley|first1=Chris|title=Trucker convoy protests, winter storm to affect OC Transpo service|url=https://ottawa.citynews.ca/local-news/trucker-convoy-protests-winter-storm-to-affect-oc-transpo-service-5077987|access-date=2022-02-18|work=CityNews Ottawa|publisher=Rogers Media|date=2022-02-18|location=Ottawa ON}} Convoy organizers Chris Barber and Tamara Lich were arrested in separate instances, and Lich stated that her personal bank account had been frozen. Several other people were arrested after ignoring multiple orders to leave the area.{{cite web|last=Neustaeter|first=Brooklyn|date=2022-02-17|title=Ottawa police make arrests as protesters ignore demands to leave|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/ottawa-police-arrest-convoy-organizers-but-others-remain-defiant/|access-date=2022-02-17|website=CTVNews}} [[Pat King (activist)|Pat King]], another convoy organizer, was arrested by police on February 18.{{cite web|author1=Michael Woods|author2=Josh Pringle|date=2022-02-18|title=Ottawa police arrest 70 people as officers move to clear convoy protest|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/ottawa/article/ottawa-police-arrest-more-than-100-people-as-officers-move-to-clear-convoy-protest/|access-date=2022-02-18|website=CTV News}} That same day, police made at least 70 arrests and towed 21 vehicles by mid-afternoon. The police operation consisted of an initial wave of horse-mounted police officers and other officers on foot wearing high-visibility jackets, followed by tactical teams in green-camouflage gear, and then officers in light armoured vehicles and on horseback bringing up the rear. In at least one case, officers broke a truck's window to reach its occupant, who refused to exit their vehicle. Police also removed children from the site, citing a fear for their safety during the operation. A protester was arrested and charged for allegedly throwing a bicycle and hitting the legs of a horse from the Toronto Police Service Mounted Unit.{{cite news|last=Stoodley|first=Chris|date=2022-02-18|title=Police send in mounted officers to 'create critical space' from 'assaultive' protesters|work=CityNews Ottawa|url=https://ottawa.citynews.ca/police-beat/police-send-in-mounted-officers-to-create-critical-space-from-assaultive-protesters-5082341|access-date=2022-02-18}} In the ensuing skirmish, some pepper spray was used.{{cite news|title=Trucker convoy: Police are making arrests in Ottawa|work=CTV News|access-date=2022-02-18|date=2022-02-18|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/police-moving-in-on-ottawa-protesters-making-arrests-1.5786632|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220218164647/https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/police-moving-in-on-ottawa-protesters-making-arrests-1.5786632|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 18, 2022}} Ottawa Police reported that several officers and horses had been assaulted by demonstrators while attempting to clear the red zone, and over 100 people had been arrested by the evening of February 18. In the afternoon of February 19, police were present in force and were mopping up the area. By late afternoon, the last large group of protesters was corralled near the corner of Bank and Sparks Streets, with police holding a line for several hours as the crowd swelled to approximately 1,000 by dusk and a street party (with an impromptu DJ) ensued. After nightfall, the line of police unexpectedly advanced on the celebratory crowd, pushing it a block south to Queen Street, spraying pepper gas as they did so. Fencing was erected afterwards, and the crowd gradually dispersed over the course of the night.{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Oqm1_o5NSok|title=LIVE Ottawa – RAW Footage: Freedom Convoy 2022 Day 23- 2ND DAY OF CHAOS – Saturday, Feb 19|author=Zoke (uploader)|website=YouTube|date=2022-02-19|access-date=2022-02-20}}{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OAKU9MO59Ww|title=Truckers Freedom Convoy 2022 In Ottawa, Canada Feb 19 Part 2|author=Travel Fun 69|website=YouTube|date=2022-02-19|access-date=2022-02-20}} In total, 170 people had been arrested and a few dozen trucks towed. Protest leaders advised leaving the area and were asking for permission to do so. Pepper gas had been used; one police officer suffered a minor injury.{{cite news|author1=Miriam Berger|title='Freedom Convoy' demonstrators admit protest is near end but vow to reorganize: 'We are going to win'|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2022/02/19/canada-freedom-convoy-protest/|access-date=2022-02-19|newspaper=The Washington Post|date=2022-02-19}} === Municipal response === [[List of mayors of Ottawa|Ottawa Mayor]] [[Jim Watson (Canadian politician)|Jim Watson]] described the situation as "the most serious emergency our city has ever faced".{{cite news|last1=Paas-Lang|first1=Christian|title=Ottawa declares state of emergency as police boost enforcement, target protest's fuel supply|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/convoy-protest-ottawa-sunday-impasse-1.6341548|access-date=2022-02-06|work=CBC News|date=2022-02-06|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite news|title=Ottawa declares state of emergency amid trucker convoy protest|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8599205/ottawa-state-of-emergency-trucker-convoy/|access-date=2022-02-06|work=Global News|agency=The Canadian Press|date=2022-02-06|location=Toronto ON}} === Provincial response === On February 11, Ontario Premier Doug Ford declared a state of emergency in the province.{{cite web|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/premier-ford-declares-state-of-emergency-in-ontario-over-blockade-court-grants-ambassador-bridge-injunction/|title=Premier Ford declares state of emergency in Ontario over blockade; court grants Ambassador Bridge injunction|date=2022-02-11|website=CTVNews}} On the same day, Ontario Superior Court granted an injunction to remove protesters from the Ambassador Bridge.{{cite news|date=2022-02-11|title='Freedom convoy' protests: Injunction granted to remove Windsor blockade; Ontario's state of emergency|language=en-CA|work=The Toronto Star|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/canada/2022/02/11/freedom-convoy-protests-ford-declares-state-of-emergency-over-siege-says-protesters-risk-losing-truck-licences.html|access-date=2022-02-11|issn=0319-0781}} The injunction was sought by multiple industry groups affected by the blockade including the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association.{{cite news|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/windsor/protesters-ambassador-bridge-windsor-covid-deadline-1.6349424|title=Protest crowd remains on Ambassador Bridge after police clear out part of blockade|work=CBC|date=2022-02-22|author1=Mark Gollom|author2=Samantha Craggs}} On February 23 at 5:00 pm, Ontario ended the state of emergency.{{cite web|last1=Davidson|first1=Sean|last2=Alberga|first2=Hannah|date=2022-02-23|title=Ontario terminates state of emergency put in place for trucker convoy protest|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/toronto/article/ontario-terminates-state-of-emergency-put-in-place-for-trucker-convoy-protest/|access-date=2022-02-24|website=CTV News Toronto}} === Federal response === ==== Emergencies Act ==== {{see also|Emergencies Act#Canada convoy protest}} On February 14, Trudeau invoked the ''[[Emergencies Act]]''. The Act gave police extraordinary powers to "end border blockades and the occupation of downtown Ottawa by so-called 'Freedom Convoy' protesters."{{cite news|last=MacCharles|first=Tonda|date=2022-02-23|title=Justin Trudeau lifts Emergencies Act|work=The Toronto Star|url=https://www.thestar.com/politics/2022/02/23/canadian-press-newsalert-trudeau-set-to-revoke-emergencies-act.html|access-date=2022-02-23|issn=0319-0781}}{{cite news|last=Tunney|first=Catharine|date=2022-02-14|title=Federal government invokes Emergencies Act for first time ever in response to protests|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/trudeau-premiers-cabinet-1.6350734|access-date=2022-02-14}}{{cite news|last=Gallant|first=Jacques|url=https://www.thestar.com/politics/federal/2022/02/14/the-emergencies-act-has-been-invoked-what-does-that-mean.html|date=2022-02-14|access-date=2022-02-14|title=The Emergencies Act has been invoked. What does that mean?|work=The Star}} Deputy Prime Minister [[Chrystia Freeland]] said that under the ''Act'', protesters' "trucks can be seized, their corporate accounts frozen and vehicle insurance suspended".{{cite news|last1=MacCharles|first1=Tonda|last2=Ballingall|first2=Alex|date=2022-02-14|access-date=2022-02-14|url=https://www.thestar.com/politics/federal/2022/02/14/ottawa-protesters-remain-defiant-as-federal-government-ramps-up-emergency-response.html|title=Justin Trudeau invokes Emergencies Act to stop 'Freedom Convoy' protests|work=The Star}} While Trudeau insisted that the military would not be deployed, Emergency Preparedness Minister [[Bill Blair (politician)|Bill Blair]] did not rule out deployment, and called the lack of enforcement by Ottawa Police "inexplicable".{{cite news|author1=Catharine Tunney|author2=Christian Paas-Lang|title=Government considering emergency powers with 'appropriate caution,' federal minister says|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/emergencies-act-military-ottawa-protests-1.6350236|access-date=2022-02-18|work=CBC News|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-13|location=Ottawa ON}} The ''Emergencies Act'' replaced the former ''[[War Measures Act]]'', which was famously invoked by Trudeau's father, former Prime Minister [[Pierre Trudeau]], to deploy the [[Canadian Forces]] against the ''[[Front de libération du Québec]]'' during the 1970 [[October Crisis]]. This was the first time the act had been invoked since its 1988 inception. After the invocation of the Act, [[Chrystia Freeland]], [[Deputy Prime Minister (Canada)|Deputy Prime Minister]] and [[Minister of Finance (Canada)|Minister of Finance]] announced that the purview of the [[Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada]] was expanded to include the monitoring of funds sent through [[crowdfunding]] platforms such as [[GoFundMe]], where protestors had raised millions that were ultimately refunded, as well as payment providers formerly outside its scope. Freeland specifically cited [[cryptocurrency]] transactions, which the protestors turned to after GoFundMe, as a type of digital transaction that the new measures were meant to cover. Canadian banks were also temporarily given the authority to freeze accounts suspected of being used to support the protests without the need to obtain court orders, were granted legal immunity if they chose to do so, and were allowed to more freely share information with law enforcement and government agencies.{{cite news|last1=Bradshaw|first1=James|last2=Curry|first2=Bill|date=2022-02-14|title=Emergencies Act will expand powers of Canadian banks to freeze accounts, halt funds|work=[[The Globe and Mail]]|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/business/article-emergencies-act-powers-to-broaden-scope-of-anti-money-laundering-rules/|access-date=2022-02-15}} On February 21, the House of Commons voted to confirm the ''Emergencies Act'', with 185 for and 151 opposing the motion.{{cite news|last=Major|first=Darren|title=Emergencies Act passes crucial House of Commons vote with NDP support|work=CBC News|access-date=2022-02-21|date=2022-02-21|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/politics/trudeau-emergencies-act-vote-1.6359243}} On February 23, Trudeau told a press conference that he would lift the ''Emergencies Act'' at 5:00 p.m. He warned that any violations of law during the emergency would be investigated.{{cite web|date=2022-02-23|title='Immediate emergency situation is over': PM Trudeau revokes Emergencies Act|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/politics/article/immediate-emergency-situation-is-over-pm-trudeau-revokes-emergencies-act/|access-date=2022-02-24|website=CTVNews}} ==Related events== ===Other border crossing obstructions=== ====British Columbia–Washington==== Beginning February 5, protesters attempted to blockade the [[Pacific Highway Border Crossing]] between [[Blaine, Washington]], and [[Surrey, British Columbia]].{{cite news|title=Vaccine mandate protesters force highway closure at Canada-U.S. border in Surrey as rallies enter 3rd weekend|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/british-columbia/surrey-border-protest-feb-12-1.6349787|access-date=2022-02-14|work=CBC News|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-13|location=Vancouver BC}} Protesters returned on February 12, where a police barricade was set up. A convoy led by a privately owned vehicle painted in military style broke through the police barricades,{{cite news|last1=Little|first1=Simon|title=Convoy vehicles break through RCMP barricades as B.C. border crossing effectively closed|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8615476/b-c-braces-for-another-day-of-anti-covid-19-mandate-convoys/|access-date=2022-02-14|work=Global News|date=2022-02-12|location=Vancouver BC}} and the border crossing was subsequently closed.{{cite news|title=Surrey Mounties blocking anti-vaccine mandate truck convoy at U.S. border|url=https://vancouversun.com/news/local-news/surrey-mounties-blocking-anti-vaccine-mandate-truck-convoy-at-border|access-date=2022-02-14|work=Vancouver Sun|publisher=Postmedia|date=2022-02-13|location=Vancouver BC}} Surrey RCMP moved into the area to clear it on February 13, making 12 arrests.{{cite news|title=Arrests as RCMP clear protest near Pacific Highway border crossing in B.C.|url=https://www.cp24.com/news/arrests-as-rcmp-clear-protest-near-pacific-highway-border-crossing-in-b-c-1.5782376|access-date=2022-02-18|work=CP24|agency=The Canadian Press|publisher=BellMedia|date=2022-02-15|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite news|last1=Smith|first1=Charlie|title=Surrey RCMP arrest four protesters near Pacific Highway border crossing|url=https://www.straight.com/covid-19-pandemic/news/surrey-rcmp-arrest-four-protesters-near-pacific-highway-border-crossing|access-date=2022-02-14|work=The Georgia Straight|date=2022-02-13|location=Vancouver BC}} On February 15, the border crossing reopened.{{cite news|author1=Claire Fenton|author2=Monika Gul|title=Pacific Highway border open, 12 more protesters arrested|url=https://vancouver.citynews.ca/2022/02/15/pac-highway-crossing-protests/|access-date=2022-02-18|publisher=Rogers Media|date=2022-02-15|location=Vancouver BC}} ====Manitoba–North Dakota==== Beginning February 10, a blockade closed off the [[Pembina–Emerson Border Crossing]] in [[Emerson, Manitoba]].{{cite web|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/winnipeg/article/emerson-border-remains-blocked-no-arrests-made-rcmp/|title=Emerson border remains blocked, no arrests made: RCMP|date=2022-02-11|website=CTV News Winnipeg}} Through RCMP negotiating, protesters voluntarily left the blockade on February 16 and the border crossing was reopened.{{cite web|last=Bernhardt|first=Darren|date=2022-02-16|title='A perfect solution': Manitoba border blockade ends as RCMP escort protesters away|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/manitoba/emerson-border-blockade-protest-manitoba-rcmp-update-1.6353568|access-date=2022-02-22|website=CBC News}}{{cite web|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/winnipeg/article/blockade-protesters-leave-emerson-border-with-police-escort/|title=Blockade protesters leave Emerson border with police escort|date=2022-02-16|website=CTV News Winnipeg}} The blockage at Emerson, Manitoba border crossing was cleared February 15, 2022.{{cite news|author1=Ian Austen|author2=Dan Bilefsky|title=Ontario provincial police mobilize in possible preparation to disperse protesters.|url=https://www.nytimes.com/live/2022/02/17/world/canada-ottawa-protests-news#ontario-provincial-police-mobilize-in-possible-preparation-to-disperse-protesters|access-date=2022-02-17|work=The New York Times|date=2022-02-17}} === Other Canadian protests === ====Toronto==== [[File:Toronto version of Freedom Convoy protest Feb 5.png|thumb|Toronto version of the Freedom Convoy protest on February 5, outside of the [[Royal Ontario Museum]]]] On February 4, 2022, some vehicles and tractors arrived outside the [[Royal Ontario Museum]] on [[Avenue Road, Toronto|Avenue Road]] north of [[Queen's Park (Toronto)|Queen's Park]] in Toronto. Also on February 4, [[Toronto Police Service|Toronto Police]] started closing roads in [[downtown Toronto|the downtown]] in anticipation of a large protest and told residents to expect a larger police presence during the weekend.{{cite web|last=Wilson|first=Kerrisa|date=2022-02-05|title=Huge crowd of protesters hold anti-mandate protest in downtown Toronto|url=https://www.cp24.com/news/huge-crowd-of-protesters-hold-anti-mandate-protest-in-downtown-toronto-1.5769371|access-date=2022-02-05|website=CP24}}{{cite news|date=2022-02-05|title=Protesters, counter-protesters flood the downtown core; Ontario farmers' group denounces tractor rallies|language=en-CA|work=The Toronto Star|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/gta/2022/02/05/trucker-protest-in-toronto.html|access-date=2022-02-05|issn=0319-0781}} On February 5, truckers, tractor drivers, and protesters arrived in Queen's Park to protest COVID-19 vaccine mandates. In response, a group of healthcare workers staged a counter-protest. Protest activity later moved north to [[Bloor Street]] and later to the intersection of Bloor Street West and Avenue Road. Hundreds of protesters and several trucks blocked the intersection for hours. Long stretches of major arterial roads, including [[University Avenue (Toronto)|University Avenue]], [[College Street (Toronto)|College Street]], and [[Yonge Street]], were shut down. Fearing possible threats against healthcare workers, [[University Health Network]] and [[Sinai Health System]], which operate major hospitals in the protest area, warned their employees to avoid wearing any clothing or badges that would identify them as health-care workers outside hospital premises.{{cite web|last=Aguilar|first=Bryann|date=2022-02-04|title=Health-care workers announce counter protest to convoy in Toronto|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/toronto/article/health-care-workers-announce-counter-protest-to-convoy-in-toronto/|access-date=2022-02-06|website=CTV News Toronto}} During the protest, a 22-year-old man was arrested and charged with assault with a weapon, use of a [[smoke bomb]] (administering a noxious substance), and public mischief. A second man was arrested for assault with a weapon after throwing [[feces]] at another person.{{cite web|last=Jones|first=Hannah|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8598933/man-arrested-feces-throwing-toronto-protests/|title=Man, 34, arrested after feces thrown during anti-mandate protest in Toronto|website=Global News|date=2022-02-06}} ====Winnipeg==== On February 5, at a related protest in [[Downtown Winnipeg]], four people were injured in a [[vehicle ramming attack|ramming]] incident. One was hospitalized and later released.{{cite web|last=Reimer|first=Will|date=2022-02-05|title=Hit-and-run injures 4 at site of anti-mandate protest in downtown Winnipeg, one man arrested|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8597464/hit-and-run-truck-convoy-manitoba-legisltature/|access-date=2022-02-06|website=Global News}} As of February 7, protests were on their fourth day. Police have yet to write tickets or lay charges against protesters, despite numerous noise complaints, fireworks without permit, and incidents of "homophobia, racism, and gender-based harassment."{{cite news|last1=MacLean|first1=Cameron|title=Downtown Winnipeg residents losing patience with convoy protest noise|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/manitoba/winnipeg-convoy-protest-noise-1.6343010|access-date=2022-02-07|work=CBC News Manitoba|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-07|location=Winnipeg MB}}{{cite news|last1=Thompson|first1=Sam|title=Winnipeg has fared 'better than any other city' with truck protest: police|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8600439/winnipeg-freedom-convoy-truck-protest/|access-date=2022-02-07|work=Global News|date=2022-02-07}} ====Fredericton==== A protest targeting the [[New Brunswick Legislature]] in [[Fredericton]] began on Friday, February 11.{{cite news|title=Hundreds gather in Fredericton to protest COVID-19 measures|first=Kevin|last=Bissett|agency=Canadian Press|publisher=CTV News|date=2022-02-11|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/atlantic/article/hundreds-gather-in-fredericton-to-protest-covid-19-measures/|access-date=2022-02-14}} [[Fredericton Police]] established [[security checkpoint|checkpoints]] leading into the city's downtown, and refused entry to any large truck without a valid [[manifest (transportation)|manifest]].{{cite news|title=Despite larger crowds, Fredericton mayor finds second day of protests peaceful|first=Isabelle|last=Leger|date=2022-02-12|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/new-brunswick/protest-convoy-fredericton-day-2-1.6349477|access-date=2022-02-14}} A group of about 300 protesters gathered at the [[New Brunswick Legislative Building]], growing to a peak of 700 by Saturday. By Sunday afternoon, police reported that about 70 protesters remained in the city, and that there had been three arrests related to the protest, and numerous tickets issued for traffic and city bylaw infractions.{{cite news|title=Police say Fredericton protest 'ongoing' as Day 3 wraps up|first1=Aidan|last1=Cox|first2=Isabelle|last2=Leger|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/new-brunswick/covid-nb-protest-day-3-fredericton-1.6350108|access-date=2022-02-14}} ====Nova Scotia==== On January 30, the Government of [[Nova Scotia]] made it illegal for people to gather on [[Nova Scotia Highway 104|Highway 104]] along the border with New Brunswick in anticipation of protesters blockading the highway.[https://www.foxnews.com/world/nova-scotia-outlaws-gathering-highway-freedom-convoy-truckers-vaccine-mandate Nova Scotia outlaws support on highway for 'freedom' truckers days after massive protest] Fox News On February 6, hundreds of vehicles drove through [[Halifax, Nova Scotia|Halifax]] as part of the "Freedom Convoy".[https://www.ctvnews.ca/atlantic/article/hundreds-of-vehicles-ride-through-halifax-in-freedom-convoy/ Hundreds of vehicles ride through Halifax in 'Freedom Convoy'] CTV News On February 12, hundreds of people from around Nova Scotia travelled to Halifax to protest. The [[Halifax Regional Police]] blocked off access to some roads in downtown Halifax in response to the protest.[https://globalnews.ca/news/8615284/ns-freedom-convoy-halifax-protest-feb-12/ Nova Scotia 'Freedom Convoy' makes its way to Halifax] Global News ==== Edmonton ==== Protestors gathered in [[Edmonton]] for three consecutive Saturdays, and an [[Court of Queen's Bench of Alberta|Alberta Court of Queen's Bench]] Justice granted an interim injunction on February 11, 2022, prohibiting protest participants from causing incessant sound with horns and megaphones within city limits.{{cite web|author1=Emily Mertz|author2=Caley Gibson|date=2022-02-12|title=Judge grants City of Edmonton interim injunction for downtown protest convoys|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8613568/edmonton-interim-injunction-downtown-protest-truck-convoys/|access-date=2022-02-15|website=Global News|language=en-US}} [[Edmonton Police Service]] (EPS) was criticized by City Councilor Michael Janz for taking stronger enforcement measures against a counter protest than it did with the main protest.{{cite web|last=Chacon|first=Chris|date=2022-02-13|title=Anger, frustration over how Edmonton police handled counter-protest and convoy noise: councillor|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8617331/edmonton-police-handled-counter-protest-convoy/|access-date=2022-02-15|website=Global News|language=en-US}} According to EPS, the responses varied as they tried to protect public and officer safety, and keep traffic moving. ==== Other ==== [[File:Convoi de la liberté à Québec 02.jpg|thumb|Freedom Convoy ({{langx|fr|link=no|Convoi de la liberté}}) protest in Quebec City on February 5, 2022]] Truckers and protesters have gathered in [[Oakville, Ontario|Oakville]], [[Mississauga]], [[Vaughan]], other parts of the [[Greater Toronto Area]], [[Orangeville, Ontario|Orangeville]], Quebec City, [[Calgary]], Vancouver, [[Vancouver Island]], [[Kelowna]], [[Regina, Saskatchewan|Regina]], and [[Victoria, British Columbia|Victoria]].{{cite web|last=Lee|first=Michael|date=2022-02-05|title=Demonstrators descend on Ottawa as 'freedom convoy' protests spread beyond capital|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/demonstrators-descend-on-ottawa-as-freedom-convoy-protests-spread-beyond-capital/|access-date=2022-02-05|website=CTV News}}{{cite web|last=Rosella|first=Louie|date=2022-02-05|title=Freedom Convoy of truckers in Oakville, Square One, Sherway Gardens, Vaughan Mills and across the GTA en route to Queen's Park in Toronto|url=https://www.insidehalton.com/news-story/10567188-live-now-freedom-convoy-of-truckers-in-oakville-square-one-sherway-gardens-vaughan-mills-and-across-the-gta-en-route-to-queen-s-park-in-toronto/|access-date=2022-02-05|website=Inside Halton|language=en-CA}}{{cite web|last=Little|first=Simon|date=2022-02-05|title=Five arrested as anti-COVID-19 mandate convoys, rallies staged in British Columbia|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8597602/anti-covid-19-mandate-convoys-rallies-b-c/|access-date=2022-02-06|website=Global News|language=en-US}}{{cite news|last1=Quon|first1=Alexander|title=Health mandate protesters near Sask. Legislature leave after police issue more than 30 tickets|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/saskatchewan/vaccine-legislature-covid-19-protest-1.6342128|access-date=2022-02-07|work=CBC News Saskatchewan|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-07}}{{cite web|last=Toy|first=Adam|date=2022-02-07|title=Calgary police lay 80 charges in connection with convoy protest|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8601724/80-charges-laid-calgary-convoy-protest/|access-date=2022-02-21|website=Global News|language=en-US}} A convoy in [[Charlottetown]] attracted hundreds of protesters on February 12, mostly driving personal vehicles and farm equipment.{{cite news|title=Vaccine-mandate protest rolls through downtown Charlottetown|date=2022-02-12|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/prince-edward-island/pei-freedom-convoy-charlottetown-1.6348335|access-date=2022-02-14}} ===International protests=== {{see also|France convoy protest|2022 Wellington protest|Convoy to Canberra}} The Freedom Convoy also inspired several Facebook and [[Telegram (software)|Telegram]] groups organizing similar protests in 34 countries including [[Argentina]], [[Austria]], [[Cyprus]], New Zealand, Australia and the United States.{{cite news|url=https://www.thestar.com/news/canada/2022/02/03/canadas-freedom-convoy-is-inspiring-protests-in-other-countries.html|title=How Canada's 'Freedom Convoy' is inspiring protests in other countries|first1=Alex|last1=McKeen|first2=Lex|last2=Harvey|first3=Kieran|last3=Leavitt|date=2022-02-03|work=[[Toronto Star]]}}{{cite news|last1=Tindale|first1=Lanie|last2=Crowe|first2=Alex|title=Latest updates on Convoy to Canberra protest Millions Against Mandatory Vaccination impact on ACT|url=https://www.canberratimes.com.au/story/7609361/live-blog-convoy-to-canberra-protests-hit-nations-capital/|access-date=2022-02-07|work=[[Canberra Times]]|date=2022-02-07|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220207083233/https://www.canberratimes.com.au/story/7609361/as-it-happened-protesters-heckle-police-after-stockade-flag-pulled-down/|archive-date=2022-02-07|url-status=live}} As is the case in Canada, demonstrations were partly supported by local far-right groups; in Finland by members of [[Neo-fascism|neo-fascist]] [[Blue-and-Black Movement]], [[Soldiers of Odin]] and [[Power Belongs to the People]], and in Germany by the [[Reichsbürger movement]] and [[Alternative for Germany]].{{cite news|last=Mac Dougall|first=David|url=https://www.euronews.com/2022/02/04/protests-highlight-finnish-frustrations-at-ongoing-covid-restrictions|title=Protests highlight Finnish frustrations at ongoing COVID restrictions|date=2022-02-09|work=[[Euronews]]}}{{cite news|last=Clermont-Goulet|first=Jean-Michel|url=https://www.24heures.ca/2022/02/08/le-convoi-de-la-liberte-dottawa-en-a-inspire-plus-dun-a-travers-le-monde|title=Le "convoi de la liberté" d'Ottawa en a inspiré plus d'un à travers le monde|date=2022-02-09|work=[[24heures.ca]]|language=fr}}{{cite news|last1=Hämäläinen|first1=Veli-Pekka|last2=Mattinen|first2=Johanna|url=https://yle.fi/uutiset/3-12300898|title="Me saadaan oikeasti koko Helsinki tukkoon" – tästä on kyse sosiaalisen median kapinaryhmässä, joka aikoo aiheuttaa kaaoksen perjantaina Helsingissä|date=2022-02-09|work=[[Yle]]}} In February 2022, a solidarity protest was organized in the United Kingdom with a convoy driving from [[Scotland]] to London with stops in cities throughout the British isles. Other convoys were reported in [[Wales]] and parts of England.{{cite web|last=Walker|first=David|url=https://www.scottishdailyexpress.co.uk/news/scottish-news/scottish-freedom-convoy-heading-london-26142526|title=Scottish 'Freedom Convoy' heading for London to support Canadian truckers' and call for end to Covid rules|website=Scottish Daily Express|date=2022-02-04|access-date=2022-02-08}} Officials in Paris and [[Brussels]] banned protests relating to the Freedom Convoy, following information from organizers of a similar event opposed to [[Vaccine passports during the COVID-19 pandemic#France|France's Health Pass]], ''le Convoi de la Liberté'', that five convoys from across France were due to reach Paris between February 11 and 14.{{cite web|author1=Joseph Ataman|author2=Lianne Kolirin|author3=Mick Krever|author4=Aliza Kassim Khalidi|title=Paris and Brussels ban protests linked to French 'Freedom Convoy'|url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/02/10/europe/paris-freedom-convoy-banned-intl/index.html|access-date=2022-02-10|website=CNN|date=2022-02-10}}{{cite web|date=2022-02-10|title=Paris police ban 'Freedom Convoy' protest against COVID restrictions|url=https://www.euronews.com/2022/02/10/paris-police-ban-canada-style-freedom-convoy-protest-against-french-covid-restrictions|access-date=2022-02-10|website=euronews|language=en}} [[Law enforcement in France|French Police]] used [[tear gas]] to disperse protesters, in addition to intercepting hundreds of vehicles and issuing hundreds of fines to those participating in the convoy.{{cite web|last=Cokelaere|first=Hanne|date=2022-02-12|title=French police fine hundreds in an attempt to fend off 'freedom convoys'|url=https://www.politico.eu/article/french-police-fine-hundreds-in-an-attempt-to-fend-off-freedom-convoys/|access-date=2022-02-16|website=[[Politico]]|language=en-US}}{{cite web|last=Schrader|first=Adam|title=French police tear gas 'Freedom Convoy' protesters in Paris|url=https://www.upi.com/Top_News/World-News/2022/02/12/france-french-police-tear-gas-freedom-convoy-protesters-paris/5001644691622/|access-date=2022-02-13|website=UPI|language=en}} On February 23, a group of American truckers began a solidarity protest convoy from California to Washington, D.C. called the "People's Convoy".{{cite news|last1=Younis|first1=Omar|last2=Swanson|first2=David|date=2022-02-23|title=Inspired by Canadian truck protests, 'People's Convoy' heads to Washington|work=Reuters|url=https://www.reuters.com/world/us/us-truckers-plan-pandemic-protest-inspired-by-canadian-counterparts-2022-02-23/|access-date=2022-02-25}} In response, [[The Pentagon]] approved the deployment of 700 unarmed [[National Guard (United States)|National Guard]] troops to D.C.{{cite web|last1=Khalil|first1=Ashraf|last2=C. Baldor|first2=Lolita|date=2022-02-23|title=National Guard to deploy 700 troops in D.C. to control truck convoy, says Pentagon|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8639304/national-guard-700-troops-truck-convoy-pentagon/|access-date=2022-02-25|website=Global News|language=en-CA}} In early March, a demonstration was held by convoy protesters in D.C. near the [[Washington Monument]], but only a small number of people attended.{{cite web|last=Gonzalez|first=Oscar|date=2022-03-02|title=Truck Convoys Heading to DC but With Less Fanfare|url=https://www.cnet.com/news/trucker-protest-convoys-head-to-dc-but-with-less-force-than-canada-demonstrations/|access-date=2022-03-02|website=CNET}} One convoy called the "US Freedom Convoy" was scheduled to arrive in D.C. on March 1, but disbanded when only five trucks arrived in [[Las Vegas]] from Los Angeles. Also in early March, more than 1,000 large trucks, recreational vehicles and cars gathered in the outskirts of D.C. as part of the "People's Convoy" protest. A website for the protest said that they will not enter "D.C. proper". One participant, who described himself as the lead trucker, said he would drive his truck into the city.{{cite news|last=Hesson|first=Ted|date=2022-03-05|title=More than 1,000 trucks and cars roll in to Washington area in 'People's Convoy'|work=National Post|url=https://nationalpost.com/news/more-than-1000-trucks-and-cars-roll-in-to-washington-area-in-peoples-convoy|access-date=2022-03-06}} After three weeks of protesting, on March 27, People's Convoy co-organizer Mike Landis announced that the convoy would leave D.C. and drive back to California.{{cite news|last=Petrizzo|first=Zachary|date=2022-03-28|title=People's Convoy Gives Up After Accomplishing Nothing in D.C.|work=[[The Daily Beast]]|url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/peoples-convoy-gives-up-will-leave-washington-dc-area-after-three-weeks-of-doing-nothing|access-date=2022-03-28}} On May 20, 2022, the People's Convoy disbanded and declared "victory" despite never reentering D.C. as planned. Several smaller splinter groups formed in the aftermath of the disbandment, such as the 1776 Restoration Movement.{{cite web|last=Marquis|first=Erin|date=2022-05-23|title=The People's Convoy Is Over|url=https://jalopnik.com/the-peoples-convoy-is-over-1848962972|access-date=2022-06-26|website=Jalopnik|language=en-us}}{{cite web|last=Marquis|first=Erin|date=2022-05-26|title=Why Canada's Freedom Convoy Succeeded Where the U.S. People's Convoy Failed|url=https://jalopnik.com/why-canadas-freedom-convoy-succeeded-where-the-u-s-peo-1848977701|access-date=2022-06-27|website=Jalopnik|language=en-us}} ===Rolling Thunder Ottawa=== {{Update|part=section|date=June 2022}} A motorcycle convoy of "hundreds" is scheduled to enter Ottawa on April 29. The group's route intends to stop at the [[National War Memorial (Canada)|National War Memorial]] and Parliament Hill. Looking to avoid of a repeat of the previous convoy, Ottawa Police announced that vehicles would not be allowed near the Hill. Ottawa Police are to be joined by members of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, Ontario Provincial Police, and various municipal police services.{{cite news|title=Convoy organizer warns of 'free-for-all' if police ban protest from downtown Ottawa|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/ottawa/article/convoy-organizer-warns-of-free-for-all-if-police-ban-protest-from-downtown-ottawa/|access-date=2022-04-28|work=CTV News Ottawa|agency=The Canadian Press|publisher=BellMedia|date=2022-04-26|location=Toronto ON}} Up to 831 RCMP are available as special constables.{{cite news|last1=Laucius|first1=Joanne|title='Rolling Thunder' starts out slowly, but situation starts bubbling up in evening|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/local-news/rolling-thunder-starts-out-slowly-but-situation-starts-bubbling-up-in-evening|access-date=2022-04-30|work=Ottawa Citizen|publisher=Postmedia|date=2022-04-30|location=Ottawa ON}} The Royal Canadian Legion distanced itself from the protest, stating in part "The Legion defends these sacred sites of Remembrance, and asserts that they should never be used as a backdrop for any other agenda beyond the act of Remembrance. Our hope and appeal to all who may gather before the National War Memorial this weekend is that they do so with a focus purely on Remembrance."{{cite news|last1=Miller|first1=Jacquie|title=Rolling Thunder: What we know about the weekend motorcycle rally right now|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/local-news/rolling-thunder-what-we-know-about-the-weekend-motorcycle-rally-right-now|access-date=2022-04-28|work=Ottawa Citizen|publisher=Postmedia|date=2022-04-28|location=Ottawa ON}} A large crowd gathered on Rideau Street in the evening, in front of Rideau Centre. After an escalation of crowd aggression, police with helmets and shields were deployed. An attempted to occupy a parking lot was overturned. In total, seven people were arrested on the first evening.{{cite news|last1=Quenneville|first1=Guy|title=Ottawa police arrest 7, tow vehicles on Day 1 of weekend rally|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/rolling-thunder-ottawa-police-shields-aggressive-crowd-1.6436764|access-date=2022-04-30|work=CBC News Ottawa|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-04-29|location=Ottawa ON}} A professor who studies the dynamics of protests counted less than a thousand people at the rally, including police and journalists. A counter protest, described as an "unwelcoming party", was held by locals at Strathcona Park.{{cite news|last1=Lapierre|first1=Matthew|title=Ottawans unite to show opposition to 'Rolling Thunder' rally|url=https://ottawacitizen.com/news/local-news/ottawans-unite-to-show-opposition-to-rolling-thunder-rally|access-date=2022-04-30|work=Ottawa Citizen|publisher=Postmedia|date=2022-04-29|location=Ottawa ON}} Saturday's planned events include a rally at Parliament Hill featuring COVID-19 conspiracy theorist [[Chris Sky|Christopher Saccoccia]], widely referred to as Chris Sky. Rolling Thunder's organizer, Neil Sheard, has attempted to distance himself from Saccoccia. Another group, "Ottawa Unity Chain," intends to form a human chain. It claims to have collaborated with Sheard's group. == Results and aftermath == ===Parliamentary committee investigations=== The [[House of Commons of Canada|House of Commons]] committee structure lent itself to ongoing investigations, in the [[Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance]] (FINA), the [[Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Public Safety and National Security]] (SECU) and the [[Canadian House of Commons Standing Committee on Procedure and House Affairs]] (PROC). Early in March 2022, a [[Special Joint Committee on the Declaration of Emergency]] (DEDC) of the [[Senate of Canada|Senate]] and the House of Commons was struck to investigate the events associated with it.{{cite news|title=Committees DEDC Special Joint Committee on the Declaration of Emergency|url=https://www.parl.ca/Committees/en/DEDC|access-date=2022-05-22|publisher=Parliament of Canada}}{{cite news|last1=Wherry|first1=Aaron|title=The committee reviewing Trudeau's use of the Emergencies Act faces a daunting
