Oh and #freedomconvoy22 is making an impact. The last thing they want is an American trucker convoy.|url=https://twitter.com/RepGosar/status/1491837757580140570|access-date=2022-02-10|website=Twitter}} [[Marjorie Taylor Greene]],{{cite web|last1=Lardner|first1=Richard|last2=Smith|first2=Michelle|last3=Swenson|first3=Ali|date=2022-02-17|title=US funds for Canada protests may sway American politics too|url=https://mynbc15.com/news/coronavirus/us-funds-for-canada-protests-may-sway-american-politics-too-canadian-prime-minister-justin-trudeau-democrats-republicans-congress-senate-house-of-representatives-marjorie-taylor-greene-ted-cruz-ottawa-trucker-protest-coronavirus-restrictions-pandemic|access-date=2022-02-17|website=mynbc15.com}} [[Jim Jordan]],{{cite web|last=Jordan|first=Jim|date=2022-01-29|title=God Bless the truck drivers in Canada protesting vaccine mandates.|url=https://twitter.com/jim_jordan/status/1487458563949740033|access-date=2022-02-01|website=Twitter}} [[Kevin McCarthy]],{{cite web|last=McCarthy|first=Kevin|date=2022-01-31|title=Truckers never stopped working throughout the pandemic, and government should not slow them down now.|url=https://twitter.com/gopleader/status/1488280193181515779|access-date=2022-02-01|website=Twitter}} [[Chip Roy]], [[Steve Scalise]], [[Governor (United States)|Governor]] [[Ron DeSantis]],{{cite web|last=McNaull|first=Steve|date=2022-02-01|title=Freedom Convoy works with media strategist to improve image|url=https://www.kelownanow.com/news/news/National_News/Freedom_Convoy_works_with_media_strategist_to_improve_image/|access-date=2022-02-04|website=KelownaNow}} [[Kay Ivey]],{{cite web|last1=Tran|first1=Paula|last2=Skjerven|first2=Kelly|date=2022-02-16|title=Kenney, Moe join forces with U.S. governors calling for end to border vaccine mandate|url=https://globalnews.ca/news/8625028/kenney-moe-us-governors-covid-vaccine-mandate-letter/|access-date=2022-02-16|website=Global News}} former US President [[Donald Trump]],{{cite web|last=Yun|first=Ton|date=2022-01-29|title=Donald Trump voices support for truckers convoy protest in Ottawa|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/donald-trump-voices-support-for-truckers-convoy-protest-in-ottawa/|access-date=2022-01-30|website=CTV News}} former Governor [[Mike Huckabee]], former [[White House Chief of Staff]] [[Mark Meadows]],{{cite web|last=Meadows|first=Mark|date=2022-01-30|title=God bless these truckers standing up for freedom!|url=https://twitter.com/markmeadows/status/1487942256451588097|access-date=2022-01-31|website=Twitter}} TV personalities [[Donald Trump Jr.]],{{cite web|last1=Trump|first1=Donald Jr.|title=Video: Heroic Truck Driver Stands Up To Medical Tyranny!|url=https://www.facebook.com/watch/?v=2712087222431976|access-date=2022-01-26|website=Facebook}} [[Lara Trump]],{{cite web|last=Bauder|first=David|date=2022-02-11|title=U.S. conservative figures cheer on Canadian trucker protest|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/entertainment/u-s-conservative-figures-cheer-on-canadian-trucker-protest-1.5777742|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220211171208/https://www.ctvnews.ca/entertainment/u-s-conservative-figures-cheer-on-canadian-trucker-protest-1.5777742|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 11, 2022|access-date=2022-02-11|website=CTV News}} and singer [[Ted Nugent]].{{cite web|last=De La Cruz|first=Rene Ray|date=2022-02-18|title='People's Convoy' to protest government COVID-19 mandates to begin in Barstow|url=https://www.vvdailypress.com/story/news/2022/02/18/american-peoples-convoy-covid-19-protest-begin-barstow/6838584001/|access-date=2022-02-18|website=Daily Press}} Fox News' personalities [[Tucker Carlson]], [[Sean Hannity]] and [[Laura Ingraham]]{{cite web|last=Ward|first=Myah|title=What's happening with the Canadian trucker convoy? Here's what you need to know|url=https://www.politico.com/news/2022/02/14/canadian-truckers-block-bridge-strike-protests-00008620|access-date=2022-02-19|website=POLITICO|date=2022-02-14}} expressed support for the protestors in broadcasts. Former [[United States Ambassador to Canada]] [[Bruce Heyman]] criticized American groups' support and funding of the protests.{{cite web|author1=Claire Thornton|author2=John Bacon |author3=Jorge L. Ortiz |title=US closely tied to 'nationwide insurrection' in Canada against COVID mandates: Live updates|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/health/2022/02/06/us-tied-to-covid-protests-canada-updates/6678370001/|access-date=2022-02-10|website=USA Today|language=en-US}}{{cite web|last=Gillies|first=Rob|title=US Groups Need to Stop Interfering in Canada: Ex-US Envoy|url=https://www.nbcbayarea.com/news/national-international/us-groups-need-to-stop-interfering-in-canada-ex-us-envoy/2802813/|access-date=2022-02-10|website=NBC Bay Area|date=2022-02-06|language=en-US}} On February 10, US Transportation Secretary [[Pete Buttigieg]] and Homeland Security Secretary [[Alejandro Mayorkas]] contacted their Canadian counterparts, recommended the use of Canadian federal powers, and offered the support of the Departments of Transportation and Homeland Security.{{cite web|title=U.S. truckers planning protest convoy, perhaps starting in L.A. for Super Bowl, DHS warns|url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/trucker-protest-convoy-super-bowl-los-angeles-dhs-warning/|access-date=2022-02-19|website=www.cbsnews.com|date=2022-02-10|language=en-US}} Michigan Governor [[Gretchen Whitmer]] stated of the convoy, "It's hitting pay checks and production lines. That is unacceptable."{{cite news|date=2022-02-11|title=Freedom Convoy: US urges Canada to end blockade by truckers|language=en-GB|work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-60342800|access-date=2022-02-19}} According to CBC News, "While the Democrats and the White House said almost nothing about Trudeau invoking the Emergencies Act, the Republican ecosystem sprouted thickets of indignation." Carlson called Trudeau "Canadian dictator Justin Trudeau" and ran the headline: "Trudeau has declared Canada a dictatorship."{{cite news|last=Panetta|first=Alexander|date=2022-02-19|title=In America's partisan carnival, Justin Trudeau is now on display|work=CBC News|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/america-justin-trudeau-partisan-1.6357882|access-date=2022-06-30}} Facebook stated that they had removed fake users that were set up in overseas [[content farm]]s, in [[Romania]], [[Vietnam]], and [[Bangladesh]], which were promoting the convoy protests in Canada. After this announcement, New York [[Democratic Party (United States)|Democratic]] Congresswoman [[Carolyn Maloney]] questioned Facebook as to the number of the accounts, when they were identified and how many impressions they had on US and Canadian users, comparing it to [[Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections|Russian interference in the 2016 US elections]].{{cite web|title=U.S. Congress asks Facebook: What role did fake overseas accounts have in promoting Canada convoys?|url=https://ca.news.yahoo.com/u-congress-asks-facebook-role-225804159.html|access-date=2022-02-19|website=Yahoo News|date=2022-02-18|language=en-CA}} In a letter to Zuckerberg, Maloney cited that "One Bangladeshi firm was responsible for attracting more than 170,000 members to some of the largest 'Freedom Convoy' organizing groups on Facebook."{{cite web|title=Letter to Zuckerberg|publisher=Congress of the United States|url=https://oversight.house.gov/sites/democrats.oversight.house.gov/files/2022-02-14.CBM%20to%20Zuckerberg-Meta%20re%20Inauthentic%20Accounts.pdf|access-date=2022-02-19|archive-date=2022-02-23|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220223032726/https://oversight.house.gov/sites/democrats.oversight.house.gov/files/2022-02-14.CBM%20to%20Zuckerberg-Meta%20re%20Inauthentic%20Accounts.pdf|url-status=dead}} After Ottawa was cleared, conservative commentator [[Candace Owens]] called for American troops to be sent to Canada "to deal with the tyrannical reign of Justin Trudeau Castro." Alt-right personality [[Mike Cernovich]] described Trudeau as a [[Fascism|fascist]].{{cite news|last1=Wade|first1=Peter|title=MAGA Chuds to Ukraine: Drop Dead|url=https://rollingstoneindia.com/maga-chuds-to-ukraine-drop-dead/|access-date=2022-02-21|work=Rolling Stone India|date=2022-02-21|location=Lower Parel (w), Mumbai, India}} ===Trucking industry and labour groups=== In their February 7 statement, [[Teamsters Canada]], representing more than 55,000 professional drivers, including approximately 15,000 long-haul truck drivers, of whom 90 per cent are vaccinated, said that the protest "serves to delegitimize the real concerns of most truck drivers today". The statement said that the convoy and "the despicable display of hate led by the political right and shamefully encouraged by elected conservative politicians does not reflect the values of Teamsters Canada."{{cite web|title=The Real Enemy for Truckers is Covid-19|work=[[Teamsters Canada]]|access-date=2022-02-08|date=2022-02-07|url=https://teamsters.ca/en/blog/2022/02/07/the-real-enemy-for-truckers-is-covid-19/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220208173405/https://teamsters.ca/en/blog/2022/02/07/the-real-enemy-for-truckers-is-covid-19/|archive-date=2022-02-08}} On January 25, the [[Canadian Trucking Alliance]] (CTA), a truckers' [[trade association]], and the federal government issued a formal statement reinforcing the use of vaccinations, along with other public health measures, to protect Canadian health care and to reduce COVID-19 risk. In the statement, the CTA and the government committed to working together to respond to supply chain constraints.{{cite web|title=Federal government, CTA issue joint statement on vaccines, supply chain|work=Truck News|access-date=2022-02-01|date=2022-01-25|url=https://www.trucknews.com/transportation/federal-government-cta-issue-joint-statement-on-vaccines-supply-chain/1003156603/}} A January 29 CTA statement cautioned the public that a "great number of protestors" have no connection to the trucking industry and "have a separate agenda beyond a disagreement over cross border vaccine requirements".{{cite news|last=Murphy|first=Jessica|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-60164561|title=Freedom Convoy: Why Canadian truckers are protesting in Ottawa|work=BBC News|date=2022-01-29}}{{cite web|date=2022-01-29|title=Statement by Canadian Trucking Alliance President on Ottawa Protests|url=https://cantruck.ca/statement-by-canadian-trucking-alliance-president-on-ottawa-protests/|access-date=2022-01-30|website=cantruck.ca}} CTA's January 22 statement had announced that they do "not support and strongly disapprove of any protests on public roadways, highways, and bridges" and the disruption of the "motoring public on highways and commerce at the border". CTA members can express their disagreement with government policies by holding an "organized, lawful event on Parliament Hill". CTA's president, Stephen Laskowski, said the trucking industry "must adapt and comply with this mandate".{{cite web|date=2022-01-22|access-date=2022-01-30|title=Canadian Trucking Alliance Statement to Those Engaged in Road/Border Protests|url=https://cantruck.ca/canadian-trucking-alliance-statement-to-those-engaged-in-road-border-protests/|website=cantruck.ca}}{{cite web|title=Federal government, CTA issue joint statement on vaccines, supply chain|work=Truck News|date=2022-02-01|access-date=2022-01-25|url=https://www.trucknews.com/transportation/federal-government-cta-issue-joint-statement-on-vaccines-supply-chain/1003156603/}} The Private Motor Truck Council of Canada noted concern about the convoy's "racist remarks", citing the comparisons to [[Nazism|Nazis]] and [[communism]].{{cite web|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/embarrassment-for-the-industry-not-all-truckers-support-the-freedom-convoy/|title='Embarrassment for the industry': Not all truckers support the 'freedom convoy'|date=2022-01-27|website=CTV News}} The Atlantic Provinces Trucking Association and the British Columbia Trucking Association both criticized the protest.{{cite web|last=MacInnis|first=Jonathan|date=2022-01-25|title=Atlantic trucking association speaks out against vaccine mandate protests|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/atlantic/article/atlantic-trucking-association-speaks-out-against-vaccine-mandate-protests/|access-date=2022-01-26|website=CTV News Atlantic}} The [[Canadian Labour Congress]] called the protest in Ottawa "an occupation by an angry mob trying to disguise itself as a peaceful protest".{{cite web|date=2022-02-09|title=Joint statement from Canada's unions on the Ottawa occupation|url=https://canadianlabour.ca/joint-statement-from-canadas-unions-on-the-ottawa-occupation/|access-date=2022-02-10|website=[[Canadian Labour Congress]]|language=en-US}} Various trucking companies have tried to distance themselves from the convoys, claiming that the vehicles are owner-operated, despite featuring their logos.{{cite news|last1=Ireton|first1=Julie|title=Truckers misrepresenting employers by joining Ottawa protest, companies say|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/ottawa/truck-drivers-companies-ottawa-convoy-protest-1.6343705|access-date=2022-02-09|work=CBC News Ottawa|publisher=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation|date=2022-02-09|location=Ottawa ON}} === Mass media outlets === According to ''[[The New York Times]]'', "In an increasingly polarized political environment, the Canadian truckers became an instant [[cause célèbre]]."{{cite news|date=2022-02-10|title=Opinion: The Ottawa Trucker Protests Are a Test of Democracy|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/02/10/opinion/ottawa-trucker-protests.html|access-date=2022-02-12|issn=0362-4331}} Though declaring "We disagree with protestors' cause", the newspaper conceded that "[protestors] have a right to be noisy and even disruptive."{{cite news|last=Morrissey|first=Ed|date=2022-02-15|title=Opinion: Trudeau's a hypocrite about trucker protests, says ... the NYT editorial board?|language=en-US|work=Hotair|url=https://hotair.com/ed-morrissey/2022/02/15/trudeaus-a-hypocrite-about-trucker-protests-says-the-nyt-editorial-board-n448641}} In late January, CBC host [[Nil Köksal]] pondered that "there is concern that Russian actors could be continuing to fuel things as this protest grows. But perhaps even instigating it from, from the outset".{{cite web|title=Canadian news host slammed for suggesting Russia behind massive 'freedom' trucker protest|url=https://news.yahoo.com/canadian-news-host-slammed-suggesting-181934492.html|access-date=2022-02-19|website=Yahoo News|language=en-US}}{{cite web|title=Ethnic media provides added perspectives on "Freedom Convoy"|url=https://ca.sports.yahoo.com/news/ethnic-media-provides-added-perspectives-141225012.html|access-date=2022-02-19|website=ca.sports.yahoo.com|language=en-CA|archive-date=2022-02-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220219062847/https://ca.sports.yahoo.com/news/ethnic-media-provides-added-perspectives-141225012.html|url-status=dead}} In October 2022, the [[Canadian Security Intelligence Service]] said no foreign actors had funded or supported the convoy protests.{{Cite news|last=Walsh|first=Marieke|date=2022-10-19|title=CSIS found no foreign actors funding the convoy protests, according to public inquiry evidence|language=en-CA|work=The Globe and Mail|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/politics/article-csis-ottawa-convoy-protests-funding/|access-date=2022-10-19}} A 2023 study by [[Caroline Orr Bueno]] found that RT covered the protests far more than any other outlet.{{cite journal|last=Orr Bueno|first=Caroline|date=2023-01-31|title=Russia's Role in the Far-Right Truck Convoy: An analysis of Russian state media activity related to the 2022 Freedom Convoy|journal=The Journal of Intelligence, Conflict, and Warfare|volume=5|issue=3|pages=1–22|doi=10.21810/jicw.v5i3.5101|issn=2561-8229|doi-access=free}} [[Rich Lowry]] of ''Politico'' argued that "The embrace of the Canadian trucker protesters by the American right is a sign that the [[Tea Party movement|Tea Party]] spirit circa the early Obama years was never fully extinguished. Since then, everything has changed — most importantly, the rise of Donald Trump and his movement — and nothing has changed. It is freedom that remains the most natural and powerful Republican rallying cry."{{cite web|last=Lowry|first=Rich|date=2022-02-17|title=How Canadian Truckers Brought Peace to the GOP's Warring Tribes|url=https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2022/02/17/canadian-truckers-peace-gop-civil-war-warring-tribes-trump-florida-libertarians-populists-republicans-00009798|access-date=2022-06-30|website=Politico}} === Miscellaneous === Canadian scholar [[Peter McLaren]] criticized the convoy protesters' "concept of freedom" as "sorely lacking in dialectical analysis", arguing that "they often limited their analysis to the blanket assertion that 'freedom' means the right to say no to any government restrictions on vaccinations, especially those impeding their ability to cross national borders, in this case, crossing freely between Canada and the United States". McLaren also argued that their concept of freedom "is historically linked to settler colonialism, that is, a form of colonialism grounded in exogenous domination, a form of colonialism that seeks to displace the original population of the colonized territory with new groups of settlers who typically justify such colonization through an identification with imperial authority."{{cite journal|last=McLaren|first=Peter|date=2022-06-07|title=Some thoughts on Canada's 'Freedom Convoy' and the settler colonial state|journal=[[Educational Philosophy and Theory]]|volume=54|issue=7|pages=867–870|doi=10.1080/00131857.2022.2051478|s2cid=247752804|issn=0013-1857|doi-access=free}} General [[Wayne Eyre]], Canada's [[Chief of the Defence Staff (Canada)|Chief of the Defence Staff]], said he was "sickened to see protesters dance on the [[Canadian Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] and desecrate the [[National War Memorial (Canada)|National War Memorial]]", after video of such events surfaced online January 29.{{cite web|last=Pringle|first=Josh|title=Top Canadian defence officials condemn protesters dancing on Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|website=CTV News|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/ottawa/article/top-canadian-defence-officials-condemn-protesters-dancing-on-tomb-of-the-unknown-soldier/|date=2022-01-29|access-date=2022-01-30}} In late January 2022, [[Elon Musk]] tweeted "Canadian truckers rule" and followed it up with "if you scare people enough, they will demand removal of freedom. This is the path to tyranny".{{cite web|last=Stimson|first=Brie|date=2022-01-28|title=Elon Musk offers support to Canadian truckers amid COVID vaccine mandate|url=https://www.foxbusiness.com/business-leaders/elon-musk-offers-support-to-canadian-truckers-amid-covid-vaccine-mandate|access-date=2022-01-28|website=[[Fox Business]]|language=en-US}}{{cite news|date=2022-01-27|title=Elon Musk tweets in support of Canadian truckers ahead of 'Freedom Rally' protest|work=National Post|url=https://nationalpost.com/news/canada/elon-musk-tweets-in-support-of-canadian-truckers-ahead-of-freedom-rally-protest|access-date=2022-01-28}} Musk also tweeted "if Canadian government is suppressing peaceful protests, that's where fascism lies".{{cite web|last=Neate|first=Rupert|date=2022-02-17|title=Elon Musk criticised for likening Justin Trudeau to Adolf Hitler in tweet|url=https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2022/feb/17/elon-musk-criticised-for-comparing-justin-trudeau-to-adolf-hitler-tweet-auschwitz|access-date=2022-02-18|website=The Guardian}} On February 17, Musk tweeted a meme comparing Justin Trudeau to [[Adolf Hitler]], using a meme sympathetic to Hitler. The tweet was later deleted.{{cite news|last1=Mastrangelo|first1=Dominick|date=2022-02-17|title=Elon Musk blasted for tweeting Adolf Hitler meme|work=The Hill|publisher=Nexstar Media Inc.|location=Washington DC|url=https://thehill.com/homenews/594711-elon-musk-blasted-for-tweeting-adolf-hitler-meme|access-date=2022-02-17}}{{cite news|last1=Trudell|first1=Craig|date=2022-02-17|title=Elon Musk Ratchets Up Trudeau Criticism With Hitler Comparison Meme|work=Bloomberg|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-02-17/musk-ratchets-up-trudeau-criticism-with-hitler-comparison-meme|access-date=2022-02-17}}{{cite news|author1=Craig Trudell|author2=Bob Van Voris|date=2022-02-17|title=Elon Musk compares Trudeau to Hitler via Twitter meme|work=driving.ca|publisher=Postmedia|location=Toronto ON|url=https://driving.ca/auto-news/industry/elon-musk-compares-trudeau-to-hitler-via-twitter-meme|access-date=2022-02-17}}{{cite news|last=Abdel-Baqui|first=Omar|date=2022-02-17|title=Elon Musk Compares Justin Trudeau to Hitler in Since-Deleted Tweet|language=en-US|work=The Wall Street Journal|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/elon-musk-compares-justin-trudeau-to-hitler-in-since-deleted-tweet-11645130462|access-date=2022-02-17|issn=0099-9660}} The [[American Jewish Committee]] condemned Musk's tweet and demanded an apology from him. The [[Auschwitz Memorial]] also condemned Musk's tweet. Barry Prentice, transportation economy professor at [[University of Manitoba]], stated that the truckers should be treated differently than flight crews or passenger-train employees, and that the positive aspects of vaccine mandates should be evaluated against the disruptions they would cause to the freight industry.{{cite web|date=2022-01-23|title=COVID-19 vaccine mandates will worsen trucker shortage, affecting consumers, experts say|url=https://www.theglobeandmail.com/business/article-vaccine-mandates-will-worsen-trucker-shortage-impact-consumers-at/|access-date=2022-01-30|website=The Globe and Mail}} English comedian [[Russell Brand]] released a video decrying the media for ignoring reporting on the protest. Brand also said in the video that "truckers, who were previously regarded as heroes when they were delivering vital goods and working during the lockdown, are now villains as they protest vaccine mandates".{{cite web|title=American media personalities have jumped onto the convoy story. Here's what that could mean for Canada|url=https://www.ourwindsor.ca/news-story/10562962-american-media-personalities-have-jumped-onto-the-convoy-story-here-s-what-that-could-mean-for-canada/|access-date=2022-01-29|website=OurWindsor.ca|language=en-CA|archive-date=2022-01-29|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220129032433/https://www.ourwindsor.ca/news-story/10562962-american-media-personalities-have-jumped-onto-the-convoy-story-here-s-what-that-could-mean-for-canada/|url-status=dead}}{{cite news|author=|date=2022-01-28|title=Comedian Russell Brand voices support for Canadian truckers|work=National Post|url=https://nationalpost.com/news/canada/elon-musk-tweets-in-support-of-canadian-truckers-ahead-of-freedom-rally-protest|access-date=2022-01-29}} [[Krista Haynes]], daughter of Ontario Premier [[Doug Ford]] and active anti-lockdown and anti-vaccine campaigner,{{cite web|last=O'Neil|first=Lauren|title=Doug Ford's daughter tells people not to snitch on big illegal Christmas parties|url=https://www.blogto.com/city/2021/12/doug-fords-daughter-asks-people-not-snitch-neighbours-who-have-big/|access-date=2021-12-21|website=www.blogto.com}}{{cite news|last=DiManno|first=Rosie|date=2021-11-26|title=Opinion: At this point in the pandemic, there's no more time for nonsense|language=en-CA|work=The Toronto Star|url=https://www.thestar.com/opinion/star-columnists/2021/11/26/at-this-point-in-the-pandemic-theres-no-more-time-for-nonsense.html|access-date=2021-12-20|issn=0319-0781}}{{cite web|date=2021-12-24|title=Krista Ford's Hubby Says Toronto Police Revoked His Badge As Part Of The 'Unpaid Leave'|url=https://www.narcity.com/toronto/krista-ford-haynes-hubby-says-toronto-police-revoked-his-badge-as-part-of-the-unpaid-leave|access-date=2021-12-24|website=Narcity}} attended a rally in support of the truckers as they headed to Ottawa.{{cite news|last1=Landau|first1=Jack|title=Doug Ford's daughter is out protesting with the trucker convoy in Toronto|url=https://www.blogto.com/city/2022/01/doug-fords-daughter-trucker-convoy-toronto/|access-date=2022-01-30|work=blogTO|date=2022-01-27|location=Toronto ON}}{{cite news|last1=Taylor|first1=Brooke|title=Doug Ford's daughter, Krista Haynes, attended the "Freedom Convoy" on Thursday|url=https://dailyhive.com/vancouver/doug-ford-daughter-krista-haynes-attended-freedom-convoy|access-date=2022-01-30|work=DailyHive|date=2022-01-28|location=Vancouver BC}} The Canadian government attracted strong reaction from Indian [[social media]]. Several commentators ridiculed Trudeau, calling the protest ''[[Karma]]'' for supporting the [[2020–2021 Indian farmers' protest]].{{cite web|date=2022-01-31|title=Taste of own medicine or Karma? Indian Twitter trolls Trudeau over truckers protests|language=en-US|work=Wion|url=https://www.wionews.com/world/taste-of-own-medicine-or-karma-indian-twitter-trolls-trudeau-over-truckers-protests-449273}}{{cite web|date=2022-01-31|title=Understanding why Canada is witnessing massive protests and why #Karmastrikes went viral on Twitter|language=en-US|work=Firstpost|url=https://www.firstpost.com/world/understanding-why-canada-is-witnessing-massive-protests-and-why-karmastrikes-went-viral-on-twitter-10334361.html}}{{cite web|date=2022-01-30|title='Karma strikes', says Indian social media users as Canadian PM hides from anti-vax protests|language=en-US|work=The Print|url=https://theprint.in/world/karma-strikes-says-indian-social-media-users-as-canadian-pm-hides-from-anti-vax-protests/816740/}} Actor [[Kangana Ranaut]] posted on [[Instagram]], "Canadian PM Trudeau was encouraging Indian protestors... now in the midst of protests in his country he is hiding at a secret location... law of ''Karma'' strikes again."{{Cite web|date=2022-01-31|title=Kangana Ranaut reacts to Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau 'hiding' amid protests: 'Karma strikes again'|language=en-US|work=Hindustan Times|url=https://www.hindustantimes.com/entertainment/bollywood/kangana-ranaut-reacts-to-canadian-prime-minister-justin-trudeau-hiding-amid-protests-karma-strikes-again-101643627025310.html}}{{cite web|date=2022-01-31|title=Kangana Ranaut took a dig on Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau calling "Karma strikes again." over Truckers Protest|language=en-US|work=The Tech Outlook|url=https://www.thetechoutlook.com/internet/entertainment/kangana-ranaut-took-a-dig-on-canadian-prime-minister-justin-trudeau-calling-karma-strikes-again-over-truckers-protest/|access-date=November 25, 2023|archive-date=November 25, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231125165004/https://www.thetechoutlook.com/internet/entertainment/kangana-ranaut-took-a-dig-on-canadian-prime-minister-justin-trudeau-calling-karma-strikes-again-over-truckers-protest/|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|date=2022-01-31|title=Kangana Ranaut took a dig on Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau calling "Karma strikes again." over Truckers Protest|language=en-US|work=Times of India|url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/entertainment/hindi/bollywood/news/karma-strikes-again-says-kangana-ranaut-as-canadian-prime-minister-justin-trudeau-moves-to-a-secret-location-amid-protests/articleshow/89249832.cms}} Sportsperson [[Surendra Poonia]] tweeted "''Karma'' returns! Truckers protest in Canada intensifies... [Trudeau] supported tractors on roads of Delhi now facing same in his own country." [[Amish Devgan]] tweeted "''Karma'' Strikes harder, Trudeau supported Tractors protests in Delhi last year on [[Republic Day (India)|Jan 26th]], and now [he] & his family ran away to secret location due to security threat." [[Indo-Canadians|Indian diaspora in Canada]] expressed concern over the imposition of emergency in Canada, drawing comparisons with the absence of such extreme measures when the Indian government had to deal with year-long protests by farmers.{{cite web|date=2022-02-17|title='Learn from Modi': Indians in Canada advise Trudeau on truck protests|language=en-US|work=Hindustan Times|url=https://www.hindustantimes.com/videos/learn-from-modi-indians-in-canada-advise-trudeau-on-truck-protests-101645114898718.html}}{{cite web|last=Bhattacharyya|first=Anirudh|date=2022-02-16|title=Indo-Canadian organizations slam Trudeau's emergency over truckers protest|language=en-US|work=Hindustan Times|url=https://www.hindustantimes.com/world-news/canada-justin-trudeau-s-decision-to-impose-emergency-criticised-101644992398395.html}}{{cite web|date=2022-02-15|title=Canadian author Gad Saad wants to escape from 'dictatorship'; Writes to PM Modi asking for a room in India|language=en-US|work=East Coast In|url=https://www.eastcoastdaily.in/2022/02/15/canadian-author-gad-saad-wants-to-escape-from-dictatorship-writes-to-pm-modi-asking-for-a-room-in-india.html|access-date=November 25, 2023|archive-date=November 25, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231125184435/https://www.eastcoastdaily.in/2022/02/15/canadian-author-gad-saad-wants-to-escape-from-dictatorship-writes-to-pm-modi-asking-for-a-room-in-india.html|url-status=dead}} Canada India Global Forum, an Indo-Canadian organization, urged Canadian PM to "follow the example of [Indian] Prime Minister [Narendra] [[Narendra Modi|Modi]] in handling peaceful protests through democratic means.”{{cite web|date=2022-02-17|title=Follow PM Modi's example of handling farmers' protest: Canada-India Forum to Trudeau|language=en-US|work=India Today|url=https://www.indiatoday.in/world/story/pm-modi-handling-farmers-protest-justin-trudeau-canada-1914123-2022-02-17}} National Association of Indo-Canadians president Azad Kaushik also criticized the Canadian government, tweeting "Canada’s image as a soft power suddenly turns into a hard one unexpected of a 21st century democracy, a loss to not just Canadians but the world." Former President of Iran, [[Mahmoud Ahmadinejad|Mahmoud Ahmadenijad]], praised the protests, tweeting "violent crackdown on #FreedomConvoy2022 has nothing to do with freedom of speech and human rights. How coercion could be related to liberty & freedom of choice?" and signed off with "#TruckersForFreedom".{{cite news|title=Iran's Mahmoud Ahmadinejad offers support to Canada's 'Freedom Convoy'|url=https://www.jpost.com/international/article-696881|access-date=2022-02-19|newspaper=The Jerusalem Post|language=en-US}} [[Great Barrington Declaration]] co-author [[Martin Kulldorff]] tweeted in support of the protests.{{cite web|last=Butler|first=Kiera|date=2022-02-15|title=These doctors' groups are cheering on the anti-vax truckers|url=https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2022/02/ottawa-convoy-protest-promoters-elite-medical-credentials-financial-incentives-astroturf-libertarian/|access-date=2022-03-04|website=[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]]|language=en-US}} According to the ''[[Washington Examiner]]'', psychologist and conservative commentator [[Jordan Peterson]] offered a "message of congratulations and caution" to the convoy.{{cite web|date=2022-02-14|title=Jordan Peterson's message to Freedom Convoy: 'You are now faced with a difficult choice'|url=https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/news/jordan-petersons-message-to-freedom-convoy-you-are-now-faced-with-a-difficult-choice|access-date=2022-02-19|website=Washington Examiner}} The [[Royal Canadian Legion]] called protesters dancing on the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier "shocking" and "strongly condemn[ed]" their actions. On February 12, as a convoy passed through [[Charlottetown]], a lone veteran stood guard at the [[cenotaph]] outside [[Province House (Prince Edward Island)|Province House]] to prevent the same disrespect of the monument.{{cite news|title=P.E.I. veteran praised for guarding cenotaph during anti-mandate rally|first=Tony|last=Davis|work=CBC News|date=2022-02-13|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/prince-edward-island/pei-vet-cenotpah-protest-feb-2022-1.6350365|access-date=2022-02-14}} The [[Simon Wiesenthal Center]] for Holocaust Studies denounced the use of [[Nazi symbolism|Nazi symbols]] by some of the protesters. The [[Justice Centre for Constitutional Freedoms]] provides legal support of the convoy and its contacts with media.{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=elEOtg8dNT0|title=Truck convoy leaders hold news conference in Ottawa – February 3, 2022|date=February 3, 2022 |via=www.youtube.com}}{{cite web|url=https://www.jccf.ca/justice-centre-representing-2022-truckers-freedom-convoy/|title=Justice Centre representing 2022 Truckers' Freedom Convoy|date=2022-02-03}}{{cite web|url=https://tnc.news/2022/02/03/justice-centre-lawyers-in-ottawa-representing-freedom-convoy-truckers/|title=Justice Centre lawyers in Ottawa representing Freedom Convoy truckers|first=Jonathan|last=Bradley|website=True North|date=2022-02-03}} On February 12, Facebook removed anti-vaccine, pro-convoy Facebook groups that were run from Vietnam, Bangladesh, and Romania.{{cite web|title=Facebook removed anti-vaccine trucker protest groups run by overseas actors|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/facebook-removes-anti-vaccine-protest-groups-215953184.html|access-date=2022-02-19|website=finance.yahoo.com|date=2022-02-12|language=en-US}} Singer and activist Bill Fries, whose song "[[Convoy (song)|Convoy]]" (recorded under his alias [[C. W. McCall]]) was used as a rallying cry for the movement, gave his implicit approval for the use of the song in that manner, and was pleased that the movement had caused an uptick in interest in the song, in the final interview he gave before he died.{{cite web|url=https://tasteofcountry.com/c-w-mccall-hospice/|title='Convoy' Singer C.W. McCall is in Hospice|date=2022-02-17}} [[Durham Regional Police Service]] Constable Erin Howard, who made a video while in uniform in her squad car supporting the protesters and later posted it online in January, was suspended and faced six charges under the [[Police Services Act (Ontario)|Police Services Act]], including discreditable conduct and insubordination. Her first court hearing was held over [[Zoom (software)|Zoom]] on May 5, 2022, with the proceeding then being adjourned until June 2022.{{cite news|last=Mitchell|first=Jeff|date=2022-05-05|title=Hearing for Durham officer charged over Freedom Convoy video is adjourned to June|url=https://www.thestar.com/local-oshawa/news/2022/05/05/hearing-for-durham-officer-charged-over-freedom-convoy-video-is-adjourned-to-june.html|work=Oshawa This Week/Toronto Star|access-date=2022-06-06}}{{cite news|author=|date=2022-03-30|title=Durham cop who posted video supporting 'Freedom Convoy' facing police act charges|url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/toronto/durham-police-lockdown-measures-1.6402177|work=CBC News|access-date=2022-06-06}} Some members of the [[Left-wing politics|political left]] were initially hesitant to criticize the convoy because "the truckers were often described as a working-class movement". ===Opinion polls=== In an [[Abacus Data]] survey of 1,410 Canadian adults, undertaken before the protest turned into an occupation, between January 31 and February 2, 2022, 68 per cent stated that they "had very little in common" with the demonstrators and their ideology and 32 per cent of respondents stated that they "had a lot in common" with the protestors and their worldview. Furthermore, 43 per cent of respondents stated that they felt the protest was "respectful and appropriate", while 57 per cent viewed it as "offensive and inappropriate".{{cite web|title=Pandemic frustration may be running high, but more don't side with the so-called "Freedom Convoy"|url=https://abacusdata.ca/freedom-convoy-public-reaction-february-2022/|access-date=2022-02-07|website=Abacus Data|date=2022-02-03}}{{cite news|date=2022-02-07|title=Canada trucker protest: Ottawa declares emergency|language=en-GB|work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-60281088|access-date=2022-02-07}} A study by ''Vox Pop Labs'' among a randomly pre-selected sample of 2,339 respondents, conducted between January 4 and 10, 2022, found that around 70 per cent of Canadians were willing to support a general vaccine mandate for all non-exempt adults over the age of 18, while around 30 per cent disagreed.{{cite web|last1=Beyer|first1=Alexander G. W.|last2=Linden|first2=Clifton van der|title=Majority of Canadians disagree with 'freedom convoy' on vaccine mandates and lockdowns|url=http://theconversation.com/majority-of-canadians-disagree-with-freedom-convoy-on-vaccine-mandates-and-lockdowns-176323|access-date=2022-02-07|website=The Conversation|date=2022-02-03}} A routine political poll by [[Léger (company)|Léger]] in Canada questioned respondents on the protest between February 4 and 6, 2022, and found that it was opposed by 62 per cent of respondents, with 47 per cent strongly opposing it. 32 per cent supported it, while 7 per cent were unsure. On its subsequent questionnaire, a further 65 per cent expressed the belief that the convoy represented "a small minority of selfish Canadians," 57 per cent viewed it as "not about vaccine mandates and pandemic restrictions" but rather "an opportunity for right wing supremacist groups to rally and voice their frustrations about society," and 52 per cent likened it to the [[2021 United States Capitol attack]]. Opposition to the convoy was highest among respondents living in urban areas, those vaccinated for COVID-19, and those aged 55 and up; while support for the convoy was highest among the unvaccinated, respondents aged 18 to 54, and respondents living in [[Alberta]].{{cite web|title=North American Tracker|url=https://leger360.com/surveys/legers-north-american-tracker-february-8-2022/|publisher=Léger|date=2022-02-08|access-date=2022-02-08}} Out of all ethnic groups, only [[White Canadian]]s and [[Filipino Canadian]]s had a majority support for the event. A poll by Maru Public Opinion found that 64 per cent of Canadians polled would support the aid of the [[Canadian Armed Forces]] to tow trucks from the protests and 53 per cent of Canadians polled would support the use of force by Ottawa Police to clear out Ottawa protesters.{{cite web|last=Marchesan|first=John|date=2022-02-12|title=Two-thirds of Canadians support military force to end Ottawa protests: poll|url=https://toronto.citynews.ca/2022/02/12/two-thirds-of-canadians-support-military-force-to-end-ottawa-protests-poll/|access-date=2022-02-12|website=CityNews}} An [[Angus Reid Institute]] poll released on February 14 found that 72 per cent of Canadians polled thought that it was time for protesters to "go home, they have made their point." The poll also found that 65 per cent polled believed Trudeau's response has worsened the situation and 42 per cent said opposition leader Candice Bergen's response has worsened the situation.{{cite web|url=https://angusreid.org/trudeau-convoy-trucker-protest-vaccine-mandates-covid-19/|title=Blockade Backlash: Three-in-four Canadians tell convoy protesters, 'Go Home Now'|date=2022-02-14}} A [[Nanos Research]] poll commissioned by CTV News and released on February 25 found that 51 per cent of Canadians polled thought the protests were ineffective, 15 per cent thought they were somewhat ineffective, 20 per cent thought they were somewhat effective, 12 per cent thought they were effective, and 2 per cent were unsure of the protests' impact.{{cite web|last=Neustaeter|first=Brooklyn|date=2022-02-25|title=Two in three Canadians say trucker convoy protests were ineffective: Nanos survey|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/canada/article/two-in-three-canadians-say-trucker-convoy-protests-were-ineffective-nanos-survey/|access-date=2022-02-27|website=CTV News}} An [[The Economist|Economist]]/[[YouGov]] poll conducted from February 12 to 15 found that 80% of Americans had heard of the convoy protests. The poll also found that 44 per cent of Americans opposed the convoy protests, while 40 per cent supported them. Among Republicans, 71 per cent supported the convoy protests, compared to 18 per cent of Democrats. ==See also== * [[List of protests in the 21st century]] * [[Yellow vests protests#Canada|Yellow Vest Protests in Canada]] * [[Populism in Canada]] * [[2022 Wellington protest]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{Commons category-inline|Canada convoy protest}} * ''[https://publicorderemergencycommission.ca/ Public Order Emergency Commission],'' [[Government of Canada]], 2022 * [https://orders-in-council.canada.ca/attachment.php?attach=41560&lang=en ''Special Temporary Measures for Public Order Emergency,'' PC 2022-0106], text of the Order in Council invoking the ''[[Emergencies Act]]'' in response to the convoy * ''The Fifth Estate'' episode "[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y2OlAXDW7W0 The convoy and the questions: How a protest paralyzed a capital]", aired 24 February 2022. {{Canada convoy protest}} {{COVID-19 pandemic in Canada}} {{Justin Trudeau}} {{Doug Ford}} {{COVID-19 pandemic}} {{Canada–United States relations}} {{Portal bar|Medicine|Canada|Ontario|Viruses|COVID-19}} [[Category:Canada convoy protest| ]] [[Category:2022 protests]] [[Category:2022 in Canada]] [[Category:January 2022 in Canada]] [[Category:February 2022 in Canada]] [[Category:January 2022 crimes in North America]] [[Category:February 2022 crimes in North America]] [[Category:Protests in Canada]] [[Category:Protests over responses to the COVID-19 pandemic]] [[Category:COVID-19 pandemic in Canada]] [[Category:Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on politics]] [[Category:QAnon]] [[Category:Trucking subculture]] [[Category:Occupations (protest)]] [[Category:Premiership of Justin Trudeau]] [[Category:Canada–United States trade relations]] [[Category:Right-wing populism in Canada]] [[Category:2022 in Ottawa]]

{{short description|Historic and contemporary immigration statistics of Canada}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2022}} {{Use Canadian English|date=November 2022}} {{Canadian citizenship}} Since before [[Canadian Confederation|confederation in 1867]] through to the contemporary era, [[decade|decadal]] and demi-decadal [[census in Canada|census reports in Canada]] have compiled detailed immigration statistics. During this period, the highest annual immigration rate in [[Canada]] occurred in 1913, when 400,900 new immigrants accounted for 5.3 percent of the total population, while the largest number of immigrants admitted to Canada in single year occurred in 2024, with 483,640 persons accounting for 1.2 percent of the total population.{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=3 January 2023 |title=Canada welcomes historic number of newcomers in 2022 |url=https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/news/2022/12/canada-welcomes-historic-number-of-newcomers-in-2022.html |access-date=2023-01-06 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}} In a linear timeline following initial [[British colonization of the Americas|British]] and [[New France|French colonization]], what is now Canada has seen four major waves (or peaks) of [[immigration]] and settlement of non-[[Indigenous peoples in Canada|Indigenous Peoples]] take place over a span of nearly two centuries. Canada is currently undergoing its fifth wave. == Annual immigration and rate == Since [[Canadian Confederation|confederation in 1867]], the highest annual immigration rate in Canada occurred during the early 20th century, including 1913 (new immigrants accounted for 5.3 percent of the total population), 1912 (5.1 percent), 1911 (4.6 percent), 1907 (4.3 percent) and 1910 (4.1 percent). At this time, immigration from the [[British Isles]] increased, supplemented by a rapid increase in immigration flows from [[continental Europe]], especially Germany, [[Scandinavia]], and the [[Soviet Union]]. {{Col-begin}} {{Col-break}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Annual immigration statistics

of Permanent Residents by Country of Citizenship |access-date=2023-01-06}}}} {{Col-end}} {{clear}} == Immigration graph == [[File:Immigration_all.png|Immigration]]. == Sources of immigration == Canada receives its immigrant population from almost every country in the world. Statistics Canada projects that immigrants will represent between 29.1% and 34.0% of Canada's population in 2041, compared with 23.0% in 2021,{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=26 October 2022 |title=Immigrants make up the largest share of the population in over 150 years and continue to shape who we are as Canadians |url=https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/daily-quotidien/221026/dq221026a-eng.htm |access-date=2022-11-06 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}} while the Canadian population with at least one foreign born parent ([[Immigrant generations#First generation|first]] and [[Immigrant generations#Second generation|second generation]] persons) could rise to between 49.8% and 54.3%, up from 44.0% in 2021.{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=26 October 2022 |title=Visible minority and population group by generation status: Canada, provinces and territories, census metropolitan areas and census agglomerations with parts |url=https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/t1/tbl1/en/tv.action?pid=9810032401 |access-date=2022-11-06 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=8 September 2022 |title=Canada in 2041: A larger, more diverse population with greater differences between regions |url=https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/daily-quotidien/220908/dq220908a-eng.htm |access-date=2022-11-05 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=8 September 2022 |title=Projected population by racialized group, generation status and other selected characteristics (x 1,000) |url=https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/t1/tbl1/en/tv.action?pid=1710014601 |access-date=2022-11-05 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}} The number of visible ethno-cultural composition of population will double and make up the minority of the population of [[cities in Canada]].{{cite web|title=Parties prepare to battle for Immigrant votes|url=https://www.ctvnews.ca/parties-prepare-to-battle-for-immigrant-votes-1.492090|date=14 March 2010|website=[[CTV News]]|publisher=Bell Media|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100316052329/http://www.ctv.ca/servlet/ArticleNews/story/CTVNews/20100314/Minority_Report_100314/20100314?hub=Canada|archive-date=2010-03-16|access-date=2010-03-15}} === 2021 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (2021).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 2021 census]] The [[2021 Canadian census|2021 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 8,361,505 individuals or 23.0 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were India (898,045 persons or 10.7%), Philippines (719,580 persons or 8.6%), China (715,835 persons or 8.6%), United Kingdom (464,135 persons or 5.6%), United States (256,085 persons or 3.1%), Pakistan (234,110 persons or 2.8%), Hong Kong (213,855 persons or 2.6%), Italy (204,065 persons or 2.4%), Iran (182,940 persons or 2.2%), and Vietnam (182,095 persons or 2.2%).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2022-10-26 |title= Immigrant status and period of immigration by place of birth and citizenship: Canada, provinces and territories and census metropolitan areas with parts |url=https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/t1/tbl1/en/tv.action?pid=9810030201 |access-date=2022-11-26 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2022-10-26 |title= Focus on Geography Series, 2021 Census of Population Canada Immigration, place of birth, and citizenship |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/as-sa/fogs-spg/page.cfm?topic=9&lang=E&dguid=2021A000011124 |access-date=2022-11-26 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[2021 Canadian census]]
''Immigrant'' refers to all those who hold or have ever held permanent resident status in Canada, including [[Naturalization|naturalized citizens]]. ! Total immigrants ! 8,361,505 ! {{Percentage | 8361505 | 8361505 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 8361505 | 36328475 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 2016 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (2016).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 2016 census]] The [[2016 Canadian census|2016 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 7,540,830 individuals or 21.9 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were India (668,565 persons or 8.9%), China (649,260 persons or 8.6%), Philippines (588,305 persons or 7.8%), United Kingdom (499,120 persons or 6.6%), United States (253,715 persons or 3.4%), Italy (236,635 persons or 3.1%), Hong Kong (208,935 persons or 2.8%), Pakistan (202,255 persons or 2.7%), Vietnam (169,250 persons or 2.2%), and Iran (154,420 persons or 2.1%).{{cite web|last=Statistics Canada|date=25 October 2017|title=Immigrant population by place of birth, period of immigration, 2016 counts, both sexes, age (total), Canada, 2016 Census – 25% Sample data {{!}} Immigration and Ethnocultural Diversity Highlight Tables|url=http://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2016/dp-pd/hlt-fst/imm/Table.cfm?Lang=E&T=21&Geo=01&SO=4D|access-date=27 May 2018|website=[[Statistics Canada]]|publisher=Government of Canada}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[2016 Canadian census]]
''Immigrant'' refers to all those who hold or have ever held permanent resident status in Canada, including [[Naturalization|naturalized citizens]]. ! Total immigrants ! 7,540,830 !{{Percentage | 7540830 | 7540830 | 2 }} !{{Percentage | 7540830 | 34460060 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 2011 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (2011).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 2011 census]] The [[2011 Canadian census|2011 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 6,775,765 individuals or 20.6 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[India]] (547,890 persons or 8.1 percent), [[China]] (545,535 5persons or 8.1 percent), [[United Kingdom]] (537,040 persons or 7.9 percent), [[Philippines]] (454,340 persons or 6.7 percent), [[United States]] (263,475 persons or 3.9 percent), [[Italy]] (256,825 persons or 3.8 percent), [[Hong Kong]] (205,430 persons or 3.0 percent), [[Vietnam]] (165,125 persons or 2.5 percent), [[Pakistan]] (156,860 persons or 2.3 percent), and [[Germany]] (152,345 persons or 2.3 percent).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2019-01-23 |title= 2011 National Household Survey: Data tables Profile - Immigration and Ethnocultural Diversity, and Aboriginal Peoples for Canada, Provinces, Territories, Census Divisions and Census Subdivisions, National Household Survey, 2011 |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/nhs-enm/2011/dp-pd/dt-td/Rp-eng.cfm?LANG=E&APATH=3&DETAIL=0&DIM=0&FL=A&FREE=0&GC=0&GID=0&GK=0&GRP=1&PID=105539&PRID=0&PTYPE=105277&S=0&SHOWALL=0&SUB=0&Temporal=2013&THEME=95&VID=0&VNAMEE=&VNAMEF= |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2015-11-27 |title=NHS Profile, Canada, 2011 |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/nhs-enm/2011/dp-pd/prof/details/page.cfm?Lang=E&Geo1=PR&Code1=01&Data=Count&SearchText=Canada&SearchType=Begins&SearchPR=01&A1=All&B1=All&Custom=&TABID=1 |access-date=2023-03-04 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2018-07-25 |title= Immigration and Ethnocultural Diversity in Canada |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/nhs-enm/2011/as-sa/99-010-x/99-010-x2011001-eng.cfm |access-date=2022-11-16 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[2011 Canadian census]]

Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (579,620 persons or 9.4%), [[China]] (466,940 persons or 7.6%), [[India]] (443,690 persons or 7.2%), [[Philippines]] (303,195 persons or 4.9%), [[Italy]] (296,850 persons or 4.8%), [[United States]] (250,535 persons or 4.1%), [[Hong Kong]] (215,430 persons or 3.5%), [[Germany]] (171,405 persons or 2.8%), [[Poland]] (170,490 persons or 2.8%), and [[Vietnam]] (160,170 persons or 2.6%).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2009-03-23 |title= Place of birth for the immigrant population by period of immigration, 2006 counts and percentage distribution, for Canada, provinces and territories - 20% sample data |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2006/dp-pd/hlt/97-557/T404-eng.cfm?Lang=E&T=404&GH=4&GF=1&SC=1&S=1&O=D |access-date=2022-11-16 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2020-05-01 |title= 2006 Census Topic-based tabulations Immigrant Status and Period of Immigration (8) and Place of Birth (261) for the Immigrants and Non-permanent Residents of Canada, Provinces, Territories, Census Metropolitan Areas and Census Agglomerations, 2006 Census - 20% Sample Data |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2006/dp-pd/tbt/Rp-eng.cfm?LANG=E&APATH=3&DETAIL=1&DIM=0&FL=A&FREE=1&GC=0&GID=0&GK=0&GRP=1&PID=89424&PRID=0&PTYPE=88971&S=0&SHOWALL=No&SUB=0&Temporal=2006&THEME=72&VID=0&VNAMEE=&VNAMEF= |access-date=2022-11-16 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2009-11-20 |title= 2006 Census: Immigration in Canada: A Portrait of the Foreign-born Population, 2006 Census: Findings |url=https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2006/as-sa/97-557/index-eng.cfm |access-date=2022-11-16 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[2006 Canadian census]]

Total immigrants ! 6,186,950 ! {{Percentage | 6186950 | 6186950 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 6186950 | 31241030 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 2001 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (2001).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 2001 census]] The [[2001 Canadian census|2001 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 5,448,480 individuals or 18.4 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (605,995 persons or 11.1%), [[China]] (332,825 persons or 6.1%), [[Italy]] (315,455 persons or 5.8%), [[India]] (314,690 persons or 5.8%), [[United States]] (237,920 persons or 4.4%), [[Hong Kong]] (235,620 persons or 4.3%), [[Philippines]] (232,670 persons or 4.3%), [[Poland]] (180,415 persons or 3.3%), [[Germany]] (174,070 persons or 3.2%), and [[Portugal]] (153,535 persons or 2.8%).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2013-12-23 |title= 2001 Census Topic-based tabulations Immigrant Status and Period of Immigration (10A) and Place of Birth of Respondent (260) for Immigrants and Non-permanent Residents, for Canada, Provinces, Territories, Census Metropolitan Areas and Census Agglomerations, 2001 Census - Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (655,535 persons or 13.2%), [[Italy]] (332,110 persons or 6.7%), [[United States]] (244,695 persons or 4.9%), [[Hong Kong]] (241,095 persons or 4.9%), [[India]] (235,930 persons or 4.8%), [[China]] (231,055 persons or 4.7%), [[Poland]] (193,375 persons or 3.9%), [[Philippines]] (184,550 persons or 3.7%), [[Germany]] (181,650 persons or 3.7%), and [[Portugal]] (158,815 persons or 3.2%).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2019-06-04 |title= Data tables, 1996 Census Total immigrants ! 4,971,070 ! {{Percentage | 4971070 | 4971070 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 4971070 | 28528125 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 1991 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (1991).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 1991 census]] The [[1991 Canadian census|1991 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 4,342,890 individuals or 16.1 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (717,750 persons or 16.5%), [[Italy]] (351,615 persons or 8.1%), [[United States]] (249,075 persons or 5.7%), [[Poland]] (184,695 persons or 4.3%), [[Germany]] (180,525 persons or 4.2%), [[India]] (173,675 persons or 4%), [[Portugal]] (161,180 persons or 3.7%), [[China]] (157,405 persons or 3.6%), [[Hong Kong]] (152,450 persons or 3.5%), and [[Netherlands]] (129,615 persons or 3%). {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[1991 Canadian census]]
''Immigrant'' refers to all those who hold or have ever held permanent resident status in Canada, including [[Naturalization|naturalized citizens]]. ! Total immigrants ! 4,342,890 ! {{Percentage | 4342890 | 4342890 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 4342890 | 26994045 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 1986 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (1986).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 1986 census]] The [[1986 Canadian census|1986 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 3,908,140 individuals or 15.6 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (793,075 persons or 20.3%), [[Italy]] (366,820 persons or 9.4%), [[United States]] (282,025 persons or 7.2%), [[Germany]] (189,560 persons or 4.9%), [[Poland]] (156,800 persons or 4.0%), [[Portugal]] (139,635 persons or 3.6%), [[Netherlands]] (134,155 persons or 3.4%), [[India]] (130,055 persons or 3.3%), [[China]] (119,195 persons or 3.1%), and the [[Soviet Union]] (109,445 persons or 2.8%). {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[1986 Canadian census]]
''Immigrant'' refers to all those who hold or have ever held permanent resident status in Canada, including [[Naturalization|naturalized citizens]]. ! Total immigrants ! 3,908,140 ! {{Percentage | 3908140 | 3908140 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 3908140 | 25022005 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 1981 census === [[File:Immigrants in Canada (1981).jpg|thumb|Largest nation of immigrants by province and territory, 1981 census]] The [[1981 Canadian census|1981 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 3,867,160 individuals or 16.1 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (884,915 persons or 22.9%), [[Italy]] (386,505 persons or 10%), [[United States]] (312,015 persons or 8.1%), [[Germany]] (198,215 persons or 5.1%), [[Poland]] (148,940 persons or 3.9%), [[Portugal]] (139,765 persons or 3.6%), [[Netherlands]] (138,760 persons or 3.6%), [[Soviet Union]] (128,680 persons or 3.3%), [[India]] (109,660 persons or 2.8%), and [[Yugoslavia]] (91,870 persons or 2.4%).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2013-04-03 |title= 1981 Census of Canada : volume 1 - national series : population Place of birth, citizenship, period of immigration. |url=https://publications.gc.ca/site/eng/9.837638/publication.html |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2013-04-03 |title= 1981 census information on place of birth, citizenship and immigration : some facts and figures / by Walton O. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (933,045 persons or 28.3%), [[Italy]] (385,760 persons or 11.7%), [[United States]] (309,640 persons or 9.4%), [[Germany]] (211,060 persons or 6.4%), [[Soviet Union]] (160,125 persons or 4.9%), [[Poland]] (160,035 persons or 4.9%), [[Netherlands]] (133,525 persons or 4.1%), [[Scandinavia]] (85,140 persons or 2.6%), [[Greece]] (78,780 persons or 2.4%), and [[Yugoslavia]] (78,285 persons or 2.4%).{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=4 September 2022 |title= 1971 Census of Canada : population : vol. Total immigrants ! 2,844,263 ! {{Percentage | 2844263 | 2844263 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 2844263 | 18238247 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} === 1951 census === The [[1951 Canadian census|1951 census]] reported that [[Immigration to Canada|immigrants]] comprised 2,059,911 individuals or 14.7 percent of the total Canadian population. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (855,797 persons or 41.6%), [[Soviet Union]] & [[Poland]] (352,766 persons or 17.1%), [[United States]] (282,010 persons or 13.7%), [[Scandinavia]] (86,557 persons or 4.2%), [[Ireland]] (80,795 persons or 3.9%), [[Germany]] & [[Austria]] (80,291 persons or 3.9%), [[Italy]] (57,789 persons or 2.8%), [[Netherlands]] (41,457 persons or 2%), [[Hungary]] (32,929 persons or 1.6%), and [[Czechoslovakia]] (29,546 persons or 1.4%).{{rp|9}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2013-04-03 |title= Ninth census of Canada, 1951 Vol. 1. Population: general characteristics. |url=https://publications.gc.ca/site/eng/9.836019/publication.html |access-date=2025-07-09 |website=www12.statcan.gc.ca}}{{rp|675}} {| class="wikitable sortable" |+Total immigrant population by country of birth, [[1951 Canadian census]]{{rp|9}}{{rp|675}}
''Immigrant'' refers to all those who hold or have ever held permanent resident status in Canada, including [[Naturalization|naturalized citizens]]. ! Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were [[United Kingdom]] (1,031,398 persons or 44.7%), [[United States]] (344,574 persons or 14.9%), [[Poland]] & [[Soviet Union]] (305,038 persons or 13.2%), [[Scandinavia]] (120,396 persons or 5.2%), [[Ireland]] (107,544 persons or 4.7%), [[Austria]] & [[Germany]] (76,554 persons or 3.3%), [[Italy]] (42,578 persons or 1.9%), [[China]] (42,037 persons or 1.8%), [[Romania]] (40,322 persons or 1.8%), and [[Hungary]] (28,523 persons or 1.2%).{{rp|9}}{{Cite web |last=Government of Canada |first=Statistics Canada |date=2013-04-03 |title= Seventh census of Canada, 1931 . v. 1. Total immigrants ! 535,153 ! {{Percentage | 535153 | 535153 | 2 }} ! {{Percentage | 535153 | 2036065 | 2 }} ! {{N/a}} |} == Annual sources of immigration == === 2020s === [[File:Immigrant arrivals during 2020s.jpg|thumb|Largest immigrant arrival nation of last permanent residence (2020-2024)]] During the 2020s (up to June 2025/Q2), 2,191,395 immigrants have arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top five source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 2020s include [[India]] (615,495 persons or 28.1 percent), [[China]] (152,640 persons or 6.97 percent), [[Philippines]] (124,300 persons or 5.67 percent), [[Nigeria]] (90,865 persons or 4.15 percent), and [[Afghanistan]] (69,630 persons or 3.18 percent). Between March 17, 2022 and April 1, 2024, 298,128 [[Ukrainians]] arrived in Canada, the vast majority of whom were not counted in the official immigration statistics for each calendar year, instead arriving under temporary resident visas.{{cite web|title=Canada-Ukraine authorization for emergency travel: Key figures|url=https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/services/immigrate-canada/ukraine-measures/key-figures.html|website=Government of Canada|date=26 July 2024 |access-date=13 August 2025}}{{cite web|title=Ukraine immigration measures: Key figures|url=https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/services/immigrate-canada/ukraine-measures/key-figures.html|website=Government of Canada|date=30 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230102061942/https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/services/immigrate-canada/ukraine-measures/key-figures.html |access-date=6 January 2023|archive-date=2 January 2023 }}{{cite web|title=Ukraine immigration measures: Key figures|url=https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/services/immigrate-canada/ukraine-measures/key-figures.html|website=Government of Canada|date=30 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230514002156/https://www.canada.ca/en/immigration-refugees-citizenship/services/immigrate-canada/ukraine-measures/key-figures.html |access-date=10 June 2023|archive-date=14 May 2023 }} {{sticky header}}

=== 2010s === [[File:Immigrant arrivals during 2010s.jpg|thumb|Largest immigrant arrival nation of last permanent residence (2010-2019)]] During the 2010s, 2,823,572 immigrants arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top ten source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 2010s included [[India]] (450,257 persons or 16.0 percent), [[Philippines]] (375,703 persons or 13.3 percent), [[China]] (287,084 persons or 10.2 percent), [[Pakistan]] (97,801 persons or 3.46 percent), [[United States]] (87,623 persons or 3.10 percent), [[Iran]] (84,952 persons or 3.01 percent), [[Syria]] (84,828 persons or 3 percent), [[United Kingdom]] (60,361 persons or 2.14 percent), [[Nigeria]] (56,251 persons or 1.99 percent), and [[France]] (55,338 persons or 1.96 percent). {{sticky header}}

=== 2000s === [[File:Immigrant arrivals during 2000s.jpg|thumb|Largest immigrant arrival nation of last permanent residence (2000-2009)]] During the 2000s, 2,414,639 immigrants arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top ten source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 2000s included [[China]] (340,161 persons or 14.1 percent), [[India]] (303,359 persons or 12.6 percent), [[Philippines]] (172,857 persons or 7.16 percent), [[Pakistan]] (127,559 persons or 5.28 percent), the [[United States]] (75,163 persons or 3.11 percent), [[South Korea]] (68,121 persons or 2.82 percent), [[Iran]] (65,781 persons or 2.72 percent), [[United Kingdom]] (65,337 persons or 2.71 percent), [[Sri Lanka]] (49,544 persons or 2.05 percent), and [[Colombia]] (45,813 persons or 1.9 percent). {{sticky header}}

=== 1990s === [[File:Immigrant arrivals in Canada (1990s).jpg|thumb|Largest immigrant arrival nation of last permanent residence (1990-1999)]] During the 1990s, 2,204,161 immigrants arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top ten source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 1990s included [[Hong Kong]] (268,162 persons or 12.2 percent), [[India]] (164,258 persons or 7.45 percent), [[China]] (153,778 persons or 6.98 percent), [[Philippines]] (133,585 persons or 6.06 percent), [[Taiwan]] (80,566 persons or 3.66 percent), [[Sri Lanka]] (68,356 persons or 3.10 percent), [[United Kingdom]] (65,026 persons or 2.95 percent), [[Yugoslavia]] (63,746 persons or 2.89 percent), [[Poland]] (63,735 persons or 2.89 percent), and the [[United States]] (61,097 persons or 2.77 percent). {{sticky header}}

=== 1980s === [[File:Immigrant arrivals in Canada (1980s).jpg|thumb|Largest immigrant arrival nation of last permanent residence (1980-1989)]] During the 1980s, 1,259,325 immigrants arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top ten source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 1980s included [[United Kingdom]] (116,765 persons or 9.27 percent), [[Vietnam]] (104,950 persons or 8.33 percent), [[Hong Kong]] (99,415 persons or 7.89 percent), [[India]] (82,156 persons or 6.52 percent), [[United States]] (78,483 persons or 6.23 percent), [[Poland]] (62,751 persons or 4.98 percent), [[Philippines]] (60,764 persons or 4.83 percent), [[Portugal]] (40,432 persons or 3.21 percent), [[Jamaica]] (34,328 persons or 2.73 percent), and [[Guyana]] (32,096 persons or 2.55 percent). {{sticky header}}

The continental breakdown for immigrants to Canada during this time included [[Europe]] (966,450 persons or 70.7 percent), [[North America]] (202,343 persons or 14.8 percent), [[Asia]] (109,770 persons or 8.03 percent), [[Oceania]] (30,630 persons or 2.24 percent), [[Africa]] (29,178 persons or 2.13 percent), [[South America]] (25,888 persons or 1.89 percent), and Other/Not stated (2,581 persons or 0.19 percent). Furthermore, during the 1960s, the top three source countries for immigrants to Canada included the [[United Kingdom]] (336,374 persons or 24.6 percent), [[Italy]] (200,442 persons or 14.7 percent), and the [[United States]] (153,609 persons or 11.2 percent). {{sticky header}}

=== 1950s === During the 1950s, 1,544,642 immigrants arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top five source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 1950s included the [[United Kingdom]] (380,984 persons or 24.7 percent), [[Italy]] (234,369 persons or 15.2 percent), [[Germany]] & [[Austria]] (213,653 persons or 13.8 percent) [[Netherlands]] (120,757 persons or 7.82 percent), and [[Poland]] & the [[Soviet Union]] (92,986 persons or 6.02 percent).{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1952 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1952-53-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-12 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=163–174}}{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1955 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1955-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-12 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=164–176}}{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1957 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1957-58-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-12 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=154–187}}{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1960 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1960-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-12 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=216–227}} {{sticky header}}

=== 1940s === During the 1940s, 428,733 immigrants arrived in Canada. Furthermore, the top five source countries for immigrants to Canada during the 1940s included the [[United Kingdom]] (162,082 persons or 37.8 percent), [[Poland]] & the [[Soviet Union]] (76,777 persons or 17.9 percent), [[United States]] (52,604 persons or 12.3 percent), [[Netherlands]] (18,729 persons or 4.37 percent), and [[Italy]] (11,300 persons or 2.64 percent).{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1942 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1942-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-13 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=151–170}}{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1943 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1943-44-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-13 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=176–186}}{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1948 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1948-49-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-13 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=172–184}}{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Statistics |date=2017-10-11 |title=Canada Year Book 1960 |url=https://www66.statcan.gc.ca/eng/1951-eng.htm |access-date=2025-08-13 |website=www66.statcan.gc.ca |pages=138–152}} {{sticky header}}
